topics.title: Metastable alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metastable alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metastable Alloys** are non-equilibrium, kinetically-stabilized alloy systems whose free energy is **higher** than that of the corresponding equilibrium phase(s), but which persist indefinitely at ambient conditions because the activation barrier for transformation to the equilibrium state is prohibitively large at the service temperature.

In strict thermodynamic language, a **metastable phase** is a *local minimum* on the Gibbs free energy $(G)$ versus composition/temperature landscape, separated from the *global equilibrium minimum* by a finite energy barrier $\Delta G^{*}$.

$$G_{\text{metastable}} > G_{\text{equilibrium}}, \quad \Delta G^{*} \gg k_{B}T$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ball in a Valley"

Imagine a ball rolling down a rugged hillside:

- The **true bottom of the hill** = the *equilibrium phase* (lowest free energy, e.g., a crystalline intermetallic).
- A **small ledge or pothole halfway down** = the *metastable phase* (e.g., an amorphous glass).

The ball comes to rest in the pothole because, although energy *could* be lowered by rolling further, it must first climb over a hump — the **activation barrier $\Delta G^{*}$**. The same is true for atoms in a rapidly quenched alloy: they are "frozen" into a high-energy configuration because diffusion is too sluggish at low temperature to allow reorganization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric — Reduced Glass Transition Temperature:** A key indicator of glass-forming ability is the **reduced glass transition temperature** $T_{rg} = T_{g}/T_{l}$, where $T_{g}$ is the glass transition temperature and $T_{l}$ is the liquidus temperature. Values of $T_{rg} \geq \mathbf{0.6}$ indicate strong glass-forming tendency (high stability of the metastable amorphous phase).

## 1.3 Classification of Metastable Alloys

| Class | Key Feature | Representative System |
|---|---|---|
| **Amorphous (Metallic Glasses)** | No long-range atomic order | $\text{Fe}_{80}\text{B}_{20}$, $\text{Zr}_{41.2}\text{Ti}_{13.8}\text{Cu}_{12.5}\text{Ni}_{10}\text{Be}_{22.5}$ (Vitreloy 1) |
| **Quasicrystalline** | Long-range order with forbidden 5-fold/icosahedral symmetry | $\text{Al}_{65}\text{Cu}_{20}\text{Fe}_{15}$ (Shechtman phases) |
| **Nanocrystalline** | Grains of 1–100 nm embedded in amorphous matrix | $\text{Fe}_{73.5}\text{Si}_{13.5}\text{B}_{9}\text{Nb}_{3}\text{Cu}_{1}$ (FINEMET) |
| **Supersaturated Solid Solutions** | Solute concentration far above equilibrium solubility limit | Al–Si, Cu–Fe (rapidly solidified) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Basis

A phase transformation occurs when the system moves to a configuration of lower Gibbs free energy. For a metastable alloy:

$$\Delta G = G_{\text{metastable}} - G_{\text{equilibrium}} > 0$$

The driving force for transformation is therefore $-\Delta G$, but the system remains trapped because of the kinetic barrier $\Delta G^{*}$ (the activation energy for nucleation of the stable phase).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — Kinetic vs. Thermodynamic Control:** Metastability arises *purely* from kinetics. Thermodynamics favours the equilibrium state; kinetics (sluggish atomic diffusion at low $T$) prevents the system from reaching it.

## 2.2 Free Energy Hierarchy in Alloys

A schematic free energy landscape shows:

1. **Liquid (highest $G$)** — disordered, high entropy.
2. **Amorphous / supersaturated / nanocrystalline (intermediate $G$)** — metastable phases.
3. **Equilibrium crystalline phases (lowest $G$)** — intermetallics, terminal solid solutions.

The transformation pathway during rapid cooling:

$$\text{Liquid} \xrightarrow{\text{fast quench}} \text{Amorphous (frozen liquid)} \xrightarrow{\text{heat (devitrification)}} \text{Nanocrystalline} \rightarrow \text{Stable Crystals}$$

## 2.3 The Glass Transition

Upon rapid cooling, the undercooled liquid avoids crystallization at the *crystallization temperature $T_x$* and instead undergoes a *glass transition* at $T_g$, where viscosity $\eta$ rises sharply (typically to $\sim 10^{12} \text{ Pa·s}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Cooling Rate ($R_c$):** The minimum cooling rate required to bypass crystallization and obtain a fully amorphous structure. For $\text{SiO}_2$: $R_c \approx 10^{-4} \text{ K/s}$. For conventional metallic alloys: $R_c \approx 10^{6} \text{ K/s}$. For **bulk metallic glasses (BMGs)**: $R_c \approx 1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ K/s}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Metastable Alloys

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy change driving transformation |
| 2 | $T_{rg} = T_{g} / T_{l}$ | Reduced glass transition temperature; $T_{rg} \geq 0.6$ → good glass former |
| 3 | $\eta(T) = \eta_{0} \exp\left(\dfrac{D^{*} T_{0}}{T - T_{0}}\right)$ | Vogel–Fulcher–Tammann (VFT) viscosity law governing supercooled liquid flow |
| 4 | $I = I_{0} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{*}}{k_{B}T}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{k_{B}T}\right)$ | Steady-state nucleation rate (classical nucleation theory) |
| 5 | $\Delta G^{*} = \dfrac{16\pi \sigma^{3}}{3(\Delta G_{v})^{2}}$ | Critical free energy barrier for homogeneous nucleation of a spherical nucleus |
| 6 | $X(t) = 1 - \exp(-kt^{n})$ | Avrami (Johnson–Mehl–Avrami) kinetics of crystallization; $n$ = Avrami index |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{R_{c}}$ | Characteristic time for vitrification; inverse of critical cooling rate |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{x} = T_{x} - T_{g}$ | Supercooled liquid region width; larger $\Delta T_{x}$ → better thermal stability of glass |

> **Real-world Utility:** These equations are used in process design of metallic glass ribbons (melt spinning), transformer cores (FINEMET nanocrystalline soft magnets), and high-strength sporting goods (Zr-based BMGs in golf clubs, watches, smartphone cases).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Free Energy Barrier for Nucleation $\Delta G^{*}$

The classical nucleation theory (CNT) considers the formation of a spherical crystalline embryo of radius $r$ inside an undercooled liquid. The total free energy change associated with forming the embryo has two contributions:

### Step 1 — Define the Two Contributions

- **Volume free energy change** (favourable, negative): proportional to the volume of the nucleus.

$$ \Delta G_{V} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \, \Delta g_{v} $$

where $\Delta g_{v}$ is the Gibbs free energy change per unit volume of the transformed phase.

- **Surface free energy change** (unfavourable, positive): proportional to the interface area, because the new interface carries an interfacial energy $\sigma$.

$$ \Delta G_{S} = 4\pi r^{2} \, \sigma $$

### Step 2 — Total Free Energy Change

Adding both contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G(r) &= \Delta G_{S} + \Delta G_{V} \\[4pt]
&= 4\pi r^{2} \sigma + \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta g_{v}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Locate the Critical Radius

Differentiate $\Delta G$ with respect to $r$ and set $\dfrac{d\Delta G}{dr} = 0$ to find the maximum (the activation barrier):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\Delta G}{dr} &= 8\pi r \sigma + 4\pi r^{2} \Delta g_{v} = 0 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad r^{*} &= -\frac{2\sigma}{\Delta g_{v}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Delta g_{v} < 0$ (crystallization is exothermic), $r^{*}$ is positive.

### Step 4 — Compute the Critical Activation Barrier $\Delta G^{*}$

Substitute $r = r^{*}$ back into $\Delta G(r)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{*} &= 4\pi (r^{*})^{2} \sigma + \frac{4}{3}\pi (r^{*})^{3} \Delta g_{v} \\[4pt]
&= 4\pi \left(\frac{2\sigma}{\Delta g_{v}}\right)^{2}\! \sigma + \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{2\sigma}{\Delta g_{v}}\right)^{3}\! \Delta g_{v} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{16\pi \sigma^{3}}{(\Delta g_{v})^{2}} - \frac{32\pi \sigma^{3}}{3(\Delta g_{v})^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{16\pi \sigma^{3}}{3(\Delta g_{v})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** A large $\Delta G^{*}$ means nucleation of the equilibrium crystal is suppressed, favouring vitrification. This is achieved by **large $\sigma$** (slow atomic attachment) and **small $\vert \Delta g_{v} \vert$** (small thermodynamic driving force), which is exactly the regime near the nose of the TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) diagram.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reduced Glass Transition Temperature $T_{rg}$

From the VFT equation, the viscosity diverges as $T \to T_{0}$:

$$\eta(T) = \eta_{0} \exp\!\left(\frac{D^{*} T_{0}}{T - T_{0}}\right)$$

At the glass transition, $\eta(T_{g}) \approx 10^{12} \text{ Pa·s}$. Combining with the definition $T_{rg} = T_{g}/T_{l}$ and empirical correlations (Turnbull, 1969):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{c} &\propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{T_{rg}}\right) \\[4pt]
\ln R_{c} &= \ln R_{0} - \frac{K}{T_{rg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Larger $T_{rg}$ → exponentially smaller $R_{c}$ → easier glass formation. This is why deep eutectic compositions (e.g., $\text{Au}_{81}\text{Si}_{19}$, $T_{rg} \approx 0.65$) are excellent glass formers.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inoue's Three Empirical Rules for Bulk Metallic Glass Formation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Inoue (2000) formulated **three rules** that a multicomponent alloy must satisfy to form a bulk metallic glass (BMG) at low cooling rates:

| Rule | Statement | Physical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | Multi-component system (≥ 3 elements) | Atomic size mismatch frustrates crystallization; enhances $\sigma$, suppresses $\Delta g_{v}$ |
| **II** | Significant atomic size mismatch ($\geq 12\%$) | Dense, topologically disordered packing; reduced free volume |
| **III** | Negative heat of mixing among constituents | Strong chemical short-range order stabilizes the liquid and suppresses nucleation |

**Example compliant system:** $\text{Zr}_{41.2}\text{Ti}_{13.8}\text{Cu}_{12.5}\text{Ni}_{10}\text{Be}_{22.5}$ — a 5-component alloy with strong negative heats of mixing and a critical casting thickness of **$\sim 10 \text{ cm}$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Formation Routes to Metastable Alloys (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    melt["Liquid Alloy Melt"]
    rsq["Rapid Solidification Processing"]
    ma["Other Non-Equilibrium Routes"]

    melt --> rsq
    melt --> ma

    rsq --> ms1["Melt Spinning\n10^5 to 10^6 K per s"]
    rsq --> ms2["Splat Quenching\n10^6 to 10^8 K per s"]
    rsq --> ms3["Atomization\n10^3 to 10^5 K per s"]
    rsq --> ms4["Melt Extraction"]
    rsq --> ms5["Copper Mold Casting\n1 to 100 K per s for BMG"]

    ma --> ma1["Mechanical Alloying\nBall Milling"]
    ma --> ma2["Ion Implantation"]
    ma --> ma3["Vapor Deposition\nPVD / Sputtering"]
    ma --> ma4["Severe Plastic Deformation"]

    ms1 --> out1["Amorphous Ribbons\n20 to 50 micron thickness"]
    ms2 --> out2["Amorphous Foils / Flakes"]
    ms3 --> out3["Amorphous Powder Spheres"]
    ms5 --> out4["Bulk Metallic Glass\nup to several cm"]
    ma1 --> out5["Nanocrystalline / Supersaturated Powders"]
    ma3 --> out6["Amorphous Thin Films"]
```

## 4.2 Free Energy Landscape — Liquid → Metastable → Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Liquid\nHigh G, High S"]
    M["Metastable Amorphous\nIntermediate G"]
    E["Equilibrium Crystal\nLowest G"]

    L -->|Cooling faster than R_c\nTg reached first| M
    M -->|Devitrification\nHeating beyond T_x| N["Nanocrystalline Stage"]
    N -->|Further annealing\nabove T_x| E

    M -.Activation barrier deltaGstar.-> E

    style L fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style M fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style N fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style E fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.3 Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CCT["Continuous Cooling Transformation Schematic"]
        direction LR
        T["Temperature Axis\nTop to Bottom: High to Low"]
        L2["Liquid Region"]
        N["Nose of Crystallization Curve\nTime_min, T_min"]
        G["Glass Region\nBelow Critical Cooling Rate R_c"]
    end

    Rfast["Cooling Curve R1\nR1 greater than R_c\nGlass Formed"] -.passes left of nose.-> G
    Rslow["Cooling Curve R2\nR2 less than R_c\nCrystallization Occurs"] -.hits nose.-> N
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Devitrification (Crystallization) Sequence of a Metallic Glass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["As-Quenched Amorphous Alloy\nG1"]
    A -->|Anneal at T1 below T_g| B["Structural Relaxation\nFree Volume Reduction"]
    B -->|Anneal between T_g and T_x| C["Supercooled Liquid Region\nG2 less than G1"]
    C -->|Anneal above T_x| D["Primary Nanocrystallization\nFine alpha-Fe Grains in Amorphous Matrix"]
    D -->|Prolonged anneal| E["Crystal Growth + Intermetallic Precipitation\nG3 approaching Equilibrium"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**Define metastable alloys. Why are they considered kinetically stable but thermodynamically unstable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Metastable alloys are non-equilibrium alloy phases whose internal energy (Gibbs free energy) is higher than that of the corresponding equilibrium phase but which persist at room temperature because of a large activation barrier $\Delta G^{*}$ that prevents transformation to the equilibrium state. They are *thermodynamically unstable* ($G_{\text{meta}} > G_{\text{eq}}$) but *kinetically stable* because atomic diffusion is too slow at the service temperature to surmount $\Delta G^{*}$. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is meant by the "reduced glass transition temperature" $T_{rg}$, and what is its significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$T_{rg} = T_{g}/T_{l}$ is the ratio of the glass transition temperature to the liquidus temperature. A higher $T_{rg}$ (typically $\geq 0.6$) indicates that the supercooled liquid is highly viscous near $T_{m}$, suppressing nucleation and growth of crystals. Therefore, alloys with high $T_{rg}$ can be vitrified at low cooling rates, which is the empirical basis for bulk metallic glass (BMG) design. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often confuse *metastable* with *unstable*. Always state clearly that metastable ≠ unstable: metastable phases are *long-lived* under service conditions.
> 2. When drawing free-energy diagrams, label the **activation barrier $\Delta G^{*}$** explicitly. Examiners award at least 1 mark for this.
> 3. For processing techniques, students frequently omit the role of *cooling rate*. Always quantify the cooling rate for each process.
> 4. For BMG design, name at least one real alloy system (e.g., Vitreloy 1, $\text{Zr}_{41.2}\text{Ti}_{13.8}\text{Cu}_{12.5}\text{Ni}_{10}\text{Be}_{22.5}$) — a generic answer loses the "application" mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the thermodynamic and kinetic basis for the formation of metastable alloys. Use a free-energy-versus-order-parameter diagram in your answer.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Thermodynamic basis (3 marks):**
Every system seeks to minimize its Gibbs free energy $G = H - TS$. The *equilibrium phase* corresponds to the global minimum on the $G$–composition/temperature surface. However, when a liquid is cooled rapidly, atoms do not have time to rearrange into the equilibrium crystalline configuration. They are frozen into a configuration whose $G$ is locally minimized but is still above the global minimum — this is a **metastable state**. Quantitatively:

$$G_{\text{liquid}} > G_{\text{metastable (amorphous)}} > G_{\text{equilibrium crystal}}$$

**2. Kinetic basis (2 marks):**
The transformation from the metastable state to the equilibrium state requires overcoming an activation barrier $\Delta G^{*}$, primarily for nucleation of the stable crystal. The rate of crystallization is governed by nucleation rate $I$ and growth rate $U$, both of which depend exponentially on temperature through an Arrhenius-like term involving $\Delta G^{*}/k_{B}T$. Below $T_{g}$, atomic mobility is so low that $I \cdot U \to 0$, freezing the structure in its metastable form.

**3. Free-energy schematic (2 marks):**

- Y-axis: Gibbs free energy $G$.
- X-axis: order parameter (e.g., configurational entropy or crystalline fraction).
- Three minima: liquid (shallow, high $G$) → amorphous (intermediate) → equilibrium crystal (deepest).
- Label $\Delta G^{*}$ as the hump separating amorphous from crystal.
- Show cooling trajectory bypassing the nucleation barrier.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the rapid solidification techniques used to produce metastable alloys. Compare melt spinning and splat quenching.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Need for rapid solidification (2 marks):**
To bypass the "nose" of the TTT diagram, cooling rates of $10^{4}$ to $10^{8} \text{ K/s}$ are required. This suppresses heterogeneous nucleation of equilibrium phases and produces amorphous, supersaturated, or nanocrystalline structures.

**2. Melt spinning (3 marks):**
- A jet of molten alloy is ejected onto a rapidly rotating copper wheel (typical surface velocity 20–40 m/s).
- Cooling rate: $10^{5}$ to $10^{6} \text{ K/s}$.
- Product: continuous ribbons 20–50 µm thick and several mm wide.
- Used for soft magnetic alloys (Fe–Si–B, FINEMET) and brazing foils.

**3. Splat quenching (2 marks):**
- A small droplet of melt is pressed between two cold copper anvils (or shot against a chilled plate).
- Cooling rate: $10^{6}$ to $10^{8} \text{ K/s}$ — higher than melt spinning.
- Product: irregular thin flakes/foils of 10–100 µm thickness.
- Suited for laboratory-scale studies and small batch production.

**4. Comparison (additional clarity):**
- Melt spinning → continuous ribbons, scalable to industry.
- Splat quenching → smaller batches, higher cooling rates, more amorphous-prone.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the structure, properties, and applications of metallic glasses.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Structure (2 marks):**
Metallic glasses (amorphous alloys) lack long-range periodic atomic order but exhibit **short-range order** (SRO) and **medium-range order** (MRO) over 0.5–2 nm. The atoms pack densely but with no translational symmetry, resembling a "frozen liquid." X-ray diffraction shows broad diffuse halos rather than sharp Bragg peaks.

**2. Properties (3 marks):**
- **Mechanical:** very high yield strength (up to 2–5 GPa), large elastic strain limit ($\sim 2\%$), high hardness, but limited ductility in tension due to shear-band localization.
- **Magnetic:** excellent soft magnetic behavior — low coercivity, high permeability, low core loss. e.g., Metglas 2605SA1.
- **Chemical:** outstanding corrosion resistance due to absence of grain boundaries and defects.
- **Thermal:** low thermal expansion in some compositions.

**3. Applications (2 marks):**
- Distribution transformer cores (Metglas ribbons).
- High-strength sporting goods (golf clubs, tennis rackets — Liquidmetal Technologies).
- Electronic casings, watch cases, surgical instruments.
- Precision gears and magnetic sensors.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] State and explain Inoue's three empirical rules for bulk metallic glass formation. Give two examples of alloy systems that obey these rules.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Rule I — Multi-component alloy (2 marks):**
The alloy must contain three or more elements. This increases configurational entropy, stabilizes the supercooled liquid, suppresses nucleation of specific crystalline compounds, and frustrates the diffusion-controlled long-range atomic rearrangement needed for crystallization.

**2. Rule II — Atomic size mismatch $\geq 12\%$ (2 marks):**
The constituent atoms must differ significantly in size (by at least 12%). This creates a topologically dense, efficiently packed amorphous structure with low free volume. The dense packing raises $\sigma$ (interfacial energy) and lowers $\Delta g_{v}$, both of which increase $\Delta G^{*}$ and suppress nucleation.

**3. Rule III — Negative heat of mixing (1 mark):**
The constituent elements must exhibit negative enthalpies of mixing. This induces strong chemical short-range order and stabilizes the liquid relative to competing crystalline phases, again suppressing nucleation.

**4. Example alloys (2 marks):**
- **Vitreloy 1:** $\text{Zr}_{41.2}\text{Ti}_{13.8}\text{Cu}_{12.5}\text{Ni}_{10}\text{Be}_{22.5}$ — five components, large size mismatch (Zr, Ti, Be differ markedly), and strong negative $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$. Forms BMG rods up to several cm.
- **FINEMET:** $\text{Fe}_{73.5}\text{Si}_{13.5}\text{B}_{9}\text{Nb}_{3}\text{Cu}_{1}$ — multicomponent, used as soft magnetic nanocrystalline alloy after devitrification annealing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Metastable Alloys**

- **Definition:** A metastable alloy is *thermodynamically unstable* ($G_{\text{meta}} > G_{\text{eq}}$) but *kinetically persistent* due to large activation barrier $\Delta G^{*}$.
- **Key parameter:** $T_{rg} = T_{g}/T_{l} \geq 0.6$ indicates strong glass-forming ability.
- **Critical cooling rate** $R_{c}$ ranges from $10^{-4} \text{ K/s}$ (oxides) to $10^{6} \text{ K/s}$ (conventional metallic glasses) to $\sim 1 \text{ K/s}$ (BMGs).
- **Inoue's three rules** for BMG formation: (i) $\geq 3$ components, (ii) atomic size mismatch $\geq 12\%$, (iii) negative $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$.
- **Rapid solidification techniques:** melt spinning ($10^{5}$–$10^{6}$ K/s, ribbons), splat quenching ($10^{6}$–$10^{8}$ K/s, flakes), atomization ($10^{3}$–$10^{5}$ K/s, powders), copper-mold casting (BMGs).
- **Non-melt routes:** mechanical alloying, ion implantation, PVD sputtering, severe plastic deformation.
- **Types of metastable alloys:** amorphous, quasicrystalline, nanocrystalline, supersaturated solid solution.
- **Devitrification:** amorphous $\xrightarrow{\text{anneal}}$ nanocrystalline $\rightarrow$ equilibrium crystals; characterized by Avrami equation $X(t) = 1 - \exp(-kt^{n})$.
- **Properties:** high yield strength, high elastic limit, excellent soft-magnetic behavior, superior corrosion resistance.
- **Applications:** transformer cores (Metglas), precision gears, biomedical implants, sporting goods (Liquidmetal), watch cases.
- **Classic BMG:** $\text{Zr}_{41.2}\text{Ti}_{13.8}\text{Cu}_{12.5}\text{Ni}_{10}\text{Be}_{22.5}$ (Vitreloy 1).
- **Classic nanocrystalline soft magnet:** $\text{Fe}_{73.5}\text{Si}_{13.5}\text{B}_{9}\text{Nb}_{3}\text{Cu}_{1}$ (FINEMET).
- **Formulae to memorize:** $\Delta G^{*} = 16\pi\sigma^{3}/(3\Delta g_{v}^{2})$; $T_{rg} = T_{g}/T_{l}$; $\Delta T_{x} = T_{x} - T_{g}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High temperature materials: structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High temperature materials: structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Temperature Materials (HTMs)** are a specialized class of engineering materials — predominantly metallic alloys, ceramics, intermetallics, and composites — engineered to retain their **mechanical strength, microstructural integrity, dimensional stability, creep resistance, oxidation resistance, and corrosion resistance** when exposed to sustained operating temperatures typically **above 540 °C (≈ 0.4 T<sub>m</sub> in absolute terms)**, often approaching **0.7 to 0.9 of their absolute melting temperature (T<sub>m</sub>)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, "structure" refers to the **atomic, crystalline, and microstructural arrangement** of these materials, which is deliberately engineered to defeat the three dominant high-temperature degradation modes:

1. **Creep deformation** (time-dependent plastic flow)
2. **Oxidation and hot-corrosion attack**
3. **Microstructural coarsening** (grain growth, precipitate coarsening)

The **homologous temperature** criterion is the universal design benchmark:

$$
T_H = \frac{T_{service}}{T_{melting}} \; (in \; Kelvin)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When **T<sub>H</sub> > 0.4**, conventional strengthening mechanisms (cold work, solid-solution strengthening alone) become inadequate. This threshold defines the onset of the **"high-temperature regime"** for structural materials.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tortoise in a Furnace"

Imagine a load-bearing pillar inside a furnace.

- At room temperature, a **plain carbon steel pillar** carries the roof perfectly (like a healthy bone).
- As the furnace heats to **600 °C**, the steel begins to sag slowly, droop, and rust — its atoms vibrate so violently that dislocations glide freely, and iron atoms react with oxygen.
- A **high-temperature material pillar** (e.g., a nickel-based superalloy or a SiC ceramic) is engineered differently: its atoms are locked in a stiff crystal lattice, protected by a self-healing oxide skin (Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> or SiO<sub>2</sub>), and peppered with microscopic "speed-bumps" (precipitates) that block dislocation motion even at red heat.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** High temperature materials don't simply "survive heat" — they are *structurally redesigned* at the atomic scale to fight three invisible enemies: **heat, time, and chemistry**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of High-Temperature Materials

| Family | Typical T<sub>max</sub> | Example | Dominant Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Superalloys** (Ni, Co, Fe-base) | 800 – 1200 °C | Inconel 718, CMSX-4 | Jet engine turbine blades |
| **Refractory Metals** | 1500 – 2800 °C | W, Mo, Ta, Nb, Cr | Rocket nozzles, heating elements |
| **High-Temperature Ceramics** | 1200 – 2000 °C | SiC, Si<sub>3</sub>N<sub>4</sub>, Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, ZrO<sub>2</sub> | Furnace linings, turbine shrouds |
| **Intermetallics** | 700 – 1100 °C | NiAl, TiAl, FeAl | Turbocharger rotors |
| **Carbon-Carbon Composites** | 2000 °C+ | C/C with SiC coating | Spacecraft re-entry nose cones |
| **High-Temperature Polymers** | 250 – 400 °C | PBI, PI (Kapton) | Wire insulation, gaskets |

---

## 1.4 Why "Structure" Is the King at High Temperatures

At **T<sub>H</sub> > 0.4**, the dominant strengthening mechanisms shift drastically:

- **Dislocation climb** (instead of glide) becomes rate-controlling.
- **Grain boundaries** turn from strength-givers to weakness-zones (they slide and cavitate).
- **Precipitates** coarsen via Ostwald ripening.
- **Oxide scales** either protect or destroy the alloy depending on **Pilling–Bedworth ratio**.

Therefore, the *microstructural strategy* itself must change — which is what the rest of this module addresses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Structural Strategies for High-Temperature Performance

A high-temperature material engineer combines **five microstructural levers**. Mastering these is the core of this topic.

### Strategy 1 — Solid-Solution Strengthening
- Heavy solute atoms (e.g., **W, Mo, Re, Co in Ni**) distort the lattice.
- They raise the **activation energy for dislocation motion**.
- Effective up to ~0.6 T<sub>m</sub>, beyond which solute diffusion lets dislocations bypass.

### Strategy 2 — Precipitation Strengthening (γ′ and γ″ in Ni-superalloys)
- Ordered **L1<sub>2</sub> precipitates** of Ni<sub>3</sub>(Al,Ti) — called **γ′ (gamma prime)** — sit coherently in the FCC γ matrix.
- Dislocations must either **cut** or **bow around** these ordered particles.
- **Volume fraction** of γ′ can reach **60–70%** in modern single-crystal blades.

### Strategy 3 — Grain-Boundary Engineering
- Creep voids nucleate at **transverse grain boundaries**.
- Solution: **Directional solidification (DS)** or **Single-crystal (SX) casting** — eliminate grain boundaries entirely along the stress axis.
- For polycrystalline creep-resistant steels: add **boron and zirconium** to strengthen GBs, or use **dispersion strengthening** with Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>.

### Strategy 4 — Dispersion Strengthening (ODS — Oxide Dispersion Strengthened)
- Nano-scale, **thermodynamically stable** oxide particles (Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, ThO<sub>2</sub>, Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) — typically **10–50 nm**.
- Unlike γ′ which dissolves, dispersoids **never dissolve** at service T.
- Example: **MA956 (Fe-Cr-Al + Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>)** for combustion chambers.

### Strategy 5 — Protective Surface Oxide Layer
- **Selective oxidation**: add **Al, Cr, Si** so the alloy forms a dense, adherent, slow-growing oxide.
- **Alumina (Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) formers** → best above 1000 °C.
- **Chromia (Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) formers** → best up to 950 °C.
- **Silica (SiO<sub>2</sub>) formers** → excellent for SiC/Si<sub>3</sub>N<sub>4</sub> ceramics.

---

## 2.2 The Hall-Petch Inversion at High Temperature

At low T, **finer grains = stronger** (Hall-Petch):

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

At **T > 0.5 T<sub>m</sub>**, the inverse holds — **coarse columnar grains or single crystals** give higher creep life because grain-boundary sliding and cavitation dominate.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Cold = fine grains win. Hot = coarse grains (or no grains!) win.*

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Homologous temperature: $T_H = T_{service}/T_{melt}$ (K) | Defines the high-T regime | dimensionless |
| 2 | Hall-Petch (low T): $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Grain-boundary strengthening | MPa |
| 3 | Norton-Bailey creep: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c / RT)$ | Steady-state creep rate | s<sup>-1</sup> |
| 4 | Larson-Miller parameter: $LMP = T(C + \log t_r)$ with C ≈ 20 | Combines T and time-to-rupture | °C·log(h) |
| 5 | Coble creep (GB diffusion): $\dot{\varepsilon} = A_c \cdot \frac{\sigma \cdot \Omega \cdot D_{GB} \cdot \delta}{d^3 \cdot kT}$ | Creep via grain-boundary diffusion | s<sup>-1</sup> |
| 6 | Nabarro-Herring (lattice diffusion): $\dot{\varepsilon} = A_N \cdot \frac{\sigma \cdot \Omega \cdot D_L}{d^2 \cdot kT}$ | Creep via vacancy flow through lattice | s<sup>-1</sup> |
| 7 | Pilling-Bedworth ratio: $PBR = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}}$ | Predicts if oxide is protective | dimensionless |
| 8 | Orowan bowing stress: $\tau = \dfrac{Gb}{L - 2r}$ | Dislocation bypasses particle | MPa |
| 9 | Threshold stress (ODS): $\dot{\varepsilon} \propto \left(\frac{\sigma - \sigma_{th}}{\sigma_0}\right)^n$ | Dispersoids raise creep threshold | MPa |
| 10 | Coarsening (Ostwald): $r^3 - r_0^3 = K t$ | γ′ particle radius growth | nm<sup>3</sup>·s<sup>-1</sup> |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $L$ = particle spacing, $r$ = particle radius, $D$ = diffusivity, $\Omega$ = atomic volume, $\delta$ = GB width, $Q_c$ = activation energy for creep, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $k$ = Boltzmann constant.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | HT Material Used | Why Structure Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace jet engines** | Ni-superalloy single crystals | γ′ resists creep at 1100 °C in turbine blades |
| **Rocket nozzles (solid)** | W-thoria, C/C composites | Refractory survives 3000 °C oxidizing flow |
| **Gas-fired furnace tubes** | Incoloy 800H, HK40 | Cast austenitic — coarse grains resist carburization |
| **Nuclear reactor cladding** | Zircaloy-4, SiC/SiC | Low neutron absorption + corrosion resistance |
| **Heat-treatment trays** | SiC, mullite | No creep, no oxidation up to 1400 °C |
| **Glass industry** | Mo electrodes, AZS refractory | Wettability + thermal shock resistance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Creep Rate (Norton Power Law)

We start from the **Weertman climb-controlled creep** model for a material containing obstacles (precipitates or dispersoids) of spacing $L$.

**Step 1 — Force on a dislocation line of length $L$:**

The applied shear stress $\tau$ pushes the dislocation against the obstacle. The net force per unit length is:

$$
F = \tau \cdot b
$$

**Step 2 — Rate of climb (one obstacle per length $L$):**

Dislocation climb requires vacancy diffusion. The vacancy flux $J_v$ from the dislocation to a sink (grain boundary) of distance $L$ is given by Fick's first law:

$$
J_v = -D_v \frac{\Delta C_v}{L}
$$

where $D_v$ is the vacancy diffusivity and $\Delta C_v$ is the vacancy concentration difference.

**Step 3 — Expressing $\Delta C_v$ using the chemical potential of vacancies:**

The stress $\tau$ creates a vacancy supersaturation:

$$
\Delta C_v = \frac{C_v^0 \cdot \tau \cdot \Omega}{kT}
$$

where $C_v^0$ is the equilibrium vacancy concentration, $\Omega$ is the atomic volume.

**Step 4 — Climbing velocity:**

$$
v_{climb} = b \cdot J_v = \frac{D_v \, b \, \Omega \, C_v^0}{kT} \cdot \frac{\tau}{L}
$$

Using the Nernst-Einstein relation $D_v \cdot C_v^0 = D_s$ (where $D_s$ is the self-diffusivity), and substituting $D_s = D_0 \exp(-Q_s / RT)$:

$$
v_{climb} = \frac{D_s \, b \, \Omega \, \tau}{kT \, L}
$$

**Step 5 — Strain rate from Orowan's equation:**

$$
\dot{\varepsilon} = \rho \cdot b \cdot v_{climb}
$$

Assuming mobile dislocation density $\rho \propto \tau^2$ (empirical Taylor relation), and $L \propto 1/\tau$ (because higher stress activates closer obstacles):

$$
\dot{\varepsilon} \propto \tau^2 \cdot \tau \cdot \tau = \tau^{n}, \; \text{where } n \approx 3 \text{ to } 5
$$

**Step 6 — Final Norton-Bailey equation:**

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^n \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)
$$

where:
- $A$ is a microstructural constant (depends on $G$, $b$, $L$),
- $n$ is the **creep stress exponent**,
- $Q_c$ is the **activation energy for creep** (≈ self-diffusion $Q_s$ for lattice-diffusion creep).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"The Creep Trinity" — Stress, Time, Temperature."* The exponent $n$ itself tells you the deformation mechanism:
> - $n = 1$ → diffusional (Coble/Nabarro-Herring) creep
> - $n = 2$ → grain-boundary sliding
> - $n = 3$–$5$ → dislocation glide + climb (most alloys)
> - $n = 7$–$40$ → particle-strengthened (ODS alloys have very high apparent $n$)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Larson-Miller Parameter

**Goal:** Combine temperature $T$ (in Kelvin or Rankine) and rupture time $t_r$ (in hours) into a single "thermal severity" number.

**Step 1 — Assume creep rupture follows an Arrhenius-type law:**

$$
t_r = t_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log:**

$$
\ln t_r = \ln t_0 + \frac{Q}{RT}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $Q$:**

$$
Q = R T \left(\ln t_r - \ln t_0\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define the Larson-Miller parameter $P$:**

$$
P = T \left(C + \log t_r\right)
$$

where $C = \log t_0 \cdot 2.303 \approx 20$ (empirical constant for most steels; ≈ 15 for some Ni-alloys).

**Step 5 — Why it works:** If rupture is a thermally activated process, all data points $(T, t_r)$ should collapse onto a single master curve when plotted as $P$ vs. stress $\sigma$.

**Example:** A superalloy survives 1000 h at 900 °C. Its LMP is:

$$
P = (900 + 273) \cdot (20 + \log 1000) = 1173 \times 23 = 26{,}979 \; °C \cdot \log(h)
$$

The same material would then be predicted to last approximately:

$$
t_r \approx \exp\!\left(\frac{2.303 \cdot P}{T} - 2.303 \cdot C\right) \text{ hours at any other temperature}.
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Which Structure Resists What?

| Degradation Mode | Atomic Mechanism | Best Structural Defence | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dislocation glide creep** | Glide on slip planes | Fine coherent precipitates (γ′) | Ni-base superalloy |
| **Dislocation climb creep** | Vacancy diffusion-aided climb | Coherent + incoherent barriers; ODS | MA6000 |
| **Grain-boundary sliding** | GB shear | Eliminate GBs → single crystal | CMSX-10 |
| **Grain-boundary cavitation** | Vacancy condensation at GB | Boron/Zr-doped GBs, large grains | Inconel 718 |
| **Oxidation** | O<sup>−</sup> diffusion inward | Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> / Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> / SiO<sub>2</sub> layer | FeCrAlY |
| **Hot corrosion (Na<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub>)** | Fluxing of protective oxide | High Cr + Si + Al; Coatings | Inconel 738 + aluminide |
| **Particle coarsening** | Ostwald ripening | Slow-diffusing elements (Re, W, Mo); lower $T$ | Re-containing SC alloys |
| **Thermal fatigue** | Cyclic Δσ·α·E | Low α, high conductivity, ductile bond coats | TBC on CMSX-4 |
| **Phase instability** | TCP (σ, μ, Laves) precipitation | PSEUDO-ternary alloy design (PHACOMP) | René N5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Microstructure of a Ni-Base Superalloy (Single Crystal)

This diagram captures the **nanoscopic engineered architecture** of a modern single-crystal nickel-superalloy turbine blade. The γ matrix is reinforced with cuboidal γ′ precipitates, separated by thin γ-matrix corridors. A surface Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> thermally grown oxide (TGO) forms beneath a ceramic topcoat (TBC).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["SINGLE CRYSTAL Ni BASE SUPERALLOY ARCHITECTURE"]
    nodeB["GAMMA MATRIX FCC Ni SOLID SOLUTION WITH Co Cr Mo W Re"]
    nodeC["GAMMA PRIME Ni3AlTi ORDERED L1 SQUARED CUBOIDAL PRECIPITATES 60 to 70 percent VOLUME FRACTION 400 to 500 nm SIZE"]
    nodeD["GAMMA GAMMA PRIME INTERFACES COHERENT LOW MISMATCH DELTA"]
    nodeE["THERMALLY GROWN OXIDE TGO ALPHA AL2O3 1 to 2 MICRON"]
    nodeF["BOND COAT NI PT AL DIFFUSION ZONE"]
    nodeG["YTTRIA STABILIZED ZIRCONIA YSZ TOP COAT 100 to 300 MICRON"]
    nodeH["CREEP LOAD AXIS"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeH

    nodeC -.->|COHERENCY STRAIN DELTA BLOCKS DISLOCATIONS| nodeD
    nodeB -.->|PROVIDES TOUGHNESS DUCTILITY| nodeC
    nodeE -.->|GROWTH DRIVES SPALLATION| nodeF
    nodeF -.->|SUPPLIES Al FOR TGO REGROWTH| nodeE
    nodeG -.->|INSULATES FROM GAS PATH HEAT| nodeE
    nodeH -.->|CREEP STRAIN LOCALISES IN GAMMA CHANNELS| nodeB
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Guide:** The dotted (`.->`) edges capture the *dynamic relationships* — for instance, the γ-γ′ lattice misfit $\Delta$ is the very thing that *blocks dislocations* and gives the alloy its strength at 1100 °C.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Structural Strategy Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS["START CHOOSE HIGH TEMPERATURE MATERIAL"]
    nodeT1["IS SERVICE T BELOW 700 C"]
    nodeT2["IS ENVIRONMENT OXIDISING"]
    nodeT3["IS STRESS HIGH OR LONG DURATION"]
    nodeA1["USE FERRITIC STAINLESS OR NICKEL IRON ALLOY DISPERSION STRENGTHENED"]
    nodeA2["USE AUSTENITIC STAINLESS OR Fe Ni BASE SUPERALLOY WITH Cr2O3 LAYER"]
    nodeA3["USE NICKEL BASE SUPERALLOY WITH GAMMA PRIME PRECIPITATES"]
    nodeA4["USE SINGLE CRYSTAL NICKEL SUPERALLOY PLUS TBC"]
    nodeA5["USE CERAMIC SiC Si3N4 OR REFRACTORY METAL W Mo"]

    nodeS --> nodeT1
    nodeT1 -->|YES| nodeT2
    nodeT1 -->|NO| nodeT3
    nodeT2 -->|MILD| nodeA1
    nodeT2 -->|HARSH HIGH Cr CONTENT NEEDED| nodeA2
    nodeT3 -->|MODERATE LESS THAN 100 MPa 10000 h| nodeA3
    nodeT3 -->|SEVERE JET ENGINE TURBINE| nodeA4
    nodeT3 -->|EXTREME ABOVE 1400 C INERT OR REDUCING| nodeA5
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Creep Deformation Mechanism Map (Schematic)

A deformation-mechanism map (after Ashby-Frost) plots **normalized stress $\sigma / G$** (y-axis) versus **homologous temperature $T/T_m$** (x-axis). Each region is dominated by one mechanism.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR1["REGION A LOW T HIGH SIGMA PLASTIC GLIDE POWER LAW"]
    nodeR2["REGION B HIGH T HIGH SIGMA DISLOCATION CREEP CLIMB n EQUALS 3 TO 5"]
    nodeR3["REGION C HIGH T LOW SIGMA DIFFUSIONAL CREEP NABARRO HERRING n EQUALS 1"]
    nodeR4["REGION D VERY HIGH T NEAR Tm CAVITATION RUPTURE"]
    nodeR5["REGION E LOW T LOW SIGMA ELASTIC NO FLOW"]
    nodeR6["REGION F HIGH T MODERATE SIGMA GRAIN BOUNDARY SLIDING n EQUALS 2"]

    nodeR1 -.->|TRANSITION BY THERMAL ACTIVATION| nodeR2
    nodeR2 -.->|TRANSITION BY LOWERING STRESS| nodeR3
    nodeR2 -.->|TRANSITION BY TOWARDS Tm| nodeR4
    nodeR3 -.->|COMPETES WITH| nodeR6
    nodeR5 -.->|TRANSITION BY LOAD| nodeR1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned to **CO1 / CO2** of PEPET751 Module 1 and follow KTU 2024 Scheme bloom-level tagging. Marks distribution matches the **university ESE pattern** (Part A: 3 marks × short-answer; Part B: 14 marks × with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define homologous temperature. Why is the value 0.4 T<sub>m</sub> considered a threshold for high-temperature material design?**

**Model Answer:**
Homologous temperature is the ratio of the service temperature to the melting temperature, both expressed on the absolute (Kelvin) scale:

$$
T_H = \frac{T_{service}}{T_{melt}}
$$

It is dimensionless. Above $T_H \approx 0.4$, thermally activated processes such as vacancy diffusion, dislocation climb, and grain-boundary sliding become kinetically significant. Conventional low-temperature strengthening mechanisms (cold work, fine grains) lose effectiveness, so the material must be redesigned using creep-resistant strategies — hence 0.4 T<sub>m</sub> is taken as the engineering threshold for the "high-temperature regime." *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the γ′ (gamma prime) phase in nickel-base superalloys? Why is its coherency with the γ matrix important?**

**Model Answer:**
γ′ is an ordered intermetallic precipitate of composition **Ni<sub>3</sub>(Al,Ti)** with an L1<sub>2</sub> crystal structure. It precipitates coherently within the FCC γ (Ni-rich solid-solution) matrix, with a lattice misfit typically below **0.5%**. This coherency produces a long-range strain field that effectively blocks dislocation motion by forcing dislocations to either cut through ordered particles (creating antiphase boundaries requiring extra energy) or bow around them (Orowan looping). γ′ is thermally stable up to ~1150 °C and is the principal source of high-temperature strength in Ni-superalloys. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE pattern: Answer **any one** of the two questions below. Each question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **Norton-Bailey steady-state creep law**. Discuss the physical significance of the creep exponent **n** and how it helps identify the dominant creep mechanism in a high-temperature material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **microstructure of a nickel-base single-crystal superalloy**. Explain the role of (i) γ′ precipitates, (ii) refractory additions (W, Mo, Re), and (iii) the Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> thermally grown oxide in achieving high-temperature capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The Norton-Bailey law expresses the steady-state creep rate $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ as:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)
$$

where $A$ is a microstructural constant, $\sigma$ is the applied stress, $n$ is the stress exponent, $Q_c$ is the activation energy for creep, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Significance of $n$ (Stress Exponent):**
- $n \approx 1$: Diffusional creep — vacancies flow through the lattice (Nabarro-Herring) or grain boundaries (Coble). Common in fine-grained ceramics and low-stress service.
- $n \approx 2$: Grain-boundary sliding dominant, often coupled with diffusion.
- $n \approx 3$ to $5$: Dislocation creep — glide + climb. Most engineering alloys in their service window.
- $n \approx 7$ to $40$: Particle-strengthened (ODS) materials show a "threshold stress" behaviour where the apparent exponent is anomalously high.

**Identifying the mechanism:**
1. Plot $\log \dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ vs $\log \sigma$ → slope gives $n$.
2. Plot $\log \dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ vs $1/T$ at constant $\sigma$ → slope gives $Q_c$.
3. Compare $Q_c$ with $Q_{self-diffusion}$: if $Q_c \approx Q_{SD}$, lattice-diffusion creep is operative; if $\approx 0.5\,Q_{SD}$, grain-boundary diffusion creep is operative.

**Engineering use:** Knowing $n$ and $Q_c$, an engineer can extrapolate short-term lab data to long service lives using the Larson-Miller parameter, ensuring 100,000-hour design life of components like turbine blades. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

A **Ni-base single-crystal (SX) superalloy** (e.g., CMSX-4, René N5, PWA 1484) has a deliberately engineered two-phase microstructure produced by directional solidification to eliminate grain boundaries.

**Microstructure sketch description (refer to Diagram A in Section 4):**
- The **γ matrix** (continuous, ~30–40 vol%) is a disordered FCC Ni solid solution containing Co, Cr, Mo, W, and Re as heavy solutes.
- Embedded in it are **cuboidal γ′ precipitates** of Ni<sub>3</sub>(Al,Ti,Ta) — ordered L1<sub>2</sub> — occupying ~60–70 vol%, with edge length 400–500 nm.
- The lattice misfit $\delta$ is kept small and slightly negative, putting the γ matrix under compression and γ′ under tension, which enhances creep life.

**Role of (i) γ′ precipitates:**
γ′ particles are the principal strength-carriers. Dislocations must cut them in pairs (creating antiphase boundaries) or bow between them. At high temperatures, the cutting mode dominates because the APB energy creates a **positive temperature dependence of strength** — a rare and very desirable property for a structural material.

**Role of (ii) Refractory additions (W, Mo, Re):**
- W and Mo provide strong **solid-solution strengthening** and **slow down γ′ coarsening** by reducing inter-diffusivity in the γ matrix.
- Re (rhenium, 3–6 wt%) partitions to the γ matrix, **dramatically reducing the creep rate** by lowering the stacking-fault energy and slowing dislocation climb. Re also suppresses the formation of brittle TCP (topologically close-packed) phases when alloying is balanced via PHACOMP.

**Role of (iii) Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> thermally grown oxide (TGO):**
The blade is coated with an Al-rich bond coat (e.g., NiPtAl). During service, oxygen diffuses through the outer YSZ thermal-barrier coating and forms a thin, adherent **α-Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>** layer at the bond-coat interface. This TGO is:
- Thermodynamically very stable (ΔG° highly negative).
- Dense and adherent (Pilling–Bedworth ratio ≈ 1.28, ideal).
- A slow-growing barrier to inward oxygen diffusion.
The TGO also acts as a "diffusion marker" — its growth-induced rumpling drives eventual spallation, which is the practical life-limiting failure mode. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Classify **high-temperature materials** into at least four families. For each, give a representative composition, an upper service-temperature limit, and one engineering application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **ODS (oxide-dispersion-strengthened) alloys**? Explain how they differ from γ′-strengthened superalloys in terms of (i) thermal stability, (ii) creep mechanism, and (iii) processing route. Mention one commercial example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

| Family | Composition Example | T<sub>max</sub> | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nickel-base superalloys** | Ni-20Cr-10Co-6Al-2Ti-6W (CMSX-4) | ~1100 °C | Jet-engine single-crystal turbine blades |
| **Refractory metals & alloys** | W-1%La<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> (WL10) | ~2800 °C (inert) | Rocket nozzle inserts, heating filaments |
| **High-temperature ceramics** | SiC, Si<sub>3</sub>N<sub>4</sub>, Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, ZrO<sub>2</sub> | ~1600–2000 °C | Gas-turbine shrouds, heat-exchanger tubes |
| **Intermetallics** | γ-TiAl (Ti-48Al-2Cr-2Nb) | ~750–850 °C | Turbocharger rotors, low-pressure turbine blades |
| **Carbon-carbon composites** | C/C with SiC coating | ~2000 °C (oxidizing) | Re-entry nose-cones, F-1 brake discs |

Additional families include ferritic/austenitic stainless steels (T<sub>max</sub> ≈ 800–1000 °C) for superheater tubes and high-temperature polymers such as PBI (T<sub>max</sub> ≈ 400 °C) for wire insulation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**ODS Alloys — Definition:**
Oxide-Dispersion-Strengthened alloys are metallic matrices (Fe-, Ni-, or Co-base) in which **0.5–2 vol% of extremely fine (10–50 nm), thermodynamically stable oxide particles** (typically Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) are uniformly dispersed. Because the dispersoids are *oxides*, they do not dissolve, melt, or coarsen even at temperatures approaching the alloy's solidus.

**Comparison with γ′-strengthened superalloys:**

| Property | γ′-strengthened superalloy | ODS alloy |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Thermal stability** | γ′ dissolves and coarsens above ~1150 °C; strength drops sharply | Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> is stable to >0.95 T<sub>m</sub>; strength retained much higher |
| **(ii) Creep mechanism** | Dislocation cutting of ordered precipitates; rate-controlled by diffusion + APB energy | Dislocation climb over impenetrable dispersoids; rate controlled by Orowan-threshold stress, very high apparent $n$ |
| **(iii) Processing route** | Cast (poly or SX) + solution + ageing heat-treatment | Mechanical alloying of metal + oxide powder, then HIP or extrusion — powder metallurgy route |

**Commercial example:** **MA956** (Fe-20Cr-5Al-0.5Ti + 0.5 Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) — used in combustion chambers and heat-exchanger tubing in advanced gas turbines; also **PM2000** and **Inconel MA754**. ODS ferritic steels like **MA956** also form a self-healing α-Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> scale, giving both creep and oxidation resistance in one alloy. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing γ′ with γ″.** γ″ (Ni<sub>3</sub>Nb, body-centred tetragonal) is the strengthener in **Inconel 718**, not γ′. Many students wrongly state that 718 is γ′-strengthened — it isn't; its γ″ limits service to ~650 °C.
> 2. **Forgetting that LMP constant C is material-dependent.** Using C = 20 for Ni-alloys is a fair first approximation; for some stainless steels C = 18–22. Always state the assumption.
> 3. **Calling ODS "ceramic composites."** ODS is *metallic* with dispersed ceramic particles. The matrix remains metallic, so ductility is preserved.
> 4. **Mixing up PBR.** Pilling-Bedworth ratio must compare the **oxide volume to the metal volume consumed**, not the metal's atomic volume. A common error.
> 5. **Neglecting the "coherency" requirement** when describing γ′ strengthening. The very reason γ′ works is its *coherency* with γ — incoherent θ′ or σ phases are **not** useful.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Read it the morning of the exam.**

- ✅ **Homologous temperature** $T_H = T/T_m$ (K): $> 0.4$ → high-T regime.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch inverts** above ~0.5 T<sub>m</sub>: go for coarse columnar grains or single crystals.
- ✅ **Five strengthening strategies:** solid-solution, precipitation (γ′), GB engineering, dispersion (ODS), protective oxide.
- ✅ **γ′ = Ni<sub>3</sub>(Al,Ti), L1<sub>2</sub>, ordered, coherent, ~60–70 vol%, 400–500 nm cuboids** in SX superalloys.
- ✅ **γ″ = Ni<sub>3</sub>Nb** in Inconel 718 — service limited to ~650 °C.
- ✅ **Norton-Bailey:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$. Identify mechanism by slope $n$ and activation energy $Q_c$.
- ✅ **Larson-Miller Parameter:** $P = T(C + \log t_r)$ with $C \approx 20$.
- ✅ **PBR** ~1.0–2.0 = protective oxide; <1 = non-protective (porous); >2 = spalling.
- ✅ **ODS alloys** = powder-metallurgy, Y<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> dispersoids, retain strength up to 0.9 T<sub>m</sub>.
- ✅ **Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>** for >1000 °C; **Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>** for <950 °C; **SiO<sub>2</sub>** for SiC/Si<sub>3</sub>N<sub>4</sub> ceramics.
- ✅ **Refractory metals** (W, Mo, Ta, Nb) suffer catastrophic oxidation — must be coated or used in vacuum/inert atmosphere.
- ✅ **Ashby deformation maps** distinguish glide ($n$ = 3–5), diffusional ($n$ = 1), and GB sliding ($n$ = 2) regimes.
- ✅ **Lattice misfit $\delta$** between γ and γ′ must be small and tuned for cuboidal rafting — drives creep life.
- ✅ **Re, W, Mo** slow γ′ coarsening — they reduce matrix diffusivity.
- ✅ **TBC architecture**: substrate → bond coat (NiPtAl) → TGO (Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) → YSZ topcoat.

---

**End of Topic Notes — High Temperature Materials: Structure (Module 1, PEPET751).**
