# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Materials Processing** is defined as the science and engineering of transforming raw materials (metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites) into finished products with the desired geometry, microstructure, properties, and surface quality through controlled application of heat, pressure, chemical reaction, or a combination thereof.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (aligned with NEP 2020), Materials Processing is recognized as the **"M" in the classic Materials Tetrahedron** (*Structure → Processing → Properties → Performance*). Any modification at the processing stage fundamentally alters the microstructure, which in turn dictates the macroscopic properties and final in-service performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Processing):** The 2024 Scheme expects students to compare primary shaping (casting, forming, molding), secondary shaping (machining, joining), and tertiary operations (heat treatment, surface modification) across all four classes of engineering materials.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Materials Processing like **baking a cake**:
- The **raw materials** (flour, eggs, sugar) are your *ingots, powders, or monomers*.
- The **recipe and method** (mixing, baking temperature, duration) are your *processing parameters*.
- The **oven environment** is your *processing atmosphere / temperature / pressure*.
- The **final cake texture** (spongy, dense) is your *resulting microstructure*.

If you change the oven temperature or baking time with the same ingredients, you get a completely different cake. Similarly, the same raw aluminum can become a soft foil, a hard plate, or a brittle casting depending solely on **how** it is processed.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (For Quick Recall)

| Material Class | Primary Processing Routes |
|----------------|--------------------------|
| Metals | Casting, Forming (Rolling/Forging/Extrusion), Powder Metallurgy, Joining |
| Ceramics | Powder Compaction + Sintering, Slip Casting, Hot Pressing, Sol-Gel |
| Polymers | Extrusion, Injection Molding, Blow Molding, Thermoforming, Compression Molding |
| Composites | Hand Lay-up, Filament Winding, Pultrusion, Resin Transfer Molding (RTM) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Most 14-mark Module 1 questions demand a *comparative* answer (e.g., "Compare casting and forging of metals" or "Explain processing of thermosetting vs thermoplastic polymers"). Always prepare side-by-side tables.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly used in processing problems:

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Strain-Rate Range:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{4} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ (industry bench)
- **Typical Sintering Temperature for Ceramics:** $T_{\text{sint}} \approx (0.5 \text{ to } 0.85) \, T_m$ (in Kelvin)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Materials Processing

### Pillar A — Shaping (Primary Forming)
Converting bulk raw material into a near-net shape using mechanical, thermal, or chemical energy.

### Pillar B — Joining (Assembly)
Permanently bonding two or more components (welding, brazing, soldering, adhesive bonding, mechanical fastening).

### Pillar C — Surface Modification (Enhancement)
Altering only the outermost layer without changing bulk geometry (carburizing, nitriding, shot peening, coatings).

### Pillar D — Heat Treatment (Microstructural Tailoring)
Using controlled heating/cooling cycles to manipulate phase fractions, grain size, and residual stresses.

## 2.2 Analytical Logic Flow (Step-by-Step Logic Chain)

1. **Input → Process → Output Framework:** Every processing operation can be abstracted as $f(\text{Input Material, Process Parameters}) \rightarrow \text{Output Product}$.
2. **Energy Density Hierarchy:**
   - Casting / Solidification: ~ $\mathbf{10^{5}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{7}}$ J/m³ (latent heat release)
   - Plastic Forming: ~ $\mathbf{10^{7}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{9}}$ J/m³ (plastic work)
   - Machining: ~ $\mathbf{10^{9}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{11}}$ J/m³ (high local strain)
3. **Processing Map Logic (Ashby's Approach):** Plot processing temperature (homologous $T/T_m$) versus strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ to identify safe "windows" for hot working, cold working, and superplastic forming.
4. **Defect Mechanism Mapping:** Each process produces characteristic defects (e.g., casting → porosity; forming → cracks; sintering → residual porosity; welding → HAZ cracks).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Tables:** All absolute-value bars use `\vert` so the markdown table parser does not break.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_f = K \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | Flow stress during plastic forming (Power-law) | Pa | Hot working range |
| 2 | $m = \dfrac{\partial \ln \sigma}{\partial \ln \dot{\varepsilon}}$ | Strain-rate sensitivity index | dimensionless | $0 \le m \le 1$ |
| 3 | $T_H = \dfrac{T_{\text{process}}}{T_m}$ | Homologous temperature | dimensionless | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \ln \left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | True (logarithmic) strain in rolling/forging | dimensionless | Compression |
| 5 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v \, \tan \alpha}{r}$ | Strain rate in wire drawing | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | $v$ = drawing speed |
| 6 | $P_{\text{forge}} = K_f \, \bar{\sigma}_f \, A$ | Forging force estimation | N | Hot/cold forging |
| 7 | $\Delta V = \rho_m \, V$ | Volumetric shrinkage allowance in casting | $\text{m}^3$ | Pattern design |
| 8 | $J = \dfrac{D \, \Delta C}{\delta}$ | Diffusive flux (sintering) | $\text{mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$ | Fick's 1st law |
| 9 | $\tau_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{injection}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$ | Injection-molding cycle time | s | Polymer forming |
| 10 | $\Delta G_{\text{sint}} = \gamma_{sv} \, dA_{sv}$ | Driving force for sintering | J | Negative $\Delta G$ drives densification |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula 1 & 2** ($\sigma_f = K \dot{\varepsilon}^m$) — Directly used in **forging press design**, **rolling mill motor sizing**, and **superplastic forming of aerospace Ti-alloys**.
- **Formula 3** ($T_H$) — Determines whether the material behaves superplastically ($T_H > 0.5$ and $m > 0.3$).
- **Formula 10** ($\Delta G_{\text{sint}}$) — Forms the foundation of **powder metallurgy** and **ceramic sintering** economics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Strain-Rate Sensitivity Index ($m$) from Experimental Data

The strain-rate sensitivity index is a *central* concept in KTU Module 1 because it dictates the formability regime.

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical constitutive relation:**

$$\sigma_f = K \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides to linearize:**

$$\ln \sigma_f = \ln K + m \ln \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize this as a straight line in $(\ln \dot{\varepsilon}, \ln \sigma_f)$ space with slope $m$ and intercept $\ln K$.**

**Step 4 — Differentiate implicitly with respect to $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$:**

$$\frac{\partial (\ln \sigma_f)}{\partial (\ln \dot{\varepsilon})} = m$$

**Step 5 — Therefore the formal definition is recovered:**

$$m = \frac{\partial \ln \sigma_f}{\partial \ln \dot{\varepsilon}}$$

This is the **slope of the log-log plot** used in KTU lab experiments with a Gleeble thermomechanical simulator.

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

A student performs a hot-compression test on an aluminum alloy and records the following data:

| $\dot{\varepsilon}$ ($\text{s}^{-1}$) | Flow Stress $\sigma_f$ (MPa) |
|--------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 0.001 | 30 |
| 0.01  | 45 |
| 0.1   | 67 |
| 1.0   | 100 |

**Step A — Take logarithms (base 10) of both columns:**

$$\log \dot{\varepsilon}: -3, -2, -1, 0$$

$$\log \sigma_f: 1.477, 1.653, 1.826, 2.000$$

**Step B — Apply least-squares slope formula:**

$$m = \frac{N \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{N \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$

With $N = 4$:

$$\sum x = -6, \quad \sum y = 6.956, \quad \sum xy = 10.557, \quad \sum x^2 = 14$$

$$m = \frac{4(10.557) - (-6)(6.956)}{4(14) - 36} = \frac{42.228 + 41.736}{56 - 36} = \frac{83.964}{20} = 0.1998 \approx 0.20$$

**Step C — Interpret the result:** $m \approx 0.20$ indicates the alloy is in the **hot-working regime** (typically $m$ between 0.1 and 0.3 for non-superplastic materials).

**Step D — Find $K$:** Using $\sigma_f = K \dot{\varepsilon}^m$ at $\dot{\varepsilon} = 1.0$, $\sigma_f = 100$ MPa $\Rightarrow K = 100$ MPa.

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = 100 \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{0.20} \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Forging Force Estimation Under Plane Strain

For a flat-disc forging under plane-strain compression (no friction):

**Step 1 — Begin with the elemental force balance:**

$$dP = \bar{\sigma}_f \, dA$$

**Step 2 — For a rectangular billet of width $w$ and instantaneous height $h$, integrate over the projected area:**

$$P = \int_A \bar{\sigma}_f \, dA = \bar{\sigma}_f \, w \, b$$

where $b$ is the breadth.

**Step 3 — In the presence of friction (Coulomb's law), introduce the friction hill factor:**

$$P_{\text{friction}} = P_{\text{no-friction}} \left(1 + \frac{2 \, \mu \, b}{3 \, h}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P = \bar{\sigma}_f \, w \, b \left(1 + \frac{2 \, \mu \, b}{3 \, h}\right)}$$

This is the **friction-hill equation** — a KTU favourite for 7-mark derivations.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Casting vs. Forging (High-Yield)

| Parameter | Casting | Forging |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Starting Material | Molten metal | Solid ingot / billet |
| Energy Required | Heat of fusion + pouring work | Plastic deformation work |
| Typical Defects | Porosity, shrinkage cavities, hot tears | Cracks, laps, folds |
| Grain Structure | Columnar / dendritic | Refined, directional, fiber flow |
| Mechanical Properties | Lower (isotropic in cast) | Higher (anisotropic if not recrystallized) |
| Tooling Cost | Pattern cost (moderate) | Die cost (high for closed-die) |
| Production Rate | High (continuous cast) | Moderate (per-stroke) |
| Material Utilization | 80 – 95 % | 70 – 90 % |
| Suitable Alloys | All castable alloys | Ductile alloys only |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Polymer Processing Routes

| Process | Polymer State | Stress State | Typical Products |
|---------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| Extrusion | Molten thermoplastics | Shear + pressure | Pipes, sheets, films |
| Injection Molding | Molten thermoplastics | Hydrostatic pressure | Complex 3D parts |
| Blow Molding | Parison (molten tube) | Biaxial stretch | Bottles, tanks |
| Compression Molding | Thermoset preform | Compressive | Electrical switches, panels |
| Thermoforming | Sheet (rubbery state) | Vacuum / pressure | Cups, trays, dashboards |
| Rotational Molding | Powder | Gravity-driven | Large hollow tanks |
| Calendering | Compound | Compression + shear | PVC sheets, floor tiles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Classification of Materials Processing

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete decision flow used in industry to select a processing route.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Selection] --> B{Material Class?}
    B -- Metal --> C[Metal Processing]
    B -- Ceramic --> D[Ceramic Processing]
    B -- Polymer --> E[Polymer Processing]
    B -- Composite --> F[Composite Processing]

    C --> C1[Casting]
    C --> C2[Forming - Rolling Forging Extrusion Drawing]
    C --> C3[Powder Metallurgy]
    C --> C4[Joining - Welding Brazing Soldering]

    D --> D1[Powder Preparation]
    D1 --> D2[Forming - Pressing Slip Casting Injection]
    D2 --> D3[Sintering]
    D3 --> D4[Optional Hot Isostatic Pressing]

    E --> E1[Thermoplastics]
    E --> E2[Thermosets]
    E1 --> E1a[Extrusion]
    E1 --> E1b[Injection Molding]
    E1 --> E1c[Blow Molding]
    E1 --> E1d[Thermoforming]
    E2 --> E2a[Compression Molding]
    E2 --> E2b[Transfer Molding]
    E2 --> E2c[Reaction Injection Molding]

    F --> F1[Fiber Selection]
    F --> F2[Matrix Selection]
    F1 --> F3[Layup Methods]
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F3a[Hand Layup]
    F3 --> F3b[Filament Winding]
    F3 --> F3c[Pultrusion]
    F3 --> F3d[Resin Transfer Molding]
```

## 4.2 Ashby Processing Map (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Homologous Temperature T over Tm] --> Q{Low T and Low Strain Rate}
    P --> R{High T and High Strain Rate}
    P --> S{High T and Low Strain Rate}
    P --> T{Low T and High Strain Rate}

    Q --> Q1[Cold Working Region]
    R --> R1[Hot Working Region]
    S --> S1[Superplastic Forming Region]
    T --> T1[Cold Working and Machining Region]
```

## 4.3 Polymer Injection Molding Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Cycle] --> B[Clamp Mould Closed]
    B --> C[Inject Molten Polymer into Cavity]
    C --> D[Pack and Hold under Pressure]
    D --> E[Cooling Phase - Solidification]
    E --> F[Mould Opens]
    F --> G[Eject Finished Part]
    G --> H{Clamp Again}
    H --> B
```

## 4.4 Ceramic Processing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Ceramic Powder] --> B[Powder Preparation - Milling and Sieving]
    B --> C[Add Binder and Lubricant]
    C --> D[Forming - Pressing or Casting]
    D --> E[Green Body - Low Density]
    E --> F[Sintering - High Temperature Diffusion]
    F --> G[Dense Sintered Component]
    G --> H{Optional Finishing}
    H -- Yes --> I[Grinding and Polishing]
    H -- No --> J[Final Inspection]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]

**"Define homologous temperature and state its significance in hot working of metals."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Homologous temperature $T_H$ is the ratio of the working temperature $T$ to the melting temperature $T_m$ of the material, both expressed in Kelvin:

$$T_H = \frac{T}{T_m}$$

It is a dimensionless number that allows comparison of processing severity across different materials. Significance:
1. Hot working is generally defined as $T_H > 0.6$ — recovery and recrystallization occur during deformation, preventing strain hardening.
2. Cold working occurs at $T_H < 0.3$ — strain hardening dominates.
3. Warm working occurs at $0.3 < T_H < 0.6$.

*(Award full marks if all three working regimes are listed with the defining equation.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]

**"Distinguish between primary and secondary shaping processes with two examples each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Primary Shaping | Secondary Shaping |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| Purpose | Converts raw material into a useful shape | Modifies the primary shape to achieve final dimensions / finish |
| Examples | Casting, forging, extrusion, injection molding | Machining, grinding, polishing, drilling |

Examples (2 each, 2 marks):
- *Primary:* Sand casting of pump housing; Injection molding of a polymer gear.
- *Secondary:* Turning of a forged shaft; Surface grinding of a die.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**"With neat sketches, explain the various metal casting processes. Discuss the defects encountered in cast products and their remedies."**

#### Part (a) — Explain Casting Processes (7 Marks)

**Sand Casting:**
A pattern made of wood or metal is placed in a sand mold bounded by a flask. Molten metal is poured through a sprue, flows through runners and gates, fills the cavity, and solidifies. The flask is split into cope (top) and drag (bottom). After solidification, the sand mold is broken to retrieve the casting.

**Investment Casting (Lost-Wax Process):**
A wax pattern is coated with a ceramic slurry, dried, and heated to melt out the wax — leaving a thin ceramic shell. Molten metal is poured into this shell. Used for aerospace turbine blades with intricate geometries.

**Die Casting:**
Molten metal is injected under high pressure (10–140 MPa) into a reusable steel die. Two variants:
- **Hot-chamber die casting** — for low-melting alloys (Zn, Mg).
- **Cold-chamber die casting** — for high-melting alloys (Al, Cu).

**Centrifugal Casting:**
Molten metal is poured into a rotating mold. Centrifugal force pushes the metal against the mold wall, producing hollow cylindrical parts (pipes, bushings) with a fine-grained outer surface.

**Continuous Casting:**
Molten metal is continuously poured through a water-cooled copper mold. The partially solidified strand is withdrawn and cut into billets or slabs.

#### Part (b) — Casting Defects and Remedies (7 Marks)

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Shrinkage cavity | Liquid contraction during solidification | Use risers to feed liquid metal; control cooling rate |
| Porosity (gas) | Trapped gases (H₂, N₂) | Degassing; use dry molds; proper pouring temperature |
| Hot tears | Tensile stress during cooling | Use chills to control solidification direction; alloy modification |
| Cold shuts | Two streams of metal meet but don't fuse | Higher pouring temperature; proper gating design |
| Sand inclusions | Molding sand erodes | Stronger molds; filters in gating system; proper pouring rate |
| Misruns | Metal solidifies before filling cavity | Higher pouring temperature; better gating; thinner sections |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly *describe* the casting processes but forget to **draw labelled diagrams**. A neat sketch of sand-casting flask components (cope, drag, sprue, runner, gate, riser) carries **at least 2 marks** in the valuation key. Always include a figure even if it is hand-drawn.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**"Explain the various polymer processing techniques with emphasis on injection molding and extrusion. Compare their products and applications."**

#### Part (a) — Injection Molding (7 Marks)

The injection-molding cycle consists of four stages:
1. **Feeding & Melting** — Polymer granules are fed from a hopper into a heated barrel where a rotating screw (or plunger in older machines) melts them by conduction and shear.
2. **Injection** — The melt is injected under high pressure (35 – 140 MPa) through a nozzle into a closed mold cavity.
3. **Cooling & Solidification** — The part cools in the mold until it reaches the ejection temperature (typically 50–80 °C below the heat-distortion temperature).
4. **Ejection** — Ejector pins push the solidified part out of the mold.

Cycle time $t_{\text{cycle}}$ is given by:

$$\tau_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{inject}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$$

**Key components:** hopper, heated barrel, reciprocating screw, non-return valve, nozzle, clamped mold with cooling channels, ejector pins.

**Advantages:** High repeatability, low scrap, complex geometries with tight tolerances.

#### Part (b) — Extrusion and Comparison (7 Marks)

**Extrusion:**
Polymer granules are fed into a hopper and conveyed by a rotating screw through a heated barrel. The melt is forced through a die of the desired cross-section to produce a continuous profile (pipe, sheet, film, rod). Downstream equipment includes calibration tanks, cooling baths, haul-offs, and cutters.

**Process Comparison:**

| Feature | Injection Molding | Extrusion |
|---------|-------------------|------------|
| Output | Discrete parts | Continuous profile |
| Complexity | High (3D) | Low (uniform cross-section) |
| Production Rate | Moderate (cycle-bound) | High (continuous) |
| Typical Products | Gears, housings, buckets | Pipes, tubes, sheets, cables |
| Wall-Thickness Control | Excellent | Limited by cooling uniformity |

**Applications:**
- **Injection Molding:** Automotive dashboards, mobile-phone housings, medical syringes, toys.
- **Extrusion:** PVC window profiles, HDPE water pipes, polymer sheets for thermoforming, cable insulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly state that *thermosets* can be processed by injection molding. **Correct statement:** Standard injection molding processes *thermoplastics*. Thermosets require **Reaction Injection Molding (RIM)** where polymerization occurs inside the mold. Mixing this up costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Materials Tetrahedron** — Structure, Processing, Properties, Performance. Always anchor your answer here.
- [x] **Homologous temperature** $T_H = T / T_m$ defines the working regime (cold / warm / hot).
- [x] **Strain-rate sensitivity** $m$ governs formability. $m > 0.3$ indicates superplasticity.
- [x] **Metal casting** defects — shrinkage, porosity, hot tears, cold shuts. Always pair each defect with its remedy.
- [x] **Forging force** = friction-hill equation $P = \bar{\sigma}_f w b \left(1 + \frac{2 \mu b}{3 h}\right)$.
- [x] **Powder metallurgy sequence** — Powder → Compaction → Sintering → Optional HIP.
- [x] **Ceramic processing** uses $T_{\text{sint}} \approx (0.5 \text{ to } 0.85) T_m$ to drive diffusion.
- [x] **Polymer injection molding** is for **thermoplastics**, not thermosets (use **RIM** instead).
- [x] **Extrusion** gives *continuous profiles*; **injection molding** gives *discrete 3D parts*.
- [x] **Composites** — Hand lay-up (low cost, low volume), Filament winding (pressure vessels), Pultrusion (constant cross-section), RTM (medium volume).
- [x] **Ashby Processing Map** is the universal tool for process selection — plot $T/T_m$ vs $\dot{\varepsilon}$.
- [x] **Drawing neat diagrams** (with labels) carries **2+ marks** in KTU long answers.
- [x] **Cycle-time equation** for injection molding: $\tau = t_{\text{inj}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$.
- [x] **Always end comparative answers with a summary table** — KTU examiners actively look for this format.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Valuation Tip:** For 14-mark Module 1 questions, the official marking scheme typically allocates **2 marks for diagrams**, **2 marks for comparative tables**, and **10 marks for textual explanation**. A student who provides only text — even excellent text — loses up to 4 marks. Always include at least one figure and one comparison table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical behaviour and oxidation resistance of stainless steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanical behaviour and oxidation resistance of stainless steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Stainless Steels

**Stainless Steels** are iron-based alloys containing a minimum of **10.5% chromium (Cr)** by mass, often combined with other alloying elements such as nickel (Ni), molybdenum (Mo), titanium (Ti), and niobium (Nb). The chromium content is the critical parameter that imparts a self-healing, passive chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃) film on the surface, which prevents further oxidation and corrosion in aggressive environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The minimum **10.5 wt% Cr** threshold is the universal demarcation line below which an Fe-Cr alloy is classified merely as a "heat-resisting steel" and not a true "stainless steel."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a warrior (the steel) who dresses in a magical, invisible cloak (the **Cr₂O₃ passive layer**). Even when enemy arrows (oxygen, chlorides, acids) strike the surface, the cloak instantly regenerates and repels damage. The chromium inside the steel continuously "donates" itself to reform this cloak the moment it is scratched. This is exactly what makes stainless steel "stain-LESS."

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Stainless steel = Iron + ≥ 10.5% Cr + (optional Ni, Mo, etc.). The Cr creates a passive oxide skin that makes the metal corrosion-resistant in air, water, and many chemicals.

## 1.3 Mechanical Behaviour – Overview

**Mechanical behaviour** refers to the response of stainless steel to applied loads, including:
- **Elastic and plastic deformation**
- **Strain hardening (work hardening)**
- **Strength (yield, ultimate tensile)**
- **Ductility (% elongation)**
- **Hardness (Rockwell, Brinell, Vickers)**
- **Toughness (impact energy)**
- **Fatigue and creep at elevated temperatures**

## 1.4 Oxidation Resistance – Overview

**Oxidation resistance** is the ability of a material to resist chemical attack by oxygen, water vapour, and other oxidising atmospheres — particularly at **elevated temperatures**. The protective Cr₂O₃ layer grows by **solid-state diffusion** and follows predictable rate laws (parabolic, linear, logarithmic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** The **higher the Cr content**, the **better the oxidation resistance**. Additions of Al, Si, and rare-earth elements (Ce, La) further enhance high-temperature scale adherence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stainless Steels (Mechanical & Oxidation Perspective)

Stainless steels are classified into **five major families** based on their crystal structure at room temperature, which in turn dictates their mechanical and corrosion behaviour:

| Family | Crystal Structure | Typical Cr (%) | Typical Ni (%) | Key Mechanical Trait | Oxidation/Corrosion Trait |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Austenitic** | FCC (γ) | 16–26 | 8–22 | Excellent ductility, high toughness, non-magnetic, work hardens strongly | Best general corrosion resistance, good high-T oxidation |
| **Ferritic** | BCC (α) | 10.5–30 | < 1 | Moderate strength, magnetic, low ductility at thick sections | Good oxidation resistance, susceptible to pitting |
| **Martensitic** | BCT (α′) | 10.5–18 | < 1 | High hardness, high strength, magnetic, heat-treatable | Moderate corrosion resistance (lower Cr) |
| **Duplex** | FCC + BCC (50:50) | 19–28 | 4–8 | Yield strength ~2× austenitic, good toughness | Excellent pitting + SCC resistance, PREN 25–40 |
| **Precipitation-Hardening (PH)** | Martensitic + precipitates | 12–17 | 4–9 | Very high strength after aging (e.g. 17-4 PH ~ 1310 MPa UTS) | Good corrosion resistance comparable to austenitic |

## 2.2 Mechanical Behaviour – Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Strength and Hardening Mechanisms

Stainless steels derive their strength from four primary mechanisms:

1. **Solid-solution strengthening** (by Cr, Ni, Mo, C, N interstitial atoms)
2. **Work / strain hardening** (dominant in austenitic grades)
3. **Grain-boundary strengthening** (Hall-Petch effect: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$)
4. **Precipitation hardening** (in PH grades via Cu, Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Al, β-NiAl)

### 2.2.2 Austenitic Stainless Steels – Work Hardening

Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., **304, 316, 321, 347**) exhibit a remarkable **work-hardening rate** due to the metastable FCC → BCC (or HCP ε-martensite) transformation induced by plastic deformation at room temperature.

- **True stress – true strain** behaviour is described by the **Hollomon equation**:
$$\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T^n$$
where $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain-hardening exponent.
- For austenitic SS, $n$ typically lies in the range **0.30 – 0.55**, compared to ~0.10 – 0.20 for low-carbon steels.

### 2.2.3 Martensitic Stainless Steels – Hardening

Martensitic grades (e.g., **410, 420, 440C**) are hardened by **quenching from the austenite phase field**, then tempered. Hardness values up to **~60 HRC** (440C) are achievable.

### 2.2.4 Creep and High-Temperature Strength

At service temperatures above **~ 0.4 T_m** (where T_m is the melting point in Kelvin), stainless steels suffer **creep**. Austenitic grades are preferred for high-T service because:
- Their FCC structure has more slip systems → better creep ductility
- They retain strength up to ~ 870 °C (Type 310, 309)
- They form a stable, slow-growing Cr₂O₃ scale

### 2.2.5 Fatigue Behaviour

Stainless steels generally exhibit **good fatigue resistance**, but surface finish, residual stresses, and corrosive environment (corrosion fatigue) drastically reduce the **endurance limit** ($\sigma_e$). Austenitic SS in air has $\sigma_e \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$ × UTS.

## 2.3 Oxidation Resistance – Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 The Passive Film

When Cr ≥ 10.5% is present in solid solution, exposure to an oxidising medium produces a thin, adherent, and self-repairing **chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃) film** of thickness ~ 1–3 nm. This film:
- Acts as a **diffusion barrier** to oxygen and metal cations
- Has a **Pilling-Bedworth ratio (PBR)** ≈ **2.0** → compressive, adherent
- Thermodynamically stable up to ~ **900 °C** in air

### 2.3.2 Wagner-Hauffe Rules (Semiconductor Oxide Behaviour)

Cr₂O₃ is a **p-type semiconductor** oxide. The Wagner-Hauffe rule states:

> For a p-type oxide, **lower-valency** metal cations (e.g., Cr³⁺ vs Fe³⁺) **decrease** oxidation rate by reducing cation vacancy concentration.

Thus, **Cr lowers the oxidation rate** of Fe-Cr alloys and is the fundamental reason Cr is the universal "stainlessing" element.

### 2.3.3 High-Temperature Oxidation Kinetics

The oxidation kinetics of stainless steels at elevated temperatures typically follow one of three rate laws:

| Rate Law | Equation | Behaviour | Typical Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $\Delta m / A = k_l \, t$ | Constant rate, non-protective | Very high T, poor scales |
| Parabolic | $(\Delta m / A)^2 = k_p \, t$ | Diffusion-controlled, protective | Most stainless steels at 600–900 °C |
| Logarithmic | $\Delta m / A = k_{log} \log(at + 1)$ | Very slow, very thin film | Low T, thin films |

where:
- $\Delta m / A$ = mass gain per unit area (mg/cm²)
- $t$ = exposure time (s or h)
- $k_l, k_p, k_{log}$ = rate constants (temperature-dependent)

### 2.3.4 Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)

For marine / chloride-rich environments, the localised corrosion resistance is ranked by:

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

- PREN < 25 → poor (304, 430)
- PREN 25–32 → moderate (316, 904L)
- PREN > 32 → excellent (duplex, super-austenitic 254 SMO, AL-6XN)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula:** Always remember the PREN equation — it is a guaranteed KTU question in ESE.

### 2.3.5 Effect of Alloying Elements on Oxidation

| Element | Effect on Oxidation/Corrosion |
|---|---|
| **Cr** | ↑↑ Passive Cr₂O₃ film; primary stainlessing element |
| **Ni** | ↑ Austenite stability; promotes protective spinels (NiCr₂O₄) |
| **Mo** | ↑↑ Pitting resistance, especially in Cl⁻ media |
| **Si, Al** | ↑↑ High-T oxidation (form SiO₂, Al₂O₃ sub-layers) |
| **Ti, Nb** | ↑ Stabilise against Cr-carbide precipitation (sensitisation) |
| **Mn, S** | ↓ Form non-protective sulphides, reduce oxidation resistance |
| **C** | ↓ Combines with Cr → Cr-depletion at grain boundaries (sensitisation) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hollomon (work-hardening) | $\sigma_T = K \, \varepsilon_T^n$ | $K$ in MPa, $n$ dimensionless |
| 2 | Hall-Petch (grain size) | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ | $d$ in metres |
| 3 | PREN | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\%\text{Mo} + 16\%\text{N}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Parabolic rate | $(\Delta m/A)^2 = k_p \, t$ | $k_p$ in mg²/cm⁴·s |
| 5 | Linear rate | $\Delta m/A = k_l \, t$ | $k_l$ in mg/cm²·s |
| 6 | Arrhenius for k | $k = A \, \exp(-Q / RT)$ | $Q$ = activation energy (kJ/mol) |
| 7 | Pilling-Bedworth ratio | $\text{PBR} = V_{oxide} / V_{metal}$ | PBR ≈ 1–2 protective; < 1 or > 2 non-protective |
| 8 | Endurance limit (approx.) | $\sigma_e \approx 0.4 - 0.5 \times \sigma_{UTS}$ | For austenitic SS in air |
| 9 | % Elongation | $\%EL = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | Ductility indicator |
| 10 | Stress – Strain | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ (elastic) | $E \approx 200$ GPa (austenitic SS) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parabolic Oxidation Rate Constant from Fick's First Law

We will now **derive** the parabolic rate equation that governs the growth of the Cr₂O₃ scale on stainless steel at elevated temperatures.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let us consider a flat stainless-steel coupon exposed to oxygen at temperature $T$. A Cr₂O₃ layer of instantaneous thickness $x$ grows on the surface. The rate of oxidation is controlled by the **diffusion of metal cations (Cr³⁺)** outward (or O²⁻ inward) through the oxide scale.

### 3.1.2 Apply Fick's First Law

The flux $J$ of cations diffusing through the scale is given by:

$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$

Since the cation concentration difference across the scale is $\Delta C$ and the scale thickness is $x$, we approximate the gradient as $\Delta C / x$. Hence:

$$J \approx D \frac{\Delta C}{x}$$

### 3.1.3 Relate Flux to Mass Gain

The mass gain per unit area, $\Delta m / A$, is proportional to the amount of oxide formed, which in turn is proportional to the amount of cations that have diffused out. Therefore:

$$\frac{d(\Delta m/A)}{dt} = \text{const} \cdot J = \text{const} \cdot \frac{D \, \Delta C}{x}$$

### 3.1.4 Recognise that $x \propto \Delta m / A$

Because the oxide thickness $x$ is directly proportional to the cumulative mass of oxide (i.e., $x = k_1 \cdot (\Delta m/A)$), we substitute:

$$\frac{d(\Delta m/A)}{dt} = \frac{k_2}{(\Delta m/A)}$$

where $k_2 = k_1 \, D \, \Delta C / \text{const}$ is a constant at fixed temperature.

### 3.1.5 Integrate with Respect to Time

Let $y = \Delta m / A$. Then:

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{k_2}{y}$$

Rearranging:

$$y \, dy = k_2 \, dt$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$ and $0$ to $y$:

$$\int_0^y y \, dy = \int_0^t k_2 \, dt$$

$$\frac{y^2}{2} = k_2 \, t$$

$$y^2 = 2 k_2 \, t$$

### 3.1.6 Final Parabolic Equation

Defining $k_p = 2 k_2$ as the **parabolic rate constant**, we obtain:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\Delta m}{A}\right)^2 = k_p \, t}$$

This is the celebrated **Wagner parabolic oxidation law**, and it is valid for stainless steels in the 600–900 °C range where Cr₂O₃ is the rate-controlling scale.

## 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of $k_p$ (Arrhenius Form)

The diffusion coefficient $D$ in Fick's law follows the Arrhenius equation:

$$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Since $k_p \propto D$, the parabolic rate constant also follows:

$$\boxed{k_p = A_p \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_p}{RT}\right)}$$

where:
- $A_p$ = pre-exponential constant (mg²/cm⁴·s)
- $Q_p$ = apparent activation energy (typically **170 – 220 kJ/mol** for Cr₂O₃ growth on austenitic SS)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A 304 stainless-steel specimen is oxidised in air at 800 °C for 100 hours. The mass gain is found to be 1.2 mg/cm². Assuming parabolic kinetics, calculate the parabolic rate constant $k_p$. Predict the mass gain after 250 hours at the same temperature.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Use the parabolic law:

$$\left(\frac{\Delta m}{A}\right)^2 = k_p \, t$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $k_p$ at $t = 100$ h:

$$k_p = \frac{(\Delta m / A)^2}{t} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{100} = \frac{1.44}{100}$$

$$\boxed{k_p = 1.44 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{mg}^2/\text{cm}^4\cdot\text{h}}$$

**Step 3:** Predict mass gain at $t = 250$ h:

$$\frac{\Delta m}{A} = \sqrt{k_p \cdot t} = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{-2} \times 250}$$

$$= \sqrt{3.6} = 1.897 \;\text{mg/cm}^2$$

$$\boxed{(\Delta m / A)_{250h} \approx 1.90 \;\text{mg/cm}^2}$$

**Interpretation:** Although exposure time increased 2.5×, the mass gain rose only ~ 1.58× — confirming the **self-limiting, diffusion-controlled protective behaviour** of the Cr₂O₃ film.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Stainless Steels and Their Mechanical / Oxidation Signatures

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STAINLESS STEELS]:::root --> B[Austenitic FCC]
    A --> C[Ferritic BCC]
    A --> D[Martensitic BCT]
    A --> E[Duplex FCCplusBCC]
    A --> F[Precipitation Hardening]

    B --> B1[Grades 304 316 321 347]
    B --> B2[High ductility 40 to 60 percent EL]
    B --> B3[Work hardens strongly n equals 0.30 to 0.55]
    B --> B4[Best general oxidation resistance]

    C --> C1[Grades 409 430 446]
    C --> C2[Moderate strength magnetic]
    C --> C3[Good oxidation up to 900 C]
    C --> C4[Susceptible to pitting in Cl minus]

    D --> D1[Grades 410 420 440C]
    D --> D2[Hardenable up to 60 HRC]
    D --> D3[Highest hardness among SS]
    D --> D4[Lower Cr implies moderate oxidation resistance]

    E --> E1[Grades 2205 2507]
    E --> E2[Yield strength 2 times austenitic]
    E --> E3[PREN 25 to 40]
    E --> E4[Excellent pitting and SCC resistance]

    F --> F1[Grades 17-4 PH 15-5 PH]
    F --> F2[UTS up to 1310 MPa after aging]
    F --> F3[Combines high strength with SS corrosion]

    classDef root fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px;
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Mechanism of Oxidation and Passive Film Formation on Stainless Steel

```mermaid
graph LR
    O2[Oxygen from air]:::gas -->|adsorbs| SURF[Steel surface Fe Cr Ni Mo]
    SURF -->|nucleation| NUCL[Initial oxide nuclei]
    NUCL -->|lateral growth| LAYER[Thin Cr2O3 passive film 1 to 3 nm]
    LAYER -->|blocks diffusion| PROTECT[Protection against further oxidation]
    LAYER -->|if scratched| REPAIR[Re-passivation by Cr diffusion]
    REPAIR --> LAYER

    HIGH[High T 600 to 900 C]:::heat -->|favours| SCALE[Thicker Cr2O3 scale plus spinels]
    SCALE -->|follows| WAGNER[Parabolic law dm by A squared equals kp t]
    WAGNER -->|kp equals| ARRH[Arrhenius kp equals Ap exp minus Qp by RT]

    classDef gas fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366;
    classDef heat fill:#ffd2a8,stroke:#cc4400;
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture – Factors Governing Oxidation Resistance

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[OXIDATION RESISTANCE OF STAINLESS STEEL]:::title

    ROOT --> COMP[Composition]
    ROOT --> TEMP[Service Temperature]
    ROOT --> ENV[Environment]
    ROOT --> TIME[Exposure Time]

    COMP --> COMP1[Cr content above 10.5 percent]
    COMP --> COMP2[Mo for pitting]
    COMP --> COMP3[Ni for austenite and spinels]
    COMP --> COMP4[Si and Al for high T sub layer]
    COMP --> COMP5[RE elements Ce La improve scale adherence]

    TEMP --> TEMP1[Below 500 C passive film]
    TEMP --> TEMP2[500 to 900 C parabolic Cr2O3]
    TEMP --> TEMP3[Above 900 C breakaway oxidation possible]

    ENV --> ENV1[Air / O2 normal]
    ENV --> ENV2[Water vapour accelerates]
    ENV --> ENV3[SO2 forms low melting sulphides]
    ENV --> ENV4[Chlorides cause pitting]

    TIME --> TIME1[Short thin protective film]
    TIME --> TIME2[Long spallation possible]
    TIME --> TIME3[Thermal cycling induces scale cracking]

    classDef title fill:#1a1a1a,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px;
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define a stainless steel. What is the minimum chromium content required for an Fe-Cr alloy to be classified as stainless steel, and why?**

**Model Answer:**
A stainless steel is defined as an iron-based alloy containing a minimum of **10.5% chromium** by mass, with the chromium content predominantly in solid solution. This Cr content enables the formation of a **self-healing, passive Cr₂O₃ film** on the surface in oxidising environments, which imparts the characteristic corrosion and oxidation resistance. Below 10.5% Cr, the alloy cannot sustain a continuous passive layer and is merely classified as a heat-resisting or low-alloy steel.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**List the five major families of stainless steels and state one distinguishing mechanical property of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Family | Distinguishing Mechanical Property |
|---|---|
| Austenitic | Excellent ductility (40–60% EL), non-magnetic, high work-hardening rate |
| Ferritic | Magnetic, moderate strength, limited ductility in thick sections |
| Martensitic | High hardness (up to 60 HRC) achievable by heat treatment |
| Duplex | Yield strength approximately twice that of austenitic grades |
| Precipitation-Hardening | Very high strength (UTS up to ~ 1310 MPa) after aging |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** – *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the role of chromium in imparting corrosion and oxidation resistance to stainless steels. Discuss the formation, structure, and self-healing nature of the passive Cr₂O₃ film. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, describe the parabolic rate law for high-temperature oxidation of stainless steels. Derive the expression relating the parabolic rate constant to temperature using the Arrhenius equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**1. Role of Chromium:**
Chromium is the **primary stainlessing element**. When present in solid solution in iron at concentrations ≥ **10.5 wt%**, it reacts preferentially with atmospheric oxygen to form a continuous, adherent, and self-healing oxide film of **Cr₂O₃ (eskolaite)** on the metal surface.

**2. Formation of the Passive Film:**
- In the initial stages, oxygen adsorbs on the steel surface and reacts with Cr to form **discrete oxide nuclei**.
- These nuclei grow laterally and vertically until they coalesce into a **continuous, amorphous-to-crystalline film** of thickness **1–3 nm** at room temperature.
- The film consists essentially of Cr₂O₃ with small amounts of Fe and Ni in solid solution.

**3. Why Cr₂O₃ is Protective:**
- **Pilling-Bedworth ratio (PBR)** of Cr₂O₃/Cr is approximately **2.0**, which lies in the protective range (1 < PBR < 2). The film is therefore under **compressive stress**, well-adhered, and crack-free.
- Cr₂O₃ is a **p-type semiconductor** oxide. According to the **Wagner–Hauffe rule**, lower-valency Cr³⁺ ions reduce cation-vacancy concentration, thereby **slowing outward cation diffusion** and reducing the oxidation rate.

**4. Self-Healing Nature:**
- If the film is mechanically damaged or chemically dissolved, exposed Cr atoms in the matrix **diffuse to the surface** and re-oxidise to reform the Cr₂O₃ layer.
- This is only possible because the bulk steel contains a **reservoir of Cr in solid solution**, continuously supplying the surface.

**5. Effect of Increasing Cr:**
Higher Cr content → thicker, more stable Cr₂O₃ → better oxidation resistance up to ~ 900 °C. At very high Cr (≥ 25%), the alloy can form a **duplex oxide** with an inner (Cr,Fe)₂O₃ and an outer (Fe,Cr)₂O₃ spinel.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**1. Statement of Parabolic Law:**
For stainless steels oxidised in the 600–900 °C range, the oxidation kinetics obey:

$$\left(\frac{\Delta m}{A}\right)^2 = k_p \, t$$

where $\Delta m / A$ is mass gain per unit area, $t$ is time, and $k_p$ is the parabolic rate constant.

**2. Justification – Diffusion Control:**
The Cr₂O₃ film acts as a diffusion barrier. The thicker it grows, the longer the diffusion path, so the rate of further oxidation decreases. This self-limiting behaviour mathematically yields a parabolic time dependence.

**3. Arrhenius Dependence of $k_p$:**
Since oxidation is governed by the diffusion of ions through the oxide scale, and diffusion coefficients follow the Arrhenius equation, $k_p$ also follows:

$$k_p = A_p \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_p}{RT}\right)$$

**4. Typical Values:**
- $A_p$ ≈ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻² mg²/cm⁴·s
- $Q_p$ ≈ **170–220 kJ/mol** for Cr₂O₃ growth on austenitic stainless steels
- $R$ = **8.314 J/mol·K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

**5. Practical Utility:**
Plotting $\ln(k_p)$ versus $1/T$ gives a straight line whose slope yields $Q_p$. This allows extrapolation of laboratory short-term oxidation data to predict **long-term service life** of stainless components in boilers, heat exchangers, and gas-turbine parts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** – *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare the mechanical behaviour of austenitic, martensitic, and duplex stainless steels. Discuss their strength, ductility, hardness, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN). Calculate the PREN of the following stainless steels and comment on their suitability for marine applications:
- Steel A: 18% Cr, 10% Ni, 2% Mo, 0.02% N
- Steel B: 25% Cr, 5% Ni, 4% Mo, 0.20% N **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

| Property | Austenitic (304, 316) | Martensitic (410, 420) | Duplex (2205, 2507) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Crystal structure** | FCC | BCT (after quench) | FCC + BCC (50:50) |
| **Yield strength** | 200–300 MPa | 450–1700 MPa (after temper) | 400–550 MPa |
| **UTS** | 500–700 MPa | 700–2000 MPa | 700–900 MPa |
| **% Elongation** | 40–60% | 10–25% | 25–35% |
| **Hardness** | 150–200 HB | 200–600 HB | 220–280 HB |
| **Toughness** | Excellent (esp. cryogenic) | Moderate–Low | Good |
| **Magnetic** | No | Yes | Yes (mild) |
| **Typical apps** | Chemical tanks, cryogenics, kitchenware | Cutlery, surgical tools, turbine blades | Marine, offshore, desalination, chemical plants |

**Discussion:**
- **Austenitic SS** owe their high ductility and toughness to the FCC structure (12 slip systems). They cannot be hardened by heat treatment, only by **cold work**.
- **Martensitic SS** are heat-treatable; quenching produces a hard, brittle BCT phase which is then **tempered** for toughness.
- **Duplex SS** combine the **strength of ferritic and the corrosion resistance of austenitic** structures, making them highly cost-effective for chloride-rich environments.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Definition of PREN:**

The Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number is an empirical ranking that predicts the resistance of a stainless steel to pitting corrosion in chloride environments:

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

**For Steel A (18% Cr, 10% Ni, 2% Mo, 0.02% N):**

$$\text{PREN}_A = 18 + 3.3 \times 2 + 16 \times 0.02$$

$$= 18 + 6.6 + 0.32 = 24.92$$

$$\boxed{\text{PREN}_A \approx 24.9}$$

**For Steel B (25% Cr, 5% Ni, 4% Mo, 0.20% N):**

$$\text{PREN}_B = 25 + 3.3 \times 4 + 16 \times 0.20$$

$$= 25 + 13.2 + 3.20 = 41.4$$

$$\boxed{\text{PREN}_B \approx 41.4}$$

**Comments on Marine Suitability:**

- **Steel A (PREN ≈ 24.9):** Falls in the *moderate* pitting resistance range (25–32). It is similar to standard **Type 316** and is **marginally suitable** for marine splash zones, but **not recommended** for submerged or highly chloride-rich service.
- **Steel B (PREN ≈ 41.4):** Far exceeds the **32 threshold** for excellent pitting resistance. It is a **super-duplex** grade (similar to **2507 / SAF 2507**) and is **highly suitable** for marine, sub-sea, and desalination plant applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 16 for nitrogen** — many students use 10 or 20, which is incorrect; the canonical KTU-accepted factor is **16**.
> 2. **Ignoring %Ni in the formula** — Nickel does **not** appear in the PREN equation. It stabilises austenite but does not directly improve pitting resistance.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — Always quote the PREN as a **dimensionless number** and clearly state whether the steel is suitable for marine use (PREN > 32).
> 4. **Confusing PREN with oxidation resistance** — PREN is for **wet/pitting corrosion**, NOT for high-temperature oxidation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Mechanical Behaviour & Oxidation Resistance of Stainless Steels**

- ✅ **Definition:** Stainless steel = Fe + ≥ **10.5% Cr**; Cr forms self-healing **Cr₂O₃** passive film.
- ✅ **Five families:** Austenitic (FCC), Ferritic (BCC), Martensitic (BCT), Duplex (FCC+BCC), Precipitation-Hardening.
- ✅ **Austenitic SS:** Best ductility (40–60% EL), non-magnetic, work-hardening exponent **n ≈ 0.3–0.55**, cannot be hardened by heat treatment.
- ✅ **Martensitic SS:** Hardenable up to **60 HRC** (e.g. 440C); highest hardness among SS.
- ✅ **Duplex SS:** Yield strength ~ **2× austenitic**; PREN 25–40.
- ✅ **PH SS:** UTS up to **1310 MPa** (17-4 PH); Cu / Ni₃Ti / Ni₃Al precipitates.
- ✅ **Hollomon Equation:** $\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T^n$ — work-hardening law.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — grain-boundary strengthening.
- ✅ **Passive film:** 1–3 nm Cr₂O₃, PBR ≈ 2.0, self-repairing up to ~ 900 °C.
- ✅ **Wagner–Hauffe rule:** Cr³⁺ (lower valence than Fe³⁺) in p-type Cr₂O₃ reduces oxidation rate.
- ✅ **Parabolic rate law:** $(\Delta m/A)^2 = k_p t$ — valid for 600–900 °C on SS.
- ✅ **Arrhenius form:** $k_p = A_p \exp(-Q_p / RT)$; $Q_p$ ≈ **170–220 kJ/mol** for austenitic SS.
- ✅ **PREN equation:** $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\,\%\text{Mo} + 16\,\%\text{N}$.
- ✅ **PREN ranking:** < 25 poor, 25–32 moderate, > 32 excellent (super-austenitic/duplex).
- ✅ **Alloying effects:** Cr ↑↑ oxidation, Mo ↑↑ pitting, Ni ↑ austenite, Si/Al ↑↑ high-T scale, Ti/Nb ↓↓ sensitisation.
- ✅ **Sensitisation:** Cr depletion at grain boundaries due to Cr-carbide (Cr₂₃C₆) precipitation in 400–850 °C range; mitigated by **stabilisation (Ti, Nb)** or by **low-carbon "L" grades (304L, 316L)**.
- ✅ **Creep regime:** Above ~ 0.4 $T_m$, austenitic SS preferred due to FCC slip systems.
- ✅ **Endurance limit:** $\sigma_e \approx 0.4$ to $0.5 \times \sigma_{UTS}$ for austenitic SS in air.
- ✅ **Applications:** Chemical plants, marine, cryogenics, medical implants, nuclear, aerospace, food processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ni- and Co-based superalloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Superalloys — The "Working Horses" of High Temperature

A **superalloy** is a metallic alloy designed for service at extreme temperatures (typically above **540 °C / 1000 °F**), where it must retain exceptional mechanical strength, resist thermal creep deformation, withstand surface oxidation and hot-corrosion, and maintain microstructural stability for thousands of operating hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPET751 – Module 1):**
> *Superalloys are heat-resistant alloys based on nickel, cobalt, or iron-nickel matrices that derive their high-temperature strength from solid-solution strengthening, precipitation hardening (γ′, γ″, carbides), and grain-boundary / oxide-dispersion strengthening mechanisms.*

The three classical families are:
- **Ni-based superalloys** (the dominant, most widely used family)
- **Co-based superalloys** (superior hot-corrosion and wear resistance)
- **Fe-Ni-based superalloys** (lower cost, moderate temperature use)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Reinforced Concrete of the Jet Age"

Imagine a building constructed of reinforced concrete. The cement matrix (γ phase) carries compressive load, while the steel re-bars (γ′ precipitates) carry the tensile load. Heat the building to 1000 °C — ordinary concrete crumbles, ordinary steel re-bars soften. A superalloy, however, is like **heat-resistant reinforced concrete**: the γ matrix is a ductile, corrosion-resistant "thermal cement," and the γ′ precipitates are nano-sized, ordered, thermally stable "re-bars" that don't soften, dissolve, or coarsen even at temperatures approaching their melting point.

## 1.3 Why Nickel and Cobalt? — The "Why" Behind the Matrix Choice

| Property | Nickel (Ni) | Cobalt (Co) |
|---|---|---|
| Crystal structure (RT) | **FCC (γ)** stable up to Tm | **HCP (ε)** at RT, transforms to **FCC (γ)** at ~417 °C |
| Melting point (Tm) | **1455 °C** | **1495 °C** |
| Why chosen as matrix? | Forms coherent, ordered **γ′ (Ni₃Al,Ti)** — exceptional precipitate strengthener | Forms stable, hard **M₂₃C₆ / M₆C carbides** — exceptional wear + corrosion resistance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants for KTU numerical problems:**
> - Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$
> - Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$
> - Burgers vector for FCC Ni (γ): $\vert \vec{b} \vert = a\sqrt{2}/2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nickel-Based Superalloys — The Phase Architecture

A modern Ni-based superalloy is a *microstructural orchestra* with five major constituents working in concert:

### 2.1.1 The γ Matrix (Gamma — Disordered FCC Solid Solution)
- Continuous matrix of Ni with dissolved **Cr, Co, Mo, W**.
- Provides ductility, toughness, oxidation resistance (via Cr₂O₃ formation).
- Strengthened by **solid-solution strengthening**: solute atoms create local elastic strain fields that impede dislocation motion.

### 2.1.2 The γ′ Precipitate (Gamma-Prime — Ordered L1₂, Ni₃(Al,Ti))
- Composition: $\text{Ni}_3(\text{Al, Ti, Nb, Ta})$
- Structure: **Ordered FCC, L1₂ (Cu₃Au prototype)**
- Volume fraction: typically **60–70 %** in modern single-crystal turbine blades
- The **miracle feature**: γ′ is *coherent* with the γ matrix and its **yield strength INCREASES with temperature** up to ~800 °C (the "yield anomaly" — caused by cross-slip of dislocations from {111} to {100} planes where they form Kear–Wilsdorf locks).

### 2.1.3 The γ″ Precipitate (Gamma-Double-Prime — Ordered DO₂₂, Ni₃Nb)
- Composition: $\text{Ni}_3\text{Nb}$
- Structure: **Ordered BCT, DO₂₂**
- Principal strengthener in **Inconel 718** (the workhorse alloy — ~50 % of all superalloy production by mass).
- Limitation: metastable — transforms to δ-phase (orthorhombic Ni₃Nb) above ~650 °C, restricting use below 650 °C.

### 2.1.4 Carbides (MC, M₂₃C₆, M₆C)
- **MC** (M = Ti, Nb, Hf, Zr): primary carbides, form during solidification.
- **M₂₃C₆** (M = Cr): grain-boundary carbides — control grain-boundary sliding, prevent creep rupture.
- **M₆C** (M = Mo, W): replaces M₂₃C₆ in high Mo/W alloys.

### 2.1.5 Topologically Close-Packed (TCP) Phases (σ, μ, Laves)
- Brittle, plate-like intermetallics that consume solid-solution strengtheners.
- Their formation is **undesirable** — must be suppressed by compositional control (e.g., **PHACOMP** — Phase Computation method using electron vacancy number $\overline{N_v}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:**
> *"Why is γ′ coherent with γ?"*
> Because both have FCC-type lattices with a very small **lattice misfit** $\delta$, defined in the formula sheet below. Low misfit → coherent precipitation → low interfacial energy → resistance to coarsening.

## 2.2 Cobalt-Based Superalloys — The "Carbide-Strong" Family

### 2.2.1 Historical Limitation
For decades, Co-based superalloys were the *poor cousins* of Ni-based alloys because Co lacks a stable γ′ precipitate analogous to Ni₃Al. Their strength came almost entirely from:
- Solid-solution strengthening (W, Mo)
- **Carbide strengthening** (M₂₃C₆, M₆C in a Cr–W–C system)
- Oxide-dispersion strengthening (ODS) in newer grades

### 2.2.2 The 2006 Sato Breakthrough — Co–Al–W γ′ Alloys
In **2006**, J. Sato (Tohoku University) discovered a **Co₃(Al,W)** γ′ precipitate with the **L1₂ structure** in the Co–Al–W ternary system. This reopened Co as a viable γ′-strengthened matrix and triggered a new generation of Co-based superalloys.

### 2.2.3 Strengthening Mechanisms Summary
- **Solid-solution strengthening** by W, Mo, Cr, Ta
- **Carbide precipitation strengthening** (M₂₃C₆, M₆C)
- **γ′ precipitation strengthening** in new Co–Al–W alloys
- **Grain-boundary strengthening** by carbides
- **Oxide-dispersion strengthening** (ODS) — e.g., yttria (Y₂O₃) in MA 754

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \dfrac{2(a_{\gamma'} - a_{\gamma})}{a_{\gamma'} + a_{\gamma}}$ | Lattice misfit between γ′ and γ; must be small (typically 0–0.5 %) for coherence | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta \sigma_{\text{ss}} = k \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ | Labusch–Nabarro solid-solution strengthening (Fleischer model) | MPa |
| 3 | $\tau_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{APB}} \cdot f^{1/2}}{b}$ | Shear stress to cut ordered γ′ (weak-pair coupling) | MPa |
| 4 | $\tau_{\text{APB}} = \dfrac{G \cdot b^2}{2\pi a} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{APB}}}{\gamma_{\text{isf}}}\right)$ | APB energy (anti-phase boundary) relation to stacking-fault energy | J/m² |
| 5 | $\overline{N_v} = \sum_{i} N_{v,i} \cdot x_i$ | Electron-vacancy number (PHACOMP) — predicts TCP phase formation | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot D_0 \cdot \dfrac{G \cdot b}{kT} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{G}\right)^n \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | Steady-state creep (Norton–Bailey) | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $t_{r} = \dfrac{C}{\dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{min}}}$ | Creep rupture life (Monkman–Grant) | hours |
| 8 | $d^3 - d_0^3 = K_0 \cdot t \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_g}{RT}\right)$ | Coarsening kinetics of γ′ (Lifshitz–Slyozov–Wagner / Ostwald ripening) | nm³ |
| 9 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch grain-boundary strengthening | MPa |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{4(1-f)r}{3f}$ | Inter-particle spacing in γ′ dispersion (approximate) | nm |

**Where:** $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $a$ = lattice parameter, $c$ = solute concentration, $\varepsilon$ = misfit strain, $f$ = γ′ volume fraction, $r$ = γ′ radius, $\gamma_{\text{APB}}$ = anti-phase boundary energy, $Q_c$ = creep activation energy, $Q_g$ = coarsening activation energy.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ni-based superalloys**: jet engine turbine blades & discs (CMSX-4, PWA 1484), rocket engine injectors, nuclear reactor cores, marine gas turbines.
- **Co-based superalloys**: combustor liners, vanes, industrial gas turbines, dental/medical implants (Stellite 6 for wear), high-temperature springs and bolts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lattice Misfit $\delta$ Between γ and γ′

**Starting concept:** Two coherent cubic phases in a Ni-superalloy share a common {100} interface. The γ phase (Ni-rich FCC) has lattice parameter $a_\gamma$, and the γ′ phase (Ni₃(Al,Ti) ordered L1₂) has lattice parameter $a_{\gamma'}$. Because they must share a common atomic plane at the interface, one lattice is elastically strained to match the other.

**Step 1: Write the unconstrained lattice parameters.**

$$a_\gamma = a_{\gamma,0}, \qquad a_{\gamma'} = a_{\gamma',0}$$

Here $a_{\gamma,0}$ and $a_{\gamma',0}$ are the *natural*, stress-free lattice parameters of each phase at the operating temperature.

**Step 2: Express the absolute lattice difference.**

$$\Delta a = a_{\gamma'} - a_\gamma$$

This is the raw difference in the unstressed state.

**Step 3: Normalise by the average lattice parameter.**

To obtain a dimensionless parameter that does not depend on the arbitrary choice of reference, divide by the arithmetic mean:

$$\delta = \dfrac{\Delta a}{\tfrac{1}{2}(a_{\gamma'} + a_\gamma)} = \dfrac{2(a_{\gamma'} - a_\gamma)}{a_{\gamma'} + a_\gamma}$$

**Step 4: KTU-style numerical evaluation.**

Given a hypothetical Ni-superalloy with $a_{\gamma'} = 0.3589 \ \text{nm}$ and $a_\gamma = 0.3584 \ \text{nm}$, compute $\delta$:

$$\delta = \dfrac{2(0.3589 - 0.3584)}{0.3589 + 0.3584} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.0005}{0.7173}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.0010}{0.7173} = 1.394 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1394 \ \%$$

**Step 5: Engineering interpretation.**

$$0 \le \delta \le 0.5 \ \% \implies \text{coherent or semi-coherent (desirable, low coarsening)}$$

$$\delta > 1 \ \% \implies \text{incoherent, rapid coarsening, loss of strength}$$

> **KTU interpretation tip:** The closer $\delta$ is to **zero**, the more thermodynamically stable the γ/γ′ interface and the slower the Ostwald ripening of γ′.

## 3.2 Derivation of Anti-Phase Boundary (APB) Energy Relation

When a matrix dislocation (with Burgers vector $b = a/2\langle 110 \rangle$) cuts through an ordered γ′ precipitate, it creates an **anti-phase boundary (APB)** across the cut because the order is locally destroyed. The energy per unit area of this boundary is $\gamma_{\text{APB}}$.

**Step 1: Energy of a unit dislocation in the γ′ lattice.**

$$E_{\text{disl}} = \dfrac{G b^2}{2} \quad \text{per unit length (J/m)}$$

**Step 2: Energy per unit area of APB.**

The APB is the energy penalty for breaking the L1₂ order across a plane. For a low-APB-energy system (e.g., pure Ni₃Al), it can be approximated as a fraction of the stacking-fault energy $\gamma_{\text{isf}}$:

$$\gamma_{\text{APB}} = \dfrac{G b^2}{2\pi a} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{APB}}}{\gamma_{\text{isf}}}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Pair-coupling shear stress to cut γ′.**

A dislocation pair must traverse the precipitate. The leading dislocation creates the APB, and the trailing dislocation removes it. The required shear stress is:

$$\tau_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{APB}} \cdot f^{1/2}}{b}$$

where $f$ is the volume fraction of γ′. A **higher** $\gamma_{\text{APB}}$ means **stronger** γ′ (more energy required to shear it).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU high-yield result:** Modern Ni-superalloys (e.g., CMSX-4) are designed to have $\gamma_{\text{APB}} \sim 200$–$300 \ \text{mJ/m}^2$ to maximise $\tau_{\text{cut}}$ and thus high-temperature yield strength.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ni vs. Co-Based Superalloys

| Feature | Ni-Based Superalloys | Co-Based Superalloys |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary matrix phase** | γ (FCC Ni-solid solution) | γ (FCC Co-solid solution, stable above 417 °C) |
| **Main strengthener** | γ′ (Ni₃(Al,Ti)) — coherent, ordered L1₂ | M₂₃C₆ / M₆C carbides; γ′ in new Co–Al–W alloys |
| **Volume fraction of strengthener** | 50–70 % γ′ (in single crystals) | 5–15 % carbides (classical); up to 70 % γ′ (new Co–Al–W) |
| **Yield anomaly (strength ↑ with T)** | **YES** (up to ~800 °C) | NO in classical; YES in new γ′-containing Co alloys |
| **Maximum use temperature** | ~1100 °C (with TBCs: ~1250 °C) | ~1100 °C |
| **Hot-corrosion resistance** | Good | **Excellent** (Cr-rich) |
| **Wear / abrasion resistance** | Moderate | **Excellent** (Stellite 6) |
| **Weldability** | Moderate (alloy 718 is weldable) | Good |
| **Cost** | High (Ni + Co + Re) | Comparable or higher (Co volatile) |
| **Typical applications** | Turbine blades, discs, combustors | Vanes, combustors, wear surfaces, springs |
| **Key examples** | Inconel 718, Waspaloy, CMSX-4, Rene N5 | Stellite 6, Haynes 188, MAR-M509, Co–Al–W (experimental) |
| **Limitation** | Incipient melting, TCP formation | Lack of γ′ (classical); limited high-T strength |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phase Architecture of a Modern Ni-Based Single-Crystal Superalloy

The diagram below maps the multi-phase microstructure of a typical Ni-based turbine-blade superalloy (e.g., CMSX-4 / PWA 1484).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ni-based Superalloy Microstructure] --> B[gamma Matrix: FCC Ni solid solution]
    A --> C[gamma-prime Precipitate: Ni3 Al Ti - L1 ordered]
    A --> D[gamma-double-prime Precipitate: Ni3 Nb - DO22 - in 718 only]
    A --> E[Carbides: MC, M23C6, M6C]
    A --> F[Borides: M3B2 at grain boundaries]
    A --> G[TCP phases: sigma, mu, Laves - undesirable]

    B --> B1[Strengthens via solid-solution: Cr Co Mo W]
    C --> C1[Strengthens via coherency strain and APB cutting]
    C --> C2[Provides yield anomaly up to 800 C]
    D --> D1[Principal strengthener in alloy 718 below 650 C]
    E --> E1[MC primary: Ti Nb Hf]
    E --> E2[M23C6 grain boundary: Cr-rich]
    E --> E3[M6C high Mo W: replaces M23C6]
    F --> F1[Improve creep ductility]
    G --> G1[Deplete solid solution strengtheners]
    G --> G2[Suppressed by PHACOMP and Al Ti control]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Strengthening Mechanisms Hierarchy

```mermaid

    S2 --> S22[gamma-double-prime: DO22 in alloy 718]
    S2 --> S23[Cutting vs looping transition]

    S3 --> S32[Carbide decoration of GB]
    S3 --> S33[Directional and single-crystal solidification]

    S4 --> S42[MA 754 MA 956 grades]
    S4 --> S43[Stable at very high T]

    S5 --> S52[M3B2: creep ductility]
```
