# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define a superalloy and list the three major classes with one example each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A superalloy is a metallic alloy specifically designed to retain high mechanical strength, resist creep, oxidation, and hot-corrosion at temperatures typically above **540 °C**. The three major classes are:
1. **Iron-based superalloys** — e.g., A-286
2. **Nickel-based superalloys** — e.g., Inconel 718
3. **Cobalt-based superalloys** — e.g., Stellite 6

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. What is the γ′ phase in Ni-based superalloys? Why is it significant?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
γ′ is an ordered intermetallic precipitate of composition **Ni₃(Al, Ti)** with the **L1₂ crystal structure**. It is significant because:
- It is **coherent** with the FCC γ matrix, producing a strong, stable interface.
- It provides **precipitation strengthening** by APB cutting and coherency strain.
- It causes the **yield anomaly** — strength increases with temperature up to ~800 °C, making it the principal high-temperature strengthener in modern Ni-superalloys.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question]*
> **(a)** With neat schematics, explain the various strengthening mechanisms operative in Ni-based superalloys. Discuss the role of γ′ and γ″ precipates. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A Ni-based superalloy has γ lattice parameter $a_\gamma = 0.3590 \ \text{nm}$ and γ′ lattice parameter $a_{\gamma'} = 0.3598 \ \text{nm}$. Compute the lattice misfit δ and comment on the expected microstructural stability. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Strengthening mechanisms in Ni-based superalloys (7 marks):**

1. **Solid-solution strengthening (1.5 marks):** Heavy elements such as **W, Mo, Cr, Co** are dissolved in the Ni γ matrix. They produce local elastic distortions (size and modulus mismatch) that impede dislocation motion. Quantified by $\Delta \sigma_{ss} = k G \varepsilon^{3/2} c^{1/2}$.

2. **Precipitation strengthening by γ′ (2 marks):** The ordered L1₂ Ni₃(Al,Ti) precipitates are coherent with γ. Dislocations cut them in pairs — the leading dislocation creates an anti-phase boundary (APB) and the trailing removes it, requiring shear stress $\tau_{cut} = \gamma_{APB} f^{1/2} / b$. The **yield anomaly** (strength up to ~800 °C) arises from cross-slip of screw dislocations from {111} to {100} planes, forming Kear–Wilsdorf locks.

3. **Precipitation strengthening by γ″ (1 mark):** In alloys such as **Inconel 718**, the principal strengthener is the **DO₂₂ Ni₃Nb (γ″)** phase. It produces a large coherency strain (~2.6 %) and high strengthening efficiency. However, γ″ is metastable above ~650 °C and transforms to the δ-phase (orthorhombic Ni₃Nb), limiting service temperature.

4. **Carbide strengthening (1 mark):** **MC** (Ti, Nb, Hf) primary carbides form during solidification. **M₂₃C₆** (Cr-rich) precipitates at grain boundaries and prevents grain-boundary sliding during creep. **M₆C** forms in alloys with high Mo/W.

5. **Grain-boundary strengthening (1 mark):** Carbide decoration and boride formation strengthen grain boundaries. Modern single-crystal (SC) and directionally solidified (DS) alloys eliminate transverse grain boundaries to maximise creep life.

6. **Oxide-dispersion strengthening (ODS) (0.5 marks):** Fine Y₂O₃ dispersoids (e.g., MA 754, MA 956) are stable above 1200 °C.

**(b) Lattice misfit calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the misfit formula.

$$\delta = \dfrac{2(a_{\gamma'} - a_\gamma)}{a_{\gamma'} + a_\gamma}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values.

$$\delta = \dfrac{2(0.3598 - 0.3590)}{0.3598 + 0.3590} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.0008}{0.7188}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the numerator and denominator.

$$\delta = \dfrac{0.0016}{0.7188} = 2.226 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2226 \ \%$$

**Step 4:** Comment on stability.
- The misfit $\delta \approx 0.22 \ \%$ lies in the **coherent-to-semi-coherent range** (0–0.5 %).
- This is **acceptable** for good high-temperature stability.
- However, the value is on the **higher end** of the desirable range — moderate coarsening of γ′ by Ostwald ripening can be expected.
- For comparison, **CMSX-4** has $\delta \approx 0.0$ to $-0.1$ % (negative misfit — γ′ contracts around γ), giving superior thermal stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A-b):**
> Students often forget to multiply by **2** in the numerator OR forget to convert the final answer to a percentage. KTU examiners award 1 mark each for: (i) correct formula, (ii) correct substitution, (iii) correct numerical evaluation, (iv) correct unit (% or dimensionless), (v) physical interpretation. **Do not skip the final interpretive comment — it carries 2 of the 7 marks.**

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question]*
> **(a)** Compare the structure, properties, and applications of Co-based superalloys with Ni-based superalloys. Highlight the role of the Co–Al–W γ′ system. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A Ni-based superalloy contains 65 vol. % of cuboidal γ′ precipitates of average edge length 0.45 μm. Estimate the inter-particle spacing using $\lambda = 4(1-f)r / 3f$. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison and Co–Al–W system (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Ni-Based | Co-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Matrix | γ (FCC Ni solid solution) | γ (FCC Co, stable > 417 °C) |
| Principal strengthener | γ′ (Ni₃Al,Ti) | M₂₃C₆ / M₆C carbides (classical); γ′ in Co–Al–W |
| Yield anomaly | Yes, up to ~800 °C | No (classical); Yes in Co–Al–W γ′ |
| Max use T | ~1100 °C (with TBC ~1250 °C) | ~1100 °C |
| Hot-corrosion resistance | Good | **Excellent** |
| Wear resistance | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Cost | High | Comparable to high-Ni grades |
| Applications | Turbine blades, discs | Vanes, combustors, wear parts |

**The 2006 Sato Co–Al–W breakthrough (2 marks):**
- J. Sato (Tohoku University, 2006) discovered the **γ′ phase Co₃(Al,W)** with the **L1₂ structure** in the **Co–Al–W** ternary system.
- Composition: Co–9–10 at.% Al, 7–10 at.% W.
- This γ′ is **coherent with the FCC Co matrix** and gives Co-based superalloys a true precipitation-strengthening mechanism analogous to Ni-base alloys.
- Subsequent research extended to **Co–Ti–Cr, Co–Al–Mo, Co–Ge–W** γ′ systems.
- Advantages over classical Co alloys: **higher temperature capability**, higher volume fraction of γ′ (up to ~70 %).
- Limitations: γ′ solvus is still **lower** than in Ni-superalloys; ternary γ′ field is narrow; long-term stability is still under research.

**(b) Inter-particle spacing estimation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the input parameters.
- Volume fraction $f = 65 \% = 0.65$
- Edge length of cuboidal γ′ = $0.45 \ \mu \text{m} = 450 \ \text{nm}$
- For a cuboidal particle, the **equivalent spherical radius** is $r = a/2 = 225 \ \text{nm}$.

**Step 2:** Write the spacing formula.

$$\lambda = \dfrac{4(1-f)\,r}{3f}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$\lambda = \dfrac{4 \times (1 - 0.65) \times 225 \ \text{nm}}{3 \times 0.65}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the numerator.

$$4 \times 0.35 \times 225 = 0.35 \times 900 = 315 \ \text{nm}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator.

$$3 \times 0.65 = 1.95$$

**Step 6:** Final calculation.

$$\lambda = \dfrac{315}{1.95} = 161.5 \ \text{nm} \approx 0.162 \ \mu \text{m}$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation.
- A small inter-particle spacing of ~162 nm implies a **dense distribution** of γ′ precipitates, which produces **high dislocation resistance** and excellent high-temperature strength.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B-b):**
> Many students confuse **edge length** with **radius** of the cuboidal γ′. The formula uses the **radius** $r$. Converting $a = 450 \ \text{nm} \to r = 225 \ \text{nm}$ carries **1 mark**. Also, do not forget the unit conversion: $0.45 \ \mu \text{m} = 450 \ \text{nm}$. KTU paper evaluators will deduct 0.5 marks for unit mismatch.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (and Mark Deductions) for this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing γ′ and γ″:** γ′ is L1₂ Ni₃(Al,Ti) — present in almost all Ni-superalloys. γ″ is DO₂₂ Ni₃Nb — present *only* in Inconel 718-type alloys. Mixing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the yield anomaly:** The fact that γ′-strengthened alloys show *increasing* yield strength with temperature is a KTU favourite. Missing this costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Miscalculating δ:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in the numerator, or reporting in wrong units, costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring carbide role:** Students often list only γ′ and forget M₂₃C₆ / M₆C. Co-base alloys especially *depend* on carbides for strength — this is a 3-mark question by itself.
> 5. **PHACOMP oversight:** The electron-vacancy method to predict TCP phase formation (σ, μ, Laves) is a **must-mention** for the 14-mark question on phase stability.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Superalloy definition:** Heat-resistant alloy (Ni, Co, or Fe-Ni matrix) for service > 540 °C.
- **Five major microstructural constituents of Ni-superalloys:** γ matrix, γ′ precipitate, γ″ precipitate (in 718), carbides (MC / M₂₃C₆ / M₆C), and (undesirable) TCP phases (σ, μ, Laves).
- **γ′ is L1₂ (Cu₃Au prototype), coherent with γ FCC matrix.** Volume fraction in modern single-crystal alloys: **60–70 %**.
- **γ″ is DO₂₂ (BCT), only in Inconel 718-family alloys**, metastable above 650 °C, transforms to δ-phase.
- **Yield anomaly:** Strength of γ′-strengthened Ni-superalloys *increases* with T up to ~800 °C — due to Kear–Wilsdorf locks.
- **Lattice misfit δ** must be small (0 to ±0.5 %) for coherent/semi-coherent interface stability.
- **Carbide strengthening:** M₂₃C₆ at grain boundaries prevents grain-boundary sliding — critical for creep life.
- **TCP phases** (σ, μ, Laves) are **brittle and undesirable** — predicted and suppressed using **PHACOMP** (electron-vacancy number $\overline{N_v}$).
- **Co-based superalloys:** Classical = carbide-strengthened (Stellite 6, Haynes 188). Modern (2006+) = γ′-strengthened Co–Al–W alloys.
- **Co γ′** is **L1₂ Co₃(Al,W)** — discovered by **J. Sato (Tohoku University, 2006)**.
- **Co advantages over Ni:** Higher melting point (1495 vs. 1455 °C), better hot-corrosion and wear resistance.
- **Co disadvantages:** Narrow γ′ ternary field, lower γ′ solvus, lower high-T strength (still under research).
- **Key alloys to remember by name:** **Inconel 718** (γ″ workhorse), **Waspaloy** (γ′ turbine disc), **CMSX-4 / PWA 1484** (single crystal turbine blade), **Stellite 6** (Co wear alloy), **Haynes 188** (Co combustor), **MAR-M509** (cast Co).
- **Creep rate** follows **Norton–Bailey** power-law: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} \propto \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c / RT)$, with $n \approx 4$–$8$ for superalloys.
- **Coarsening of γ′** follows **Lifshitz–Slyozov–Wagner (LSW)** Ostwald ripening: $r^3 - r_0^3 = K_0 t \exp(-Q_g/RT)$.
- **Hall–Petch:** Finer grain → higher yield, but for creep at very high T, **coarse grain or single crystal** is preferred.
- **Inter-particle spacing formula:** $\lambda = 4(1-f)r / 3f$ — small λ means stronger alloy.
- **Strengthening hierarchy (descending):** γ′ precipitation > solid-solution > carbide > grain-boundary > ODS.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "compare Ni- and Co-based superalloys," always structure your answer as a **table** (matrix, strengthener, yield anomaly, max T, hot-corrosion, wear, cost, applications, example alloys) — KTU examiners award full marks for clean tabular comparison in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminides and silicides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aluminides and silicides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aluminides — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminides** are a specialized class of **intermetallic compounds** formed between aluminum (Al) and one or more transition/refractory metals (such as Nickel, Iron, Titanium, Cobalt, or Niobium). They are characterized by long-range ordered crystal structures (e.g., B2, L1₂, DO₁₉, L1₀) that yield a unique combination of low density, high melting point, and exceptional oxidation resistance at elevated temperatures.

The general stoichiometric representation of an aluminide is:

$$ M_xAl_y $$

where **M** is a transition metal (Ni, Fe, Ti, Co, etc.) and **x**, **y** are stoichiometric integers. The two most industrially relevant families are:

- **Trialuminides** → MAl₃ (e.g., NiAl₃, TiAl₃, FeAl₃)
- **Monoaluminides** → MAl (e.g., NiAl, FeAl, TiAl, CoAl)
- **Tri-aluminide ordered structures** → M₃Al (e.g., Ni₃Al — famously the basis of superalloys)

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick and Mortar" Model

Imagine a high-rise building constructed from two types of bricks: regular red bricks (the **transition metal atoms** like Ni) and lighter, heat-resistant white bricks (the **Al atoms**). In a normal alloy, the bricks are randomly placed — this gives strength but not special properties. In an **aluminide**, the placement is rigidly ordered in a strict geometric pattern (the ordered lattice), just like a perfectly designed mosaic. This ordering:
- Suppresses dislocation climb (slip)
- Creates a strong, directional bond
- Allows a thin, self-healing **Al₂O₃** layer to form on the surface, acting as a thermal shield.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** The "ordered" nature of aluminides is what differentiates them from simple alloys. The KTU syllabus specifically tests whether students understand **ordered intermetallics vs. disordered solid solutions**.

## 1.2 Silicides — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicides** are compounds formed between silicon (Si) and a more electropositive metal — typically a refractory metal such as **Molybdenum (Mo)**, **Tungsten (W)**, **Titanium (Ti)**, **Tantalum (Ta)**, or **Cobalt (Co)**. Their general formula is MₓSiᵧ, with the most common stoichiometries being **disilicides (MSi₂)**, **monosilicides (MSi)**, and **pentasilicides (M₅Si₃)**.

Silicides are uniquely positioned at the intersection of **structural high-temperature materials** and **functional microelectronic materials**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat-Proof Shield"

Picture a spacecraft re-entering Earth's atmosphere. The leading edges glow cherry-red but never melt. This is achieved using **molybdenum disilicide (MoSi₂)** — a silicide that, much like a knight's heat-resistant armor, simultaneously:
- Conducts electricity (so it can be shaped via EDM)
- Resists oxidation up to **1800 °C** in air
- Forms a passive **SiO₂ glassy layer** that self-repairs if scratched

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike aluminides (which form Al₂O₃), silicides form a **SiO₂** protective layer — a *glassy, vitreous* oxide that flows and self-heals, a property critical for ultra-high-temperature service.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **bold** physical constants and reference metrics are central to the engineering evaluation of these materials:

- **Al₂O₃ (alumina) melting point ≈ 2072 °C**
- **SiO₂ (silica glass) melting point ≈ 1710 °C**
- **NiAl density ≈ 5.86 g/cm³** (vs. Ni-base superalloy ~ 8.5 g/cm³)
- **TiAl density ≈ 3.9 g/cm³** (lighter than Ti-base alloys ~ 4.5 g/cm³)
- **MoSi₂ density ≈ 6.24 g/cm³**
- **Typical service temperature window for Ni₃Al: 1000–1100 °C**
- **Maximum service temperature for MoSi₂ heating elements: 1800 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aluminides

Aluminides are broadly classified into three structural families based on their **ordered crystal structure (Strukturbericht designation)**:

| Family | Strukturbericht | Prototype | Example | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L1₂** | Cubic, ordered | Cu₃Au-type | **Ni₃Al, Ti₃Al (α₂)** | High ductility, used in superalloys |
| **B2** | CsCl-type cubic | CsCl-type | **NiAl, FeAl, CoAl** | High melting point, brittle at RT |
| **L1₀** | Tetragonal, ordered | AuCu-type | **TiAl (γ)** | Lightweight, used in jet engines |
| **DO₁₉** | Hexagonal ordered | Ni₃Sn-type | **Ti₃Al (α₂)** | Moderate ductility, HCP-based |
| **D0₂₂** | Complex tetragonal | — | **TiAl₃** | Very brittle, limited use |

## 2.2 Classification of Silicides

| Silicide | Structure | Density (g/cm³) | Melting Point (°C) | Major Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MoSi₂** | C11ᵦ tetragonal | **6.24** | **2030** | Furnace heating elements |
| **WSi₂** | C11ᵦ tetragonal | 9.86 | 2160 | Microelectronics gates |
| **TiSi₂** | C54 orthorhombic / C49 | 4.04 | 1540 | CMOS interconnects (salicide) |
| **CoSi₂** | C1ᵇ fluorite (CaF₂-type) | 4.95 | 1326 | Low-resistivity contacts |
| **TaSi₂** | Hexagonal C40 | 9.14 | 2200 | High-temp. coatings |
| **Fe₃Si** | DO₃ cubic | 7.25 | 1200 | Soft magnetic, thermistors |
| **CrSi₂** | C40 hexagonal | 4.97 | 1490 | Thermoelectrics |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Ordered Intermetallics

The ordered lattice in aluminides arises from the **strong directional d-orbital bonding** between the transition metal (M) and aluminum. The driving physics:

1. **Size Factor:** Al has a small atomic radius (~ 0.143 nm) compared to Ni (~ 0.124 nm) and Fe (~ 0.124 nm). The size mismatch creates strain energy that is minimized by ordering.
2. **Electronegativity Difference:** Al (1.61) is more electronegative than most transition metals (1.5–1.9), promoting partial ionic character in the M–Al bond.
3. **Anti-phase Boundary (APB) Energy:** High APB energy in Ni₃Al and TiAl forces dislocations to travel in pairs (paired dislocation motion), raising yield stress with temperature — the famous **"yield strength anomaly"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield Strength Anomaly (YSA):** Unique to ordered intermetallics like Ni₃Al. As temperature **increases from RT to ~ 800 °C**, the yield strength **increases** — a phenomenon KTU examiners love to test. Reason: thermally activated cross-slip of (111) ⟨110⟩ dislocations from octahedral to cubic planes creates Kear–Wilsdorf locks.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values in any formula row to maintain valid markdown tables.

| Symbol / Equation | Expression | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Stoichiometric aluminide** | $M_x Al_y$ | General formula for transition-metal aluminides |
| **Nickel aluminide density** | $\rho_{NiAl} \approx 5.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$ | ~ 30 % lighter than superalloys |
| **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** | $PBR = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}}$ | Predicts protective vs. non-protective oxide scale |
| **Protective oxide condition** | $1 \leq PBR \leq 2$ | For Al → forms protective Al₂O₃ (PBR ≈ 1.28) |
| **Coble creep (aluminides)** | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A_c \cdot \dfrac{\sigma \cdot \Omega \cdot D_b}{k_B T \cdot d^3}$ | Grain-boundary diffusion creep at high T |
| **Arrhenius creep** | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \cdot \sigma^n \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | Creep rate; $Q_c$ = activation energy, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) |
| **Yield Strength Anomaly** | $\dfrac{\partial \tau_y}{\partial T} > 0$ for $T < T_p$ | Apparent in Ni₃Al, TiAl |
| **Si–O bond dissociation** | $\Delta H_{Si-O} \approx 798 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Very high → SiO₂ is thermodynamically robust |
| **Al–O bond dissociation** | $\Delta H_{Al-O} \approx 511 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Forms stable Al₂O₃ scale |
| **MoSi₂ oxidation product** | $2 MoSi_2 + 7 O_2 \rightarrow 2 MoO_3 \uparrow + 4 SiO_2$ | Volatile MoO₃ escapes; SiO₂ remains protective |
| **Disilicide general formula** | $MSi_2$ | Most studied silicide family |
| **Salicide (self-aligned silicide)** | $Si + M \rightarrow MSi_2$ | CMOS transistor gate contact formation |
| **Thermoelectric figure of merit** | $ZT = \dfrac{S^2 \sigma T}{\kappa}$ | $S$ = Seebeck coeff., $\sigma$ = elec. cond., $\kappa$ = thermal cond. |
| **Service temp. for MoSi₂** | $T_{max} \approx 1800 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ in air | Exceeds most metallic alloys |
| **Brittle-to-ductile transition (BDT)** | $T_{BDT} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ for silicides | Below BDT, fracture is cleavage-type |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Material Used | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **Jet engine turbine blades (HPT)** | TiAl, Ni₃Al, NiAl | Lightweight, creep-resistant, oxidation-resistant |
| **Industrial furnace heating elements (Kanthal-Super)** | MoSi₂ | Operates in air up to 1800 °C, no vacuum needed |
| **CMOS salicide contacts** | TiSi₂, CoSi₂, NiSi | Low resistivity, self-aligned process compatibility |
| **Automotive turbocharger rotors** | TiAl (γ) | 50 % lighter than Inconel, resists 750–850 °C exhaust gas |
| **High-temp. thermocouple sheaths** | MoSi₂ coatings | Protects Mo, W substrates from oxidation |
| **Thermoelectric generators (TEG)** | CrSi₂, MnSi₁.₇, FeSi₂ | High-temperature Seebeck coefficient |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Aluminides vs. Silicides

The following derivation matrix is the core comparative analysis expected in a KTU 14-mark question. Each row is derived from first principles of physical metallurgy.

| Property | Ni₃Al (γ′) | NiAl (β) | TiAl (γ) | MoSi₂ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Crystal structure** | L1₂ (ordered FCC) | B2 (CsCl-type) | L1₀ (FCC tetragonal) | C11ᵦ tetragonal |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 7.65 | 5.86 | 3.9 | 6.24 |
| **Melting point (°C)** | 1395 | 1638 | 1460 | 2030 |
| **Young's Modulus (GPa)** | 200 | 235 | 170–180 | 440 |
| **RT Ductility** | ~ 5–10 % | ~ 1–2 % (brittle) | ~ 1–2 % (brittle) | ~ 0.5 % (brittle) |
| **Oxidation resistance** | Moderate (forms Al₂O₃) | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent (forms SiO₂) |
| **Key failure mode** | Creep at > 1000 °C | Brittle cleavage at RT | Brittle + poor RT ductility | "Pest" oxidation at 400–600 °C |
| **Industrial use** | Superalloy γ′ phase | Bond coats (TBC) | LPT blades, valves | Heating elements, MOS |

## 3.2 Derivation of Protective vs. Non-Protective Oxide Scale (Pilling–Bedworth)

The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) is the **fundamental engineering tool** to predict whether a metal will form a protective oxide scale. The derivation is shown below step by step.

**Step 1 — Define the volume of the metal consumed per mole of oxide formed.**

Let the metal be of atomic mass $A_m$ and density $\rho_m$. The molar volume of the metal is:

$$ V_m = \dfrac{A_m}{\rho_m} $$

**Step 2 — Define the volume of the oxide produced per mole of oxide.**

For an oxide $M_x O_y$ with molar mass $A_{ox}$ and density $\rho_{ox}$:

$$ V_{ox} = \dfrac{A_{ox}}{n \cdot \rho_{ox}} $$

where $n$ is the number of oxygen atoms per formula unit.

**Step 3 — The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio is the ratio of the oxide volume to the metal volume that produced it.**

$$ PBR = \dfrac{V_{ox}}{V_m} = \dfrac{A_{ox} \cdot \rho_m}{n \cdot A_m \cdot \rho_{ox}} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation rule (derived empirically from oxide mechanics).**

- If $PBR < 1$ → oxide is **porous, non-protective** (oxide volume too small to cover the metal surface, e.g., MgO on Mg).
- If $1 \leq PBR \leq 2$ → oxide is **protective, adherent, compressive** (e.g., $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ on Al with $PBR \approx 1.28$).
- If $PBR > 2$ → oxide **spalls off** due to excessive compressive stress (e.g., $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ on Fe, $PBR \approx 2.15$).

**Step 5 — Application to Al and Si.**

For Al → $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$:

$$ PBR_{Al/Al_2O_3} = \dfrac{M_{Al_2O_3}/\rho_{Al_2O_3}}{2 \cdot M_{Al}/\rho_{Al}} = \dfrac{(101.96/3.98)}{2 \cdot (26.98/2.70)} \approx 1.28 $$

For Si → $\text{SiO}_2$:

$$ PBR_{Si/SiO_2} = \dfrac{M_{SiO_2}/\rho_{SiO_2}}{M_{Si}/\rho_{Si}} = \dfrac{(60.08/2.65)}{28.09/2.33} \approx 1.88 $$

Both values lie in the protective range, justifying the engineering use of aluminides and silicides in oxidizing environments.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Yield Strength Anomaly in Ni₃Al

The unique increase of yield strength with temperature in $\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}$ is rationalized by the **Kear–Wilsdorf lock mechanism**. The analytical transition is shown step by step.

**Step 1 — Dislocation in L1₂ lattice.**

In L1₂ ($\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}$), the primary slip system is the octahedral $\{111\}\langle 110\rangle$. At low temperature, a perfect dislocation dissociates into two **superpartials** separated by an anti-phase boundary (APB):

$$ [110] \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}[110] + APB + \tfrac{1}{2}[110] $$

**Step 2 — Cross-slip to cube plane.**

As temperature rises, thermal activation allows the leading superpartial to cross-slip from the octahedral $(111)$ plane onto the cube $(010)$ plane, where the APB energy is higher. This locks the dislocation.

**Step 3 — Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS) for unlocking.**

The temperature-dependent CRSS is given by:

$$ \tau_{CRSS}(T) = \tau_0 + A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta F}{k_B T}\right) $$

where $\Delta F$ is the activation free energy for cross-slip and $A$ is a constant. The exponential term explains why $\tau_{CRSS}$ rises with $T$ until a peak temperature $T_p$ (~ 800 °C for Ni₃Al), beyond which other mechanisms (climb) dominate.

**Step 4 — Practical implication.**

The YSA makes Ni₃Al an ideal **γ′-strengthener** in Ni-base superalloys — it remains strong exactly when the engine is hottest.

## 3.4 Derivation of the "Pest" Phenomenon in MoSi₂

"Pest" is a catastrophic disintegration of MoSi₂ into powder at 400–600 °C. The derivation of why it occurs is as follows.

**Step 1 — Initial oxidation reaction at intermediate temperature.**

$$ 2 MoSi_2 + 7 O_2 \rightarrow 2 MoO_3 \uparrow + 4 SiO_2 $$

**Step 2 — Volatile MoO₃ escapes** through grain boundaries and microcracks.

**Step 3 — Vacancy condensation** at grain boundaries causes intergranular cracking.

**Step 4 — Repeated penetration of oxygen** through these cracks leads to a chain reaction, fragmenting the bulk into a powder ("pest").

**Step 5 — Mitigation strategies (engineering solutions).**

- Add small amounts of W, Re, B, or C to suppress grain-boundary diffusion.
- Use **MoSi₂ + SiC or MoSi₂ + Al₂O₃ composites**.
- Apply protective **SiO₂-forming pre-oxidation treatment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often ask "Why does MoSi₂ fail at 500 °C but excel at 1500 °C?" — the answer is pesting. Above 1000 °C, a continuous, viscous SiO₂ layer forms and seals grain boundaries; below 600 °C, the SiO₂ is solid and brittle, allowing oxygen ingress.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Crystal Structure Architecture — L1₂ Ni₃Al

The ordered face-centered cubic (FCC) arrangement of $\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}$ is shown below. Corner sites are occupied by Al atoms, while face-centered sites are occupied by Ni atoms. The ordered nature gives the superalloy its high-temperature strength.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Crystal Type: L12 Ordered FCC"] --> B["Corner Site: Al Atom"]
    A --> C["Face Center Site: Ni Atom"]
    A --> D["Strukturbericht: L12"]
    A --> E["Space Group: Pm-3m"]
    A --> F["Lattice Parameter: a = 0.357 nm"]
    B --> G["Forms Al2O3 scale"]
    C --> H["Provides ductility"]
    G --> I["High temp oxidation resistance"]
    H --> J["RT ductility 5 to 10 percent"]
    I --> K["Application Jet engine turbine blade"]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — High-Temperature Protection Mechanism of MoSi₂

A **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the protective oxidation mechanism of MoSi₂ is provided below. This substitutes for a complex physical diagram.

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Hot Oxidizing Atmosphere above 1000 C"] --> stepB["O2 Diffuses to MoSi2 Surface"]
    stepB --> stepC["Reaction: 2MoSi2 + 7O2 to 2MoO3 gas + 4SiO2"]
    stepC --> stepD["MoO3 Volatilizes and Escapes"]
    stepC --> stepE["SiO2 Forms Viscous Glassy Layer"]
    stepE --> stepF["Glass Layer Seals Grain Boundaries and Cracks"]
    stepF --> stepG["Self Healing Passive Barrier Established"]
    stepG --> stepH["Continuous Protection up to 1800 C"]
    stepI["Intermediate T 400 to 600 C Regime"] --> stepJ["SiO2 Solid Brittle"]
    stepJ --> stepK["O2 Penetrates Cracks"]
    stepK --> stepL["Pest Disintegration Catastrophic"]
    stepL --> stepM["Mitigation: Add W, Re, B, or Form Composite"]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Aluminide vs. Silicide Selection Logic

The block-level decision flow for selecting between aluminides and silicides in an engineering design context is shown below.

```mermaid
graph TD
    selectA["Service Temperature Requirement"] --> selectB{"Operating T Range"}
    selectB -->|"600 to 1100 C"| selectC["Choose Aluminides"]
    selectB -->|"1000 to 1800 C"| selectD["Choose Silicides"]
    selectB -->|"400 to 600 C"| selectE["Avoid MoSi2 Pest Regime"]
    selectC --> selectF["Lightweight priority Use TiAl"]
    selectC --> selectG["Creep resistance priority Use Ni3Al gamma prime"]
    selectC --> selectH["Oxidation plus TBC bond coat Use NiAl beta"]
    selectD --> selectI["Furnace heating element Use MoSi2"]
    selectD --> selectJ["Microelectronic gate Use TiSi2 or CoSi2"]
    selectD --> selectK["Thermoelectric Use CrSi2 or MnSi"]
```

## 4.4 Microstructural Schematic — Anti-Phase Boundary (APB) in Ni₃Al

The Kear–Wilsdorf lock mechanism, which gives rise to the Yield Strength Anomaly, is mapped below as a block-level architecture.

```mermaid
graph TD
    microA["Superpartial Leading 1 by 2 110 on Octahedral 111 Plane"] --> microB["Temperature Rises to 600 to 800 C"]
    microB --> microC["Cross Slip to Cube Plane 010"]
    microC --> microD["APB Energy on Cube Higher"]
    microD --> microE["Kear Wilsdorf Lock Formed"]
    microE --> microF["Dislocation Motion Hindered"]
    microF --> microG["CRSS Increases with T"]
    microG --> microH["Yield Strength Anomaly Observed"]
    microH --> microI["Engineers Exploit: Ni3Al Strengthens Superalloy at T"]
```
