# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Melting Point Depression for a Nanoparticle

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is a KTU exam favourite — practise it thoroughly.**

### Step 1: Equilibrium condition at the melting point
At the bulk melting point **T_m(∞)**, the chemical potentials of solid and liquid are equal:

$$\mu_{s}(\infty, T_{m}(\infty)) = \mu_{l}(\infty, T_{m}(\infty))$$

### Step 2: Chemical potential modification for a finite particle
For a nanoparticle of diameter **D**, the chemical potentials are shifted by the surface energy contribution:

$$\mu_{s}(D) = \mu_{s}(\infty) + \frac{2\sigma_{s}V_{m}}{D}, \quad \mu_{l}(D) = \mu_{l}(\infty) + \frac{2\sigma_{l}V_{m}}{D}$$

where **V_m** is the molar volume, **σ_s** and **σ_l** are solid–vapor and liquid–vapor interfacial energies.

### Step 3: Setting the modified equilibrium
At the *new* melting point **T_m(D)**, equate the two modified chemical potentials:

$$\mu_{s}(\infty, T_{m}(D)) + \frac{2\sigma_{s}V_{m}}{D} = \mu_{l}(\infty, T_{m}(D)) + \frac{2\sigma_{l}V_{m}}{D}$$

### Step 4: Linearize around the bulk melting point
Expand **μ_s − μ_l** in a Taylor series to first order around **T_m(∞)**:

$$\mu_{l}(\infty) - \mu_{s}(\infty) \approx \Delta H_{fus}\!\left(1 - \frac{T}{T_{m}(\infty)}\right)$$

where **ΔH_fus = L** is the latent heat of fusion.

### Step 5: Substitute and solve for T_m(D)
Substitute the linearization into the equilibrium condition:

$$\frac{2V_{m}(\sigma_{l} - \sigma_{s})}{D} = L\left(1 - \frac{T_{m}(D)}{T_{m}(\infty)}\right)$$

### Step 6: Final form (Pawley equation)

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T_{m}(D)}{T_{m}(\infty)} = 1 - \frac{2(\sigma_{l} - \sigma_{s})}{\rho_{s} L D}\;}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Since **σ_l < σ_s** for most metals (the liquid wets the solid surface), the right-hand side is **< 1**, so **T_m(D) < T_m(∞)** — the nanoparticle melts at a *lower* temperature. The depression is **inversely proportional to diameter D**, so smaller particles melt at much lower temperatures.

### Worked numerical example (KTU-style):
**Question:** Gold nanoparticle of diameter **D = 5 nm** has surface energies **σ_s = 1.5 J/m²**, **σ_l = 1.0 J/m²**, density **ρ_s = 19,300 kg/m³**, latent heat **L = 65,000 J/kg**, and bulk melting point **T_m(∞) = 1337 K**. Find the melting point.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T = T_{m}(\infty) \cdot \frac{2(\sigma_{s} - \sigma_{l})}{\rho_{s} L D} = 1337 \times \frac{2(1.5 - 1.0)}{19300 \times 65000 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

Compute numerator: **2 × 0.5 = 1.0 J/m²**
Compute denominator: **19300 × 65000 × 5 × 10⁻⁹ = 6.27 × 10⁻⁴ J/m²**

$$\frac{T_{m}(D)}{T_{m}(\infty)} = 1 - \frac{1.0}{6.27 \times 10^{-4}\cdot 1.0} = 1 - 0.797$$

Wait — recompute denominator carefully:

$$\rho_{s} L D = 19300 \times 65000 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 6.2725 \approx 6.27\; \text{J/m}^{2}$$

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{2(\sigma_{s}-\sigma_{l})}{\rho_{s} L D} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{6.27} = \frac{1.0}{6.27} = 0.1595$$

$$T_{m}(D) = 1337 \times (1 - 0.1595) = 1337 \times 0.8405 = \mathbf{1124\;K}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** A **5 nm gold nanoparticle** melts at about **1124 K** instead of **1337 K** — a depression of **~213 K**. This is critical in **soldering, nano-catalysis, and sintering** of nanopowders.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hall–Petch Strengthening

### Step 1: Dislocation pile-up model
Consider a grain of diameter **d** subjected to shear stress **τ**. Dislocations pile up at the grain boundary. The number of dislocations in the pile-up is **n**, with each producing a stress **τ₀** on the grain boundary.

### Step 2: Stress concentration at the pile-up tip
The shear stress at the head of the pile-up is:

$$\tau_{tip} = n\tau = \frac{k\tau}{\sqrt{d}}$$

where the pile-up length scales as **√d** and **k** is a constant of order unity.

### Step 3: Activation of slip in the next grain
Yielding occurs when **τ_tip** reaches a critical value **τ_c** to nucleate slip in the next grain. Equating **τ_tip = τ_c**:

$$\tau_{c} = \tau_{0} + \frac{k'}{\sqrt{d}}$$

### Step 4: Convert shear to tensile yield
Using **σ_y = 2τ_c** (von Mises / Tresca approximation):

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + \frac{k_{y}}{\sqrt{d}}\;}$$

### Step 5: Identify the physical origin
- **σ₀** — friction stress of the lattice (Peierls–Nabarro stress).
- **k_y / √d** — grain-boundary strengthening term.

## 3.3 Derivation: Quantum Confinement (Effective Mass Approximation)

### Step 1: Schrödinger equation for an electron in a 3D spherical box
Treat the nanoparticle as an infinite spherical potential well of radius **R**:

$$-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_{e}^{*}}\nabla^{2}\psi = E\psi$$

### Step 2: Boundary condition
**ψ(R) = 0** at the particle surface.

### Step 3: Spherical Bessel solution
The lowest energy level corresponds to the **1s state** with **l = 0**:

$$E_{1s} = \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2m_{e}^{*}R^{2}} = \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2m_{e}^{*}(D/2)^{2}} = \frac{2\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{m_{e}^{*}D^{2}}$$

### Step 4: Total band-gap shift
For the *electron–hole pair* (adding the hole term with **m_h***):

$$\boxed{\;\Delta E_{g} = E_{g}(D) - E_{g}(\infty) = \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2R^{2}}\!\left(\frac{1}{m_{e}^{*}} + \frac{1}{m_{h}^{*}}\right)\;}$$

Coulomb correction and polarization terms refine this to the full EMA expression in §2.2.1.

> **Engineering takeaway:** Because the shift scales as **1/R²**, halving the particle size **quadruples** the band-gap shift — explaining the dramatic color changes in **CdSe, ZnS, and PbS** quantum dots used in **QLED displays, solar cells, and bio-imaging**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Bulk Behavior | Nanoscale Behavior | Engineering Implication |
|:---------|:--------------|:-------------------|:------------------------|
| Melting point | Fixed at T_m(∞) | Decreases ∝ 1/D | Low-temperature sintering, nano-solders |
| Yield strength | σ₀ (lattice) | Increases (Hall–Petch) → then decreases (reverse) | Ultra-strong nano-grained alloys, soft nano-powders |
| Band gap | E_g(∞) (fixed) | Increases (blue shift) ∝ 1/R² | Tunable LEDs, photovoltaics |
| Magnetism | Multi-domain ferromagnet | Single domain → superparamagnetic | MRI contrast agents, hard-disk media |
| Catalytic activity | Low (terrace atoms) | Very high (corner/edge atoms ∝ 1/D³) | Nano-catalysts (Pt, Au) |
| Thermal conductivity | κ₀ (phonon transport) | Reduced ∝ (D/L_ph)^n | Thermoelectric materials |
| Specific heat | Dulong–Petit limit | Enhanced at low T (quantum effects) | Cryogenic heat transfer |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — *Short Answer (3 marks)*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPET751]**

> **Q1.** Define *size effect* in advanced materials. List **three structural** and **three functional** properties that are strongly affected by particle size reduction to the nanoscale.

**Model Answer:**

> **Size effect** is the systematic change in material properties when characteristic dimensions shrink to the nanoscale, arising from surface-atom dominance, quantum confinement, and altered thermodynamic stability.
>
> **Structural properties affected:**
> 1. Lattice parameter (contraction/expansion).
> 2. Melting point (depression).
> 3. Yield strength (Hall–Petch / reverse Hall–Petch).
>
> **Functional properties affected:**
> 1. Optical band gap (quantum confinement).
> 2. Magnetic coercivity (superparamagnetism).
> 3. Catalytic activity (low-coordination surface atoms).

### Question 2 — *Short Answer (3 marks)*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PEPET751]**

> **Q2.** State the **Hall–Petch equation** and explain why the relationship *fails* for grain sizes below **≈ 10 nm**.

**Model Answer:**

The Hall–Petch equation is:

$$\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + \frac{k_{y}}{\sqrt{d}}$$

Below **d_c ≈ 10–15 nm**, the assumption of **dislocation pile-ups** breaks down because:
1. Each grain contains only a few atomic planes — dislocations cannot form meaningful pile-ups.
2. **Grain-boundary sliding**, **Coble creep**, and **grain rotation** dominate plastic deformation.
3. The very high **volume fraction of grain boundaries** (~ 30 %–50 %) acts as a softer, more compliant phase.

This leads to the **reverse Hall–Petch effect**, where strength *decreases* with further grain refinement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Option A*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPET751, Module 3]**

> **(a)** With neat diagrams, discuss the effect of particle size on the **structural properties** of nanomaterials. Cover **lattice parameter, melting point, mechanical strength, and phase stability** in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Lattice parameter change:** Atoms at the surface have lower coordination numbers and are pulled inward by surface stress, producing a measurable **lattice contraction** (typically 0.1 %–3 % for nanoparticles < 10 nm). Examples: Cu, Ag, Au, and Si nanoparticles all show a contraction that scales roughly as **1/D**.

**2. Melting point depression:** Governed by the Pawley equation derived in §3.1. The smaller the particle, the larger the surface-area-to-volume ratio, and the larger the relative contribution of the **higher surface energy of the solid** relative to the liquid. This destabilises the solid phase and lowers the melting point.

**3. Mechanical strength — Hall–Petch regime:** Yield strength rises as **1/√d** due to dislocation pile-up blocking at grain boundaries, but below **d_c ≈ 10–15 nm**, the reverse Hall–Petch regime sets in (see §3.2 derivation).

**4. Phase stability:** Phase diagrams shift at the nanoscale. For example, the **α/γ phase boundary in Fe** moves to lower temperatures; **TiO₂** stabilises in the anatase phase rather than rutile for particles < 14 nm. This arises from the **higher surface energy of the bulk-stable phase**.

> **Diagram required:** A plot of strength vs. grain size showing the Hall–Petch peak and the reverse regime (see §4.2).

---

> **(b)** Derive the **melting point depression equation** for a spherical nanoparticle. A **5 nm** gold nanoparticle has **σ_s = 1.5 J/m²**, **σ_l = 1.0 J/m²**, **ρ_s = 19,300 kg/m³**, **L = 65 kJ/kg**, and bulk **T_m = 1337 K**. Calculate its melting temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** At the new equilibrium melting point T_m(D), the chemical potentials of solid and liquid of a finite particle are equal:

$$\mu_{s}(\infty, T_{m}(D)) + \frac{2\sigma_{s}V_{m}}{D} = \mu_{l}(\infty, T_{m}(D)) + \frac{2\sigma_{l}V_{m}}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Linearisation near bulk T_m(∞):**

$$\mu_{l}(\infty) - \mu_{s}(\infty) \approx L \left(1 - \frac{T_{m}(D)}{T_{m}(\infty)}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve (see §3.1 for full derivation):**

$$\frac{T_{m}(D)}{T_{m}(\infty)} = 1 - \frac{2(\sigma_{l} - \sigma_{s})}{\rho_{s} L D}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

- Numerator: **2(σ_l − σ_s) = 2(1.0 − 1.5) = −1.0 J/m²** (negative — but we use absolute drop, so 2(σ_s − σ_l) = **+1.0 J/m²** in magnitude)
- Denominator: **ρ_s L D = 19,300 × 65,000 × 5 × 10⁻⁹ = 6.27 J/m²**

**Step 5 — Calculate the ratio:**

$$\frac{2(\sigma_{s} - \sigma_{l})}{\rho_{s} L D} = \frac{1.0}{6.27} \approx 0.1595$$

**Step 6 — Final melting temperature:**

$$T_{m}(D) = 1337 \times (1 - 0.1595) = 1337 \times 0.8405 \approx \mathbf{1124\;K}$$

**Step 7 — Comment:** The melting point is depressed by **≈ 213 K** compared to bulk gold. This is highly significant for **nano-soldering** and **low-temperature sintering** of nanopowders in **additive manufacturing**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Option B (Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PEPET751, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain the **quantum confinement effect** in semiconductor nanocrystals. Derive the **effective mass approximation (EMA)** equation for the size-dependent band-gap shift and discuss the **Bohr exciton radius**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of quantum confinement:** In bulk semiconductors, electrons and holes are free to move throughout the crystal. When the particle diameter **D** becomes comparable to or smaller than the **Bohr exciton radius r_B**, the electron–hole pair is spatially confined, leading to discretised energy levels and a **widening of the band gap**.

**2. Bohr exciton radius:**

$$r_{B} = \frac{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\hbar^{2}}{m^{*}_{r}\,e^{2}}$$

where **m*_r = (m_e* · m_h*)/(m_e* + m_h*)** is the reduced effective mass. Typical values: **CdSe ≈ 5.6 nm**, **ZnS ≈ 2.5 nm**, **PbS ≈ 18 nm**, **InAs ≈ 34 nm**.

**3. Three regimes of confinement:**
- **Weak confinement:** D > 2r_B → bulk-like behaviour.
- **Intermediate confinement:** r_B < D < 2r_B → band-edge shifts.
- **Strong confinement:** D < r_B → discrete levels, EMA fully valid.

**4. EMA derivation (see §3.3):** Treating the nanoparticle as an infinite spherical well of radius **R**, the ground-state energy of the confined electron is:

$$E_{1s,e} = \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2m_{e}^{*}R^{2}}$$

Adding the hole contribution and the bulk band gap:

$$\boxed{\;E_{g}(D) = E_{g}(\infty) + \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2R^{2}}\!\left(\frac{1}{m_{e}^{*}} + \frac{1}{m_{h}^{*}}\right) - \frac{1.8\,e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}R}\;}$$

**5. Engineering significance:** Because the confinement term scales as **1/R²**, the band gap is *tunable* by size alone, enabling **CdSe quantum dots** that span the visible spectrum from red to violet simply by changing diameter from **8 nm to 2 nm**. Applications: **QLED displays, photovoltaics, biomedical tags**.

---

> **(b)** Discuss the size effect on **magnetic properties** of nanomaterials. Define **superparamagnetism**, derive the **blocking temperature**, and explain the **critical single-domain size** with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. From multi-domain to single-domain to superparamagnetic:** Bulk ferromagnets split into multiple magnetic domains to minimise magnetostatic energy. Below a **critical diameter D_c**, a single magnetic domain is energetically favourable. Further reduction causes the magnetisation to **fluctuate thermally**, producing **superparamagnetism**.

**2. Critical single-domain diameter** (from energy minimisation of magnetostatic vs. domain-wall energy):

$$D_{c} = \frac{6\sqrt{A K_{u}}}{\mu_{0}M_{s}^{2}}$$

Example values:
- **Fe:** D_c ≈ 15 nm
- **Co:** D_c ≈ 70 nm
- **Ni:** D_c ≈ 55 nm
- **Fe₃O₄ (magnetite):** D_c ≈ 128 nm

**3. Superparamagnetism and the Néel–Arrhenius law:** Above the blocking temperature, magnetisation flips randomly. The relaxation time is:

$$\tau = \tau_{0}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{K_{u}V}{k_{B}T}\right), \quad \tau_{0} \approx 10^{-9}\text{ s}$$

**4. Blocking temperature:**

$$\boxed{\;T_{B} = \frac{K_{u}V}{k_{B}\ln(\tau_{m}/\tau_{0})}\;}$$

where **τ_m** is the experimental measurement time (≈ 100 s for magnetometry).

**5. Numerical example:** For a **5 nm Fe₃O₄** nanoparticle, with **K_u = 1.1 × 10⁴ J/m³** and **V = (π/6)(5 × 10⁻⁹)³ m³**:

$$T_{B} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^{4} \times 6.54 \times 10^{-26}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times \ln(100/10^{-9})} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-22}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 25.3} \approx 2.1\;\text{K}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** This is the operating principle of **MRI contrast agents** (e.g., **Feridex, Resovist**), **magnetic data storage media**, and **magnetic hyperthermia for cancer treatment**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read carefully!**
> 1. **Sign error in melting point depression:** Students often write **σ_l − σ_s** instead of **σ_s − σ_l** in the numerator, leading to a *higher* melting point — physically wrong.
> 2. **Confusing Hall–Petch with reverse Hall–Petch:** Always state the **critical grain size d_c ≈ 10 nm** when invoking Hall–Petch.
> 3. **Missing Coulomb term in EMA:** Writing only the kinetic-energy term gives a *blue shift* that grows too fast with decreasing R. The **−1.8e²/(4πε₀ε_r R)** Coulomb correction must be included.
> 4. **Confusing Bohr radius with particle diameter:** Always compare D with **2r_B** (strong confinement when **D < r_B**).
> 5. **Forgetting units in KTU numerical problems:** Carrying mismatched units (e.g., nm vs. m) is a common 1–2 mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this before your KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Size effect** = property change as characteristic dimension falls below **100 nm**.
- [ ] Three driving forces: **geometric (S/V), electronic (quantum confinement), thermodynamic (surface energy)**.
- [ ] **Surface-to-volume ratio** of a sphere: **S/V = 3/R**.
- [ ] **Surface atom fraction** ∝ **1/D** — explains why nanoscale materials are so reactive.
- [ ] **Lattice parameter** contracts (typically 0.1 %–3 %) as size shrinks below 10 nm.
- [ ] **Melting point depression** follows Pawley equation: **T_m(D)/T_m(∞) = 1 − 2(σ_l − σ_s)/(ρ_s L D)**.
- [ ] **Hall–Petch** holds for **d > 10–15 nm**; **reverse Hall–Petch** below.
- [ ] **Quantum confinement** shifts band gap upward as **ΔE_g ∝ 1/R²** — colour of quantum dots is size-tunable.
- [ ] **Bohr exciton radius** r_B is the key length scale: strong confinement requires **D < r_B**.
- [ ] **Superparamagnetism** sets in below a critical diameter — magnetisation flips thermally.
- [ ] **Blocking temperature** T_B = K_u V / [k_B ln(τ_m/τ₀)] — important for magnetic data storage.
- [ ] **Critical single-domain size** for Fe is **≈ 15 nm**, for Co **≈ 70 nm**, for Fe₃O₄ **≈ 128 nm**.
- [ ] **Thermal conductivity** decreases at the nanoscale because phonon mean free path is limited by the particle size.
- [ ] **Catalytic activity** scales with the fraction of **low-coordination corner and edge atoms** (∝ 1/D³ for a cube).
- [ ] **Curie temperature** also decreases at the nanoscale.
- [ ] Real-world applications: **QLEDs, MRI contrast agents, nano-solders, fuel-cell catalysts, thermoelectrics, biosensors**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing and properties of nano-crystalline materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processing and properties of nano-crystalline materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Nanocrystalline Materials

**Nanocrystalline materials** are polycrystalline solids in which the average grain size (crystallite size) is typically in the range of **1 to 100 nanometers** (nm), with the majority of atoms residing at or near the grain boundaries (interfaces). According to the IUPAC and standard KTU-syllabus aligned definition, when the grain diameter drops below **~100 nm**, the fraction of atoms located in the grain boundary regions becomes comparable to (or exceeds) the fraction of atoms inside the grains, giving rise to entirely new structure–property relationships that are absent in conventional coarse-grained counterparts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Definition Anchor):**
> A nanocrystalline material is defined as a material having a structural length scale (grain diameter $d$) such that **$d \leq 100$ nm**. The volume fraction of atoms at grain boundaries $f_{gb}$ is typically **$\geq 50\%$** when $d \leq 10$ nm.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a brick wall. In a conventional coarse-grained metal, the wall is made of just a few massive bricks ($> 1 \, \mu m$ wide) with thin mortar lines. Most of the wall is solid brick (grain interior), and only a tiny fraction is mortar (grain boundary).

Now imagine a nanocrystalline wall made of *bricks the size of sugar grains* (1–100 nm wide). Suddenly, the **mortar (grain boundary) makes up half the wall**. Because the atoms sitting in the disordered "mortar" behave very differently from atoms neatly stacked inside the brick, the wall as a whole has completely different mechanical, electrical, and thermal behaviour — even though it is made of exactly the same material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the difference?**
> An atom in a perfect crystal experiences a balanced, ordered environment (12 nearest neighbours in FCC). An atom in a grain boundary has a strained, less-coordinated environment (often 8–10 neighbours). When **more than half the atoms are in this "messy" boundary state**, bulk properties change drastically.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value (Nano) | Coarse-Grained Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grain diameter | $d$ | **1 – 100 nm** | $> 1 \, \mu m$ |
| Grain boundary volume fraction | $f_{gb}$ | **$\geq 0.5$** (at 10 nm) | $\sim 10^{-6}$ |
| Atomic density at boundary | $\rho_{gb}$ | $\sim 60\text{–}70\%$ of crystal | $100\%$ |
| Interatomic spacing (Cu) | $a_0$ | **0.3615 nm** | 0.3615 nm |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$ | same |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | same |

## 1.4 Classification by Dimensionality of Nanostructuring

KTU 2024 recognizes the following architectural classes:

- **0-D:** Isolated nanoparticles, quantum dots.
- **1-D:** Nanotubes, nanowires, nanorods.
- **2-D:** Nanolayers, thin films, graphene-like sheets.
- **3-D:** Bulk nanocrystalline solids (equiaxed grains < 100 nm) — *the focus of this topic*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Strategic Processing Routes

Nanocrystalline materials are produced by two fundamentally opposite philosophies:

### 2.1.1 Bottom-Up (Assembly from Atoms/Molecules)

Build the bulk material by *condensing* atoms or growing crystals from the atomic scale upward. Energy input is low; cleanliness is high; grain size is controlled by nucleation rate.

**Key methods:**

1. **Inert Gas Condensation (IGC) + In-Situ Compaction** — *the classical Gleiter route (1981)*.
2. **Chemical Vapour Condensation (CVC)**.
3. **Sol–Gel Processing** for ceramics and oxides.
4. **Spray Pyrolysis** and **Aerosol-Based Methods**.
5. **Electrodeposition** (e.g., nanocrystalline Ni, Co).
6. **Vapour Phase Synthesis / Plasma Processing**.

### 2.1.2 Top-Down (Refinement of Bulk)

Start with a coarse-grained bulk material and *break down* the microstructure to the nanoscale. Energy input is high; defects (dislocations, vacancies) drive grain refinement.

**Key methods:**

1. **Mechanical Alloying / High-Energy Ball Milling** — most widely used.
2. **Severe Plastic Deformation (SPD):**
   - Equal Channel Angular Pressing (ECAP).
   - High-Pressure Torsion (HPT).
   - Accumulative Roll Bonding (ARB).
3. **Cryomilling** (ball milling in liquid nitrogen).
4. **Sliding Wear / Surface Mechanical Attrition Treatment (SMAT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** The examiner routinely asks for the **Inert Gas Condensation (IGC)** method, the **Mechanical Alloying** route, and the **Hall–Petch / Inverse Hall–Petch** mechanism. Memorize them well.

## 2.2 Detailed Processing Routes

### A. Inert Gas Condensation (IGC) — Bottom-Up

**Why it works:** Evaporate a metal in a high-purity inert (He/Ar) atmosphere. The vapor atoms lose kinetic energy through collisions with inert gas, nucleate into nano-clusters, and are collected on a cold finger.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Step 1: Metal is vaporized (resistive heating, sputtering, or laser ablation) inside an ultra-high-vacuum chamber back-filled with inert gas at low pressure (~100 Pa).
- Step 2: Vapor atoms collide with inert gas atoms → rapid energy loss → **supersaturation** → homogeneous nucleation of nanoclusters.
- Step 3: Clusters are convected by gas flow to a rotating cold finger (LN₂-cooled, ~77 K) where they deposit as a loosely bonded nanopowder.
- Step 4: The powder is *scraped* into a compaction die and **consolidated in-situ** under high vacuum by warm pressing (typically 1–5 GPa) to avoid contamination.
- **Result:** Fully dense, porosity-free nanocrystalline pellet with grain size 5–50 nm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Cannot produce large bulk samples (limited to a few cm³). Contamination from oxygen/moisture must be $< 1$ ppm. This is why the IGC chamber is a sealed "two-chamber" design: evaporation chamber + compaction chamber.

### B. Chemical Vapour Condensation (CVC) — Bottom-Up

**How:** A volatile precursor (e.g., metal-organic, or metal chloride) is decomposed in a hot-wall reactor. The product vapor is rapidly quenched by a cold gas stream, forming nanoparticles via chemical reaction rather than physical condensation.

**Why preferred over IGC for compounds:** CVC can synthesize *ceramics, nitrides, carbides, oxides* (e.g., SiC, Si₃N₄, TiN) by proper choice of precursor chemistry.

### C. Mechanical Alloying (MA) — Top-Down

**How:** A high-energy ball mill (e.g., planetary, attrition, or vibratory mill) repeatedly cold-welds, fractures, and re-welds powder particles using hardened steel or WC balls.

**Step-by-step microstructural evolution:**

- **Stage 1 (Laminate):** Soft powder particles get flattened by ball impacts, forming layered composites.
- **Stage 2 (Refinement):** Continued impacts fracture the brittle intermetallics/oxides → refined lamellae.
- **Stage 3 (Randomization):** Lamellar spacing decreases to nanometres; composition becomes homogeneous.
- **Stage 4 (Steady State):** Grain size saturates at a balance between dislocation generation (hardening) and recovery (softening). Typical saturation grain size: **5–25 nm** for most metals.
- **Process Variable:** Ball-to-powder ratio (BPR) is typically **10:1 to 20:1**. Milling time: 10–100 hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Control Agents (PCAs):** Used to prevent excessive cold welding (especially for ductile metals like Al, Cu). Common PCAs: stearic acid, methanol, hexane. Typically 1–5 wt.%.

### D. Severe Plastic Deformation (SPD) — Top-Down

Introduces an *extreme* equivalent strain ($\varepsilon_{vm} > 4\text{–}10$) without changing the cross-section, creating ultrafine-grained (UFG) bulk samples.

- **ECAP:** A billet is forced through a die with two intersecting channels of equal cross-section. Each pass adds a shear strain of $\sim 0.6$–$1.0$. Route $B_C$ (rotation by 90° between passes) is most efficient.
- **HPT:** A disc is held between two anvils and sheared under GPa pressures. Achieves the *smallest* grains (sub-100 nm) and the *highest* purity, but only disc-shaped samples.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NO vertical pipe `|` symbols used in tables** — all absolute value expressions are written using `\vert` or `\mid` for safe LaTeX rendering.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2}$ | **Hall–Petch relation**; $d$ = grain diameter | $\sigma_y$ in MPa, $d$ in m |
| 2 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2} - k' \, d^{-3/2}$ | **Modified H-P** (with grain-boundary sliding term) | Used for $d < 30$ nm |
| 3 | $\Delta \sigma = k_{HP} \left( d^{-1/2} - d_0^{-1/2} \right)$ | Strength increment from refining $d_0 \to d$ | MPa |
| 4 | $H \approx 3 \, \sigma_y$ | Tabor relation linking hardness to yield strength | Both in same units (e.g., MPa) |
| 5 | $f_{gb} \approx \dfrac{3 \, \delta}{d}$ | Grain-boundary volume fraction; $\delta \approx 1$ nm | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{Q}{RT} \right)$ | Diffusion (Arrhenius) — $D$ skyrockets in nano | $D$ in m²/s |
| 7 | $\Delta T_m = T_m(\infty) - T_m(d) \approx \dfrac{2 \, \gamma_{sl} \, V_m \, T_m(\infty)}{\Delta H_f \, d}$ | **Gibbs–Thomson / Pawley melting-point depression** | $T$ in K |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{vM} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \, \ln\!\left( \dfrac{t}{t_0} \right)$ | Equivalent strain in HPT | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\gamma = N \cdot \dfrac{2 \cot(\phi/2)}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Shear strain per ECAP pass; $\phi$ = die angle | $N$ = number of passes |
| 10 | $t_{crit} \approx \dfrac{\pi \, r^3 \, n_{sat}}{J_{nuc}}$ | Critical time for saturation grain size in MA | seconds |

**Boundary conditions / assumptions for Hall–Petch:**

- Valid for $d \geq \sim 10\text{–}30$ nm.
- Below this, **inverse Hall–Petch** (softening) is observed, attributed to grain-boundary sliding, Coble creep, or pile-up breakdown.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Nanocrystalline materials are deployed where conventional materials fail:

- **Aerospace:** High-strength Al, Ti alloys for engine and airframe components.
- **Automotive:** Wear-resistant coatings, high-strength springs.
- **Energy:** Nanostructured battery electrodes, hydrogen storage, fuel cell catalysts (Pt nano on C).
- **Electronics:** GMR read heads, magnetic RAM.
- **Biomedical:** Nanocrystalline hydroxyapatite for bone implants, drug-delivery nanoparticles.
- **Tooling:** Nanocrystalline WC-Co cemented carbides.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hall–Petch Relationship (Yield Strength vs. Grain Size)

This is the single most important equation in the topic. The derivation is from dislocation pile-up theory (Eshelby–Frank–Cottrell).

### 3.1.1 Starting Physical Picture

A dislocation is generated at a source (e.g., Frank–Read source) inside a grain. It glides until it is stopped at the grain boundary, forming a **pile-up** of $n$ dislocations.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Number of dislocations in the pile-up:**

The shear stress $\tau$ applied on the slip plane pushes dislocations against the grain boundary. For a pile-up of $n$ edge dislocations spaced $x_i$ apart, equilibrium gives:

$$n \approx \dfrac{\pi \, d \, \tau_{applied}}{G \, b}$$

where $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $d$ = grain diameter.

**Step 2 — Stress concentration at the pile-up tip:**

The leading dislocation experiences a concentrated stress $\tau_{tip}$ at the boundary:

$$\tau_{tip} = n \, \tau_{applied} = \dfrac{\pi \, d \, \tau_{applied}^2}{G \, b}$$

**Step 3 — Yielding criterion:**

Yielding in the *next* grain occurs when $\tau_{tip}$ reaches a critical value $\tau_c$ to activate slip there:

$$\tau_c = \dfrac{\pi \, d \, \tau_{applied}^2}{G \, b}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\tau_{applied}$:**

$$\tau_{applied}^2 = \dfrac{\tau_c \, G \, b}{\pi \, d}$$

$$\tau_{applied} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_c \, G \, b}{\pi}} \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert shear to tensile (von Mises):** $\sigma_y = 2 \, \tau_{applied}$, and absorb all constants into a Hall–Petch constant $k_{HP}$:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2}\;}$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the **friction stress** (lattice resistance + solid solution), and $k_{HP} = 2 \, \sqrt{\tau_c \, G \, b / \pi}$.

**Step 6 — Engineering form using hardness** (via Tabor, $H \approx 3 \, \sigma_y$):

$$H = H_0 + 3 \, k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Range:** Holds for grain sizes from $\sim 1 \, \mu m$ down to $\sim 30$ nm. Below this, the pile-up model breaks down because a pile-up needs at least ~30 dislocations to be meaningful.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverse Hall–Petch (Softening Below ~10–30 nm)

### 3.2.1 Why Strengthening Saturates and Inverts

Three competing mechanisms operate as $d$ decreases:

1. **Dislocation pile-up strengthening** (classical H-P) — scales as $d^{-1/2}$.
2. **Grain-boundary sliding / Coble creep** — scales with grain-boundary area, i.e. $d^{-3}$.
3. **Triple-junction diffusion** — scales as $d^{-4}$.

As $d$ shrinks, mechanism 2 and 3 dominate, leading to softening.

### 3.2.2 Phenomenological Model (Nieh–Wadsworth)

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2} - k' \, d^{-3/2}$$

The third term (negative) represents grain-boundary-mediated softening.

### 3.2.3 Critical Grain Size $d_c$ for Peak Strength

Set $\dfrac{d \sigma_y}{d d} = 0$:

$$-\dfrac{1}{2} k_{HP} \, d^{-3/2} + \dfrac{3}{2} k' \, d^{-5/2} = 0$$

Solving:

$$d_c = \sqrt{3 \, \dfrac{k'}{k_{HP}}}$$

For Cu, $d_c \approx 10$–$15$ nm. For Fe, $d_c \approx 20$ nm. This matches experimentally observed peaks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Grain-Boundary Volume Fraction

Assume spherical grains of diameter $d$ with a grain boundary "shell" of thickness $\delta$ ($\delta \approx 0.5\text{–}1$ nm, typically taken as 1 nm).

**Volume of one grain interior:** $V_{int} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$

**Volume of one grain including shell:** $V_{total} = \dfrac{\pi (d+\delta)^3}{6}$

**Boundary volume fraction per grain:**

$$f_{gb} = \dfrac{V_{total} - V_{int}}{V_{total}} = 1 - \left( \dfrac{d}{d+\delta} \right)^3$$

**Taylor expansion for $\delta \ll d$:**

$$\left( \dfrac{d}{d+\delta} \right)^3 = \left( 1 + \dfrac{\delta}{d} \right)^{-3} \approx 1 - 3 \, \dfrac{\delta}{d} + 6 \left( \dfrac{\delta}{d} \right)^2 - \dots$$

Keeping the first-order term:

$$\boxed{\;f_{gb} \approx \dfrac{3 \, \delta}{d}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Plug-in sanity check:** For $d = 10$ nm, $\delta = 1$ nm $\Rightarrow f_{gb} \approx 3 \times (1/10) = 0.3$ (30%). For $d = 5$ nm, $f_{gb} \approx 0.6$ (60%). Confirms the rule-of-thumb: at $\sim 5$–$10$ nm, more than half the atoms are in the boundary.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Melting Point Depression (Gibbs–Thomson / Pawley Equation)

A nanocrystal has a very high surface-to-volume ratio. Surface atoms are less tightly bound, so the solid is less stable → melts at a lower temperature.

Starting from the equality of chemical potentials of a curved and a flat surface:

$$\mu_{nano}(T) = \mu_{flat}(T) + \dfrac{2 \, \gamma_{sl} \, V_m}{d}$$

At equilibrium, the chemical potential of liquid and solid are equal at $T_m(d)$. Combining with the latent heat of fusion $\Delta H_f = T_m \cdot \Delta S_f$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_m = T_m(\infty) - T_m(d) \approx \dfrac{2 \, \gamma_{sl} \, V_m \, T_m(\infty)}{\Delta H_f \, d}\;}$$

- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy (J/m²).
- $V_m$ = molar volume (m³/mol).
- $\Delta H_f$ = molar enthalpy of fusion (J/mol).

**Numerical example for Au nanoparticles ($d = 5$ nm):**
- $T_m(\infty) = 1337$ K.
- $\gamma_{sl} \approx 0.17$ J/m².
- $V_m \approx 10.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol.
- $\Delta H_f \approx 12.6 \times 10^3$ J/mol.

$$\Delta T_m = \dfrac{2 \times 0.17 \times 10.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1337}{12.6 \times 10^{3} \times 5 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 123 \text{ K}$$

So 5 nm Au nanoparticles melt at $\sim 1214$ K, nearly $120$ K below the bulk value — verified experimentally by Pawley.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Processing Routes

| Criterion | Inert Gas Condensation | Mech. Alloying | ECAP/HPT | Sol–Gel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Class | Bottom-up | Top-down | Top-down | Bottom-up |
| Material type | Metals, alloys | Metals, intermetallics, ceramics | Bulk ductile metals | Oxides, ceramics |
| Typical grain size | 5–50 nm | 5–25 nm | 100–300 nm (UFG) | 10–100 nm |
| Sample size | Small (cm³) | Powder, then consolidate | Bulk billet/disc | Powder, then sinter |
| Purity | Very high | Contamination from balls/PCAs | Very high | Chemical residues |
| Cost / scalability | Low scalability | High scalability | Moderate | High scalability |
| KTU weightage | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Property Changes on Nanocrystallization

| Property | Conventional (Cu) | Nanocrystalline Cu (10 nm) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yield strength | 50 MPa | 400–800 MPa | Hall–Petch |
| Hardness (HV) | 50 | 200–400 | Tabor / H-P |
| Ductility (uniform) | 50% | 1–5% | Limited dislocation storage |
| Electrical resistivity | $1.7 \, \mu\Omega \cdot cm$ | $10\text{–}20 \, \mu\Omega \cdot cm$ | GB scattering |
| Diffusion coefficient (300 K) | $10^{-30}$ m²/s | $10^{-20}$ m²/s | GB diffusion dominates |
| Saturation magnetization | $0.6$ emu/g | $0.55$ emu/g | Slight reduction |
| Coercivity (soft Fe) | low | Can be 10× higher | Domain wall pinning |
| Melting point | 1356 K | 1300 K (5 nm) | Gibbs–Thomson |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Two Strategic Routes to Nanocrystalline Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[NANOCRYSTALLINE MATERIAL PROCESSING ROUTES] --> A1[BOTTOM-UP ASSEMBLY]
    A0 --> A2[TOP-DOWN REFINEMENT]

    A1 --> A11[Inert Gas Condensation plus In-Situ Compaction]
    A1 --> A12[Chemical Vapour Condensation]
    A1 --> A13[Sol Gel Synthesis]
    A1 --> A14[Electrodeposition]
    A1 --> A15[Spray Pyrolysis]

    A11 --> A111[Evaporate metal in He or Ar at 100 Pa]
    A111 --> A112[Atom gas collision gives supersaturation]
    A112 --> A113[Homogeneous nucleation of 5 to 50 nm clusters]
    A113 --> A114[Collection on LN2 cold finger at 77 K]
    A114 --> A115[Scraped into die, warm pressed at 1 to 5 GPa]
    A115 --> A116[Dense nanocrystalline pellet]

    A2 --> A21[Mechanical Alloying or Ball Milling]
    A2 --> A22[Severe Plastic Deformation]
    A2 --> A23[Cryomilling]

    A21 --> A211[Stage 1: Laminate formation by ball impact]
    A211 --> A212[Stage 2: Fracture of brittle phases]
    A212 --> A213[Stage 3: Lamellar refinement to nanometre spacing]
    A213 --> A214[Stage 4: Steady state grain size 5 to 25 nm]

    A22 --> A221[ECAP route Bc, 4 to 8 passes]
    A22 --> A222[HPT under several GPa pressure]
    A221 --> A223[Submicron ultrafine grains 100 to 300 nm]
    A222 --> A224[Sub 100 nm grains in disc form]

    A116 --> OUT[FINAL DENSE NANOCRYSTALLINE BULK]
    A214 --> OUT
    A223 --> OUT
    A224 --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Inert Gas Condensation Apparatus (Gleiter Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[ULTRA HIGH VACUUM CHAMBER BACKFILLED WITH He OR Ar AT 100 Pa] --> P1[EVAPORATION SOURCE: W OR Al2O3 CRUCIBLE]
    P1 --> P2[METAL VAPOUR ATOMS]
    P2 --> P3[COLLISION WITH INERT GAS ATOMS]
    P3 --> P4[SUPERSATURATION AND NUCLEATION]
    P4 --> P5[NANOCLUSTERS 5 TO 50 nm]
    P5 --> P6[CONVECTION BY GAS FLOW]
    P6 --> P7[LN2 COLD FINGER AT 77 K]
    P7 --> P8[LOOSELY BONDED NANOPOWDER LAYER]
    P8 --> P9[SCRAPER BLADE]
    P9 --> P10[COMPACTION DIE]
    P10 --> P11[HYDRAULIC RAM AT 1 TO 5 GPa]
    P11 --> P12[DENSE NANOCRYSTALLINE PELLET]
    P12 --> P13[REMOVAL TO GLOVE BOX WITH LESS THAN 1 ppm O2]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Property Domination Shift with Grain Size

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[STRUCTURE PROPERTY EVOLUTION WITH GRAIN SIZE] --> B1[Coarse Grained d greater than 1 micrometre]
    B0 --> B2[Ultrafine Grained 100 to 1000 nm]
    B0 --> B3[Nanocrystalline 10 to 100 nm]
    B0 --> B4[Cluster Assembled d less than 10 nm]

    B1 --> B11[Classical Hall Petch strengthening]
    B1 --> B12[Low fraction of grain boundaries]
    B1 --> B13[Standard diffusion values]

    B2 --> B21[Moderate Hall Petch strengthening]
    B2 --> B22[Dislocation cell structures]
    B2 --> B23[UFG forming by ECAP or HPT]

    B3 --> B31[Peak strength near Hall Petch maximum]
    B3 --> B32[GB fraction 10 to 50 percent]
    B3 --> B33[Enhanced diffusion by 5 to 10 orders]
    B3 --> B34[Reduced ductility]

    B4 --> B41[Inverse Hall Petch softening]
    B4 --> B42[GB fraction greater than 50 percent]
    B4 --> B43[Triple junction and GB sliding dominate]
    B4 --> B44[Melting point depression by hundreds of K]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hall–Petch vs Inverse Hall–Petch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[STRENGTH VS GRAIN DIAMETER] --> H1[Grain d greater than 1 micrometre: weak baseline]
    H0 --> H2[Grain 30 nm to 1 micrometre: classical H-P strengthening]
    H0 --> H3[Grain 10 to 30 nm: peak strength]
    H0 --> H4[Grain d less than 10 nm: inverse H-P softening]

    H2 --> H21[sigma y rises with d to the power minus 1 divided by 2]
    H3 --> H31[GB sliding and Coble creep begin to compete]
    H4 --> H41[Triple junction diffusion dominates]
    H4 --> H42[Softening by pile up breakdown]

    H21 --> H5[sigma y equals sigma 0 plus k HP times d to the power minus 1 divided by 2]
    H41 --> H6[sigma y equals sigma 0 plus k HP d to the power minus 1 divided by 2 minus k prime times d to the power minus 3 divided by 2]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Define nanocrystalline materials. State the Hall–Petch relation and explain the significance of the exponent $-\tfrac{1}{2}$.**

**Model Answer:**

Nanocrystalline materials are polycrystalline solids with an average grain size in the range 1–100 nm, such that a significant fraction (often $\geq 50\%$) of atoms reside in or near grain boundaries.

The Hall–Petch relation is:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2}$$

where $\sigma_y$ = yield strength, $d$ = grain diameter, $\sigma_0$ = friction stress, $k_{HP}$ = Hall–Petch constant.

**Significance of the $-\tfrac{1}{2}$ exponent:** It arises mathematically from the equilibrium of a dislocation pile-up at a grain boundary. The shear stress at the pile-up tip is proportional to $d \, \tau^2$ (from dislocation mechanics), and on solving for $\tau$ we obtain $d^{-1/2}$. Physically, it indicates that *reducing grain size strengthens the material non-linearly* — halving the grain size increases $\sigma_y$ only by $\sim 41\%$ (since $2^{1/2} = 1.414$), not by $100\%$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Briefly explain the Inert Gas Condensation (IGC) method for producing nanocrystalline materials. Why is in-situ compaction necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

In IGC, a metal is evaporated (by resistive heating, sputtering, or laser) inside a vacuum chamber back-filled with low-pressure inert gas (He/Ar, $\sim 100$ Pa). The vapor atoms undergo collisions with inert gas atoms, lose kinetic energy, become supersaturated, and nucleate into nanoclusters (5–50 nm). These clusters are carried by gas convection to a liquid-nitrogen-cooled cold finger, where they form a loosely bonded powder layer. The powder is then scraped into a compaction die.

**In-situ compaction is necessary because:**
1. Nanopowders have an enormous surface area and would instantly oxidize / adsorb moisture if exposed to ambient air (contamination would ruin properties).
2. Cold-pressed nanopowders have very high friction and cannot densify by ordinary pressing.
3. Warm compaction under vacuum ($\sim 1$–$5$ GPa) consolidates the powder into a fully dense, porosity-free pellet while preserving the nanoscale grain size.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**a)** Describe the **Inert Gas Condensation (IGC)** method for producing nanocrystalline materials with a neat block diagram. Discuss its merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Derive the **Hall–Petch relationship** starting from dislocation pile-up theory. Explain the phenomenon of **inverse Hall–Petch** with a sketch of $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** IGC is a bottom-up physical vapour synthesis route in which a metal is evaporated in an inert gas atmosphere to form nanoclusters that are collected and consolidated in-situ.

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2.

**Step-by-step process (3 marks):**

1. Source metal placed in W or Al₂O₃ crucible inside an ultra-high vacuum chamber.
2. Chamber back-filled with high-purity He (or Ar) at 100 Pa.
3. Metal evaporated by resistive heating → atoms collide with inert gas.
4. Rapid energy loss → supersaturation → homogeneous nucleation of nanoclusters (5–50 nm).
5. Clusters convect to LN₂-cooled cold finger → loose powder deposit.
6. Scraper transfers powder into compaction die.
7. Warm pressing at 1–5 GPa under UHV → fully dense pellet.

**Merits (0.5 mark):**
- High purity (UHV environment).
- Wide choice of metals, alloys, and even some ceramics.
- Excellent grain-size control via evaporation rate and inert gas pressure.

**Demerits (0.5 mark):**
- Low throughput; only small samples (few cm³) possible.
- Not easily scalable to industrial production.
- High capital and operational cost.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):** Follow Section 3.1, Steps 1–5. State the pile-up equilibrium:

$$n \approx \frac{\pi d \, \tau_{applied}}{G b}$$

Tip stress: $\tau_{tip} = n \, \tau_{applied}$. Yield criterion: $\tau_{tip} = \tau_c$ (critical stress in next grain). Solving and converting to tensile:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2}$$

**Inverse Hall–Petch explanation (2 marks):**

For very small grain sizes ($d < 10\text{–}30$ nm), the pile-up model breaks down because there are not enough dislocations to form a meaningful pile-up. Grain-boundary-mediated deformation (Coble creep, GB sliding) becomes easier as $d$ decreases, leading to *softening* with further refinement. The peak strength occurs at a critical size $d_c = \sqrt{3 k'/k_{HP}}$. Plot $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$: a straight line for large $d$ (positive slope $k_{HP}$), reaching a maximum, then *decreasing* for very small $d$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the **mechanical alloying** process for nanocrystalline materials. Describe the microstructural evolution during milling and the role of process control agents. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Discuss the **mechanical, electrical, and magnetic** properties of nanocrystalline materials and compare them with their coarse-grained counterparts. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Mechanical alloying is a solid-state, top-down powder processing technique in which a high-energy ball mill repeatedly deforms, fractures, and re-welds powder particles, ultimately producing a homogeneous, nanocrystalline alloy.

**Microstructural Evolution (4 marks):**

| Stage | Mechanism | Microstructure |
|---|---|---|
| Stage 1 (initial) | Flattening + cold welding | Layered composite of starting powders |
| Stage 2 (intermediate) | Work hardening → fracture | Refined lamellae, oxide dispersion |
| Stage 3 (final) | Steady state | True alloy, random nano-grains (5–25 nm) |

**Process Variables (1 mark):**
- Ball-to-powder ratio (BPR) = 10:1 to 20:1.
- Milling time = 10–100 h.
- Atmosphere: inert (Ar) to avoid oxidation.
- Process Control Agents (PCAs) (1 mark):
  - Used for ductile metals (Al, Cu) to *prevent* excessive cold welding.
  - Reduce surface energy, inhibit agglomeration.
  - Examples: stearic acid, methanol, hexane.
  - Typical amount: 1–5 wt.%.
  - Excess PCA *contaminates* the powder; insufficient PCA → stuck to balls.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Mechanical (2.5 marks):**
- **Strength / Hardness:** Increases dramatically per Hall–Petch. Nanocrystalline Cu ($d=10$ nm) has yield strength $\sim 400$ MPa vs. 50 MPa for coarse Cu (8×).
- **Ductility:** Usually *reduced* uniform elongation (1–5%) because dislocation storage capacity is exhausted.
- **Superplasticity:** Nano/UFG materials exhibit superplastic forming at lower temperatures and higher strain rates than coarse-grained counterparts.
- **Wear resistance:** Significantly improved due to high hardness.

**Electrical (2.5 marks):**
- **Resistivity increases** 5–10× (e.g., Cu: $1.7 \to 10\text{–}20 \, \mu\Omega \cdot cm$).
- Cause: enhanced electron scattering at disordered grain boundaries (Mayadas–Shatzkes model).
- The temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) is suppressed.

**Magnetic (2 marks):**
- **Soft magnetic nanocrystalline alloys** (e.g., Fe–Si–B–Nb–Cu, *Finemet*) have ultra-low coercivity and high permeability, used in transformer cores.
- **Hard magnetic** nanocrystalline Nd–Fe–B shows enhanced remanence via exchange-coupling.
- **Giant Magnetoresistance (GMR):** Discovered in nanolayered magnetic structures; used in read heads.
- Coercivity: in single-domain regime ($\sim 10\text{–}20$ nm for Fe), $H_c$ is maximized.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing IGC and CVC:** IGC is *physical* evaporation; CVC is *chemical* decomposition of a precursor. Marks lost if you interchange them.
> 2. **Hall–Petch exponent:** Some students write $d^{-1}$ or $d^{1/2}$. The correct exponent is exactly $-\tfrac{1}{2}$. One mark deducted for the wrong sign or value.
> 3. **Forgetting in-situ compaction in IGC:** Answer is *incomplete* without it. The IGC process is *defined* by its in-situ consolidation step.
> 4. **No mention of PCAs in Mechanical Alloying:** Examiner deducts at least 1 mark if you only describe milling without explaining *why* ductile metals need PCAs.
> 5. **Inverse Hall–Petch domain:** Students often *only* write the strengthening equation and miss the *softening* regime — a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PEPET751 Module 3**

- ✅ **Definition:** Nanocrystalline = grain size **1–100 nm**; $f_{gb} \geq 50\%$ when $d \leq 10$ nm.
- ✅ **Two routes:** Bottom-up (IGC, CVC, sol–gel) vs. Top-down (MA, ECAP, HPT).
- ✅ **IGC =** Evaporate metal in inert gas (100 Pa) → cluster nucleation on LN₂ cold finger → in-situ warm compaction at 1–5 GPa. **Must be in-situ** to avoid contamination.
- ✅ **CVC =** Chemical decomposition of a precursor; used for ceramics, nitrides, oxides.
- ✅ **Mechanical Alloying =** Ball milling; 4 stages: laminate → refine → randomize → steady state; saturation grain size 5–25 nm.
- ✅ **Process Control Agents (PCAs) =** Prevent cold welding of ductile metals; 1–5 wt.% stearic acid/methanol.
- ✅ **ECAP =** Equal channel angular pressing; route $B_C$ most efficient; introduces $\varepsilon \sim 0.6$–$1.0$ per pass.
- ✅ **HPT =** High-pressure torsion; smallest grains achievable; disc geometry.
- ✅ **Hall–Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP} \, d^{-1/2}$. Exponent $-\tfrac{1}{2}$ comes from pile-up equilibrium.
- ✅ **Validity:** Holds for $d \geq \sim 30$ nm. Below this, **inverse Hall–Petch** softening due to GB sliding, Coble creep.
- ✅ **Critical grain size:** $d_c = \sqrt{3 k'/k_{HP}}$; $\sim 10$ nm for Cu, $\sim 20$ nm for Fe.
- ✅ **Hardness link:** $H \approx 3 \sigma_y$ (Tabor).
- ✅ **GB volume fraction:** $f_{gb} \approx 3\delta/d$, where $\delta \approx 1$ nm.
- ✅ **Diffusion:** GB diffusion is $10^5$–$10^{10}$× faster than lattice diffusion at low temperatures.
- ✅ **Melting point depression:** $\Delta T_m \approx \dfrac{2 \gamma_{sl} V_m T_m(\infty)}{\Delta H_f d}$ (Gibbs–Thomson). 5 nm Au melts $\sim 120$ K below bulk.
- ✅ **Mechanical properties:** ↑ Strength, ↑ Hardness, ↓ Uniform Ductility, ↑ Wear resistance, ↑ Superplastic formability.
- ✅ **Electrical properties:** ↑ Resistivity (5–10×) due to GB electron scattering.
- ✅ **Magnetic properties:** Soft magnetic *Finemet* (Fe-Si-B-Nb-Cu); GMR in multilayers; exchange-coupled hard magnets; peak $H_c$ near single-domain size.
- ✅ **Optical:** Surface plasmon resonance in metal nanoparticles; quantum confinement in semiconductors.
- ✅ **Tabor relation:** $H \approx 3 \sigma_y$ — link hardness to yield.
- ✅ **Three most-asked KTU sub-topics (high yield):**
  1. IGC apparatus and process — *guaranteed 7-mark question*.
  2. Hall–Petch derivation + Inverse Hall–Petch — *guaranteed 7-mark question*.
  3. Comparison: mechanical, electrical, magnetic properties — *common 7-mark essay question*.
- ✅ **Key constants to memorize:** Avogadro $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$; Boltzmann $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; $\delta \approx 1$ nm (typical GB thickness).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin films and multilayered coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thin films and multilayered coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **thin film** is a layer of material ranging from fractions of a nanometer to several micrometres in thickness, deposited onto a substrate (the base material) using controlled physical or chemical vapour deposition processes. When two or more such films of different materials are stacked in a deliberate sequence — with individual layer thicknesses ranging from nanometres to micrometres — the resulting architecture is termed a **multilayered coating** (or **multilayer coating**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** A *thin film* refers to a 2-D material deposit with thickness $<10\,\mu m$ where the surface-to-volume ratio is so high that surface and interface effects dominate bulk behaviour. A *multilayered coating* is a 1-D periodic (or aperiodic) stack of such films whose properties are engineered through layer composition, thickness, and interface design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Painted Wall vs. The Laminated Steel Plate"

Imagine painting a wall with a single colour of paint — that is a **monolayer (single) thin film**. It does one job: protect the wall from moisture.

Now imagine a high-end industrial cutting tool, like the drill bit used to machine a turbine blade out of Inconel. A single layer of paint (TiN) would not survive 900 °C and enormous shear stresses. Instead, engineers stack dozens of ultra-thin alternating layers — like a **plywood sheet** — where each "ply" (a few nanometres of TiN, AlTiN, CrN, or SiC) plays a different role: oxidation resistance, hardness, crack deflection, and thermal barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** The same principle is used in:
> - **Aluminium beverage cans** – a polymer-epoxy inner layer + Al + outer print coat.
> - **Aircraft turbine blades** – thermal barrier coatings (TBC) with bond coat + TGO + YSZ top coat.
> - **Anti-reflective camera lenses** – alternating high/low refractive index multilayer stacks (Bragg reflectors).
> - **PVD-coated drills** – nanolayered TiAlN/CrN for dry machining.

### 1.3 Why Thin Films Matter in Engineering

| Performance Lever | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Hardness ↑** | Hall–Petch strengthening at nm scale + interface blocking of dislocation glide |
| **Oxidation resistance ↑** | Dense, columnar-free microstructure + oxide-former additives (Al, Cr, Si) |
| **Tribology (low friction)** | Solid lubricant phases (DLC, MoS₂, WS₂) at the sliding contact |
| **Thermal barrier** | Low thermal conductivity ceramics (YSZ, Gd₂Zr₂O₇) in TBC stacks |
| **Optical function** | Bragg interference in multilayer dielectric stacks |
| **Corrosion protection** | Inert ceramic barrier + sacrificial under-layer (e.g., Cr under DLC) |

Standard metrics frequently used in this module:

- **Deposition rate:** typically **0.1 – 10 nm/s**
- **Working pressure (PVD):** **0.1 – 10 Pa**
- **Substrate temperature:** **room temperature to 800 °C**
- **Layer thickness in multilayer:** **1 nm – 1 µm** (period $\Lambda$)
- **Hardness range of modern coatings:** **20 – 50 GPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Deposition Processes

Thin-film deposition is broadly classified into two families:

- **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD):** Material is physically vaporised (atoms knocked off or thermally evaporated) and condensed on the substrate. No chemical reaction occurs in the gas phase.
  - **Thermal Evaporation** (resistive / electron-beam)
  - **Sputtering** (DC, RF, magnetron, reactive, ion-beam)
  - **Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD)**
  - **Cathodic Arc Evaporation**
  - **Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)**
- **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD):** Precursor gases react chemically on/near a hot substrate to form the solid film.
  - **Thermal CVD, LPCVD, PECVD, MOCVD, ALD**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Distinction:**
> - *PVD* — lower temperature, line-of-sight, good for metallic/ceramic coatings on tools.
> - *CVD* — higher temperature, conformal coverage, used for semiconductor and diffusion coatings.
> - *ALD* — atomic-layer precision, self-limiting surface reactions, used in sub-nm multilayer stacks.

### 2.2 Stages of Thin-Film Growth (The "Adatom Lifecycle")

When a vaporised atom (adatom) arrives at the substrate, it goes through five stages:

1. **Adsorption** — atom sticks to surface (physisorption then chemisorption).
2. **Surface diffusion** — atom migrates to find a low-energy site.
3. **Nucleation** — critical-sized cluster (nucleus) is formed.
4. **Growth & coalescence** — nuclei merge into islands, then continuous film.
5. **Thickening** — bulk-like film forms.

> [!NOTE]
> The **nucleation and growth mode** determines the final microstructure (columnar, equiaxed, amorphous) and is therefore the single most important determinant of coating quality.

### 2.3 The Three Classical Growth Modes

| Mode | Description | Condition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frank–van der Merwe (FM)** | Layer-by-layer (2-D) growth | $\gamma_s \ge \gamma_f + \gamma_i$ | GaAs on GaAs (epitaxy) |
| **Volmer–Weber (VW)** | Island (3-D) growth | $\gamma_s < \gamma_f + \gamma_i$ | Au on NaCl, metals on oxides |
| **Stranski–Krastanov (SK)** | Layer + island hybrid | After 1–2 monolayers, strain energy drives 3-D | Ge on Si, InGaAs on GaAs |

where:
- $\gamma_s$ = substrate surface energy
- $\gamma_f$ = film surface energy
- $\gamma_i$ = film/substrate interfacial energy

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise this inequality for the exam:** "If the substrate 'wants' the film, FM happens. If the film 'prefers' itself, VW happens. SK is the compromise when strain builds up."

### 2.4 Multilayered Coating Architectures

Modern coatings are designed in four architectural classes:

- **Monolithic (single layer):** e.g., TiN — simple, cheap, baseline.
- **Multilayer (periodic):** e.g., TiN/AlTiN × 500 periods — alternating composition, hard + tough.
- **Gradient (compositionally graded):** e.g., Ti → TiN → TiCN → TiAlN — composition smoothly changes.
- **Superlattice (nm-period, coherent):** e.g., TiN/NbN with $\Lambda \approx 5\text{–}10$ nm — interfaces are coherent, leading to anomalous hardness.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX-rendered formulas with units** — *No vertical pipes* (use `\vert` or `\mid` where needed).

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle S = \frac{N_{sput}}{N_{ion}}$ | **Sputtering yield** — atoms ejected per incident ion. $S = 0.5\text{–}20$ for metals under Ar⁺. |
| 2 | $\displaystyle R_d = \frac{h \, \rho \, N_A}{M}$ (for evaporation) | **Deposition rate** in monolayers/s. |
| 3 | $\displaystyle m \lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$ | **Bragg's law** for multilayer X-ray diffraction (period $d$ or $\Lambda$ measured). |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, t_f \, (1-\nu_s)} \left( \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0} \right)$ | **Stoney equation** for residual stress in a coated wafer. |
| 5 | $\displaystyle H_{HL} = H_0 + \frac{k_{HP}}{\sqrt{\Lambda}}$ | **Hall–Petch-like hardening** in nanolayered multilayers. $\Lambda$ = bilayer period. |
| 6 | $\displaystyle R_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(z_i - \bar{z})^2}$ | **Surface roughness** (RMS) — critical for optical and tribological quality. |
| 7 | $\displaystyle T = \frac{T_0}{1 + \alpha \Lambda}$ | **Thermal conductivity reduction** in nanolayered TBCs. |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \gamma_{tot} = \gamma_s - \gamma_f - \gamma_i$ | **Wetting parameter** — sign dictates FM, VW, or SK growth. |
| 9 | $\displaystyle Y_s = \frac{4 \pi \gamma}{\lambda}$ | **Young's relation** — capillary length for nucleation. |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^3}{3 (\Delta G_v)^2}$ | **Critical nucleation barrier** (homogeneous). |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cutting tools (CNC inserts):** TiAlN/CrN nanolayered coating increases tool life by 3–10× in dry machining of hardened steels.
- **Medical implants (hip joints):** DLC-on-Ti-6Al-4V multilayer reduces wear debris, extending implant life beyond 20 years.
- **Semiconductors:** ALD-deposited HfO₂/SiO₂ gate stacks are the heart of every modern CMOS transistor.
- **Aerospace TBCs:** YSZ/NiCoCrAlY 8-layer system allows turbine inlet temperatures of **1600 °C**, well above the superalloy melting point of **1300 °C**.
- **Optical coatings:** 12-layer MgF₂/ZnS anti-reflective stack gives $<0.5\%$ reflectance over visible spectrum (used in camera lenses).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sputtering Yield (Kinematic Model)

The simplest analytical model for sputtering is the **momentum-transfer (binary collision) model**, treating the incoming ion of mass $M_i$ striking a surface atom of mass $M_a$.

> **Goal:** Show that maximum energy transfer occurs for a head-on elastic collision and derive the sputtering yield scaling.

**Step 1 — Maximum fractional energy transfer in elastic collision.**

From energy-momentum conservation in a 1-D head-on elastic collision, the fraction of kinetic energy transferred to the target atom is:

$$
T_{max} = \frac{4 M_i M_a}{(M_i + M_a)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.** This is maximum (= 1) when $M_i = M_a$ (Ar⁺ on Cu, for instance, is nearly ideal). For Ar⁺ ($M_i = 40$) on W ($M_a = 184$):

$$
T_{max} = \frac{4 \cdot 40 \cdot 184}{(40 + 184)^2} = \frac{29440}{50176} \approx 0.587
$$

So roughly 59% of the ion's kinetic energy is transferred per head-on collision — the rest is carried away by the reflected ion.

**Step 3 — Sputtering yield (simplified Sigmund formula).**

Sigmund's linear-cascade model gives:

$$
S = \frac{0.042 \, \alpha \, S_n(E)}{U_s}
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ is a dimensionless function of $M_a / M_i$ ($\approx 0.15$–$0.4$),
- $S_n(E)$ is the **nuclear stopping cross-section** (energy deposited into atomic motion),
- $U_s$ is the **surface binding energy** (≈ sublimation enthalpy per atom).

**Step 4 — Typical numbers.** For 500 eV Ar⁺ on Cu: $S \approx 2.5$; for 1 keV Ar⁺ on W: $S \approx 0.6$. Heavier ions (Xe⁺) and heavier targets (Au) raise the yield significantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** If a numerical problem gives you ion energy, target material, and asks for *which gas is most efficient*, compute $T_{max}$ for the candidate gases and pick the one closest to $M_i = M_a$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Stoney Equation for Residual Stress

A thin film deposited on a thick substrate develops in-plane residual stress $\sigma_f$. The substrate curls to a radius $R$. Stoney (1909) showed that

**Step 1 — Force balance.** The film exerts a force per unit width $F = \sigma_f \, t_f$. This is balanced by the bending moment in the substrate:

$$
M = \frac{E_s \, t_s^3}{12(1 - \nu_s)} \, \kappa
$$

where $\kappa = 1/R$ is the curvature.

**Step 2 — Equating moments.** Setting $F \cdot t_s / 2 = M$ and recognising the biaxial modulus $M_s = E_s / (1-\nu_s)$:

$$
\sigma_f \, t_f \, \frac{t_s}{2} = \frac{M_s \, t_s^3}{12} \, \kappa
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $\sigma_f$:**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{M_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, t_f} \, \kappa
$$

Writing the curvature in terms of measured deflection $\delta$ across a scan length $L$, $\kappa \approx 8\delta / L^2$ for a rectangular wafer:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, t_f \, (1 - \nu_s)} \left( \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0} \right)
$$

where $R_0$ is the pre-deposition radius (subtracts substrate native curvature).

**Step 4 — Typical values.** For a 1 µm TiN film on a 500 µm Si wafer with $R = 5$ m, $\sigma_f \approx 0.4$ GPa (compressive), which is consistent with peening-mode compressive stress in sputtered nitride coatings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Validity limits** (often asked in viva):
> - $t_f \ll t_s$ (typically $< 1\%$; otherwise the thin-film approximation breaks down).
> - Elastic, isotropic, homogeneous substrate.
> - Uniform film stress, no stress gradient through thickness.
> - Bending dominates over stretching (small slopes).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hall–Petch Strengthening in Nanolayered Coatings

For a polycrystalline material, hardness scales as

$$
H = H_0 + \frac{k_{HP}}{\sqrt{D}}
$$

where $D$ is the average grain diameter and $k_{HP}$ is the Hall–Petch coefficient.

**Step 1 — Translate to multilayer geometry.** In a nanolayered coating with bilayer period $\Lambda$, dislocation motion requires gliding across the interface. The critical resolved shear stress to bow a dislocation between two pinning points a distance $\Lambda$ apart is

$$
\tau = \frac{G \, b}{\Lambda}
$$

where $G$ is the shear modulus and $b$ the Burgers vector.

**Step 2 — Convert shear strength to hardness** via the Tabor relation $H \approx 3 \sigma_y$, and $\sigma_y = M \tau$ (Taylor factor $M \approx 3$):

$$
H_{HL} = H_0 + \frac{3 \, M \, G \, b}{\Lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine both effects (grain + interface):**

$$
H_{HL} = H_0 + \frac{k_{HP}}{\sqrt{D}} + \frac{3 \, M \, G \, b}{\Lambda}
$$

For the very small $\Lambda$ values in superlattices ($\Lambda \approx 5$ nm), the third term dominates and explains the "anomalous hardness" peak observed experimentally (e.g., TiN/NbN peaks near 50 GPa at $\Lambda \approx 5$ nm).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PVD vs. CVD vs. ALD

| Property | PVD (Sputtering) | CVD (Thermal) | ALD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 25 – 500 °C | 600 – 1100 °C | 50 – 400 °C |
| Pressure | 0.1 – 10 Pa | 100 – 100 000 Pa | 10 – 1000 Pa |
| Conformality on trenches | Poor (line-of-sight) | Good | Excellent (100%) |
| Growth rate | nm/s | nm/s – µm/s | Å/cycle |
| Thickness control | Quartz monitor | Time + flow | Self-limiting cycles |
| Best for | Tool coatings, optics | Diffusion layers, SiC | Sub-nm multilayers, MOSFETs |
| Capital cost | Low – Medium | High | Very high |
| Multilayer capability | Decent (rotating fixtures) | Limited | Excellent (digital recipe) |

> [!TIP]
> If the KTU question asks *"which process can give sub-nanometre control for a 50-period HfO₂/Al₂O₃ gate stack?"* — the unambiguous answer is **ALD**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Thin-Film Deposition System (Magnetron Sputtering)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vacuum Chamber 1e-3 to 10 Pa] --> B[Turbo Molecular Pump]
    A --> C[Ar Gas Inlet Mass Flow Controller]
    A --> D[Magnetron Cathode Target e.g. Ti 99.95 percent]
    D --> E[Plasma Glow Discharge]
    E --> F[Ar Ions Bombard Target]
    F --> G[Sputtered Atoms Travel to Substrate]
    G --> H[Substrate Holder Biased 0 to 100 V]
    H --> I[Heater 25 to 800 degree C]
    I --> J[Thin Film Nucleation and Growth]
    J --> K[Multilayer Stack via Substrate Rotation or Shutter Control]
    K --> L[In situ Thickness Monitor Quartz Crystal Microbalance]
    L --> M[Final Coating on Tool or Wafer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The substrate rotates under two or more cathodes (e.g., Cr and Al–Ti) in **reactive mode with N₂** to deposit alternating CrN and TiAlN nanolayers. The **bias voltage** controls ion bombardment energy (drives film density), the **heater** controls adatom mobility (drives crystalline quality), and the **QCM** provides real-time rate feedback.

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Three Classical Growth Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Adatom Arrives] --> B{Surface Energy Balance}
    B -->|gamma_s greater than gamma_f plus gamma_i| C[Frank van der Merwe Layer by Layer 2D]
    B -->|gamma_s less than gamma_f plus gamma_i| D[Volmer Weber Island 3D]
    B -->|Initial wetting then strain relief| E[Stranski Krastanov Hybrid]
    C --> F[Atomically Smooth Film]
    D --> G[Polycrystalline Columnar Film]
    E --> H[Quantum Dots on a Wetting Layer]
```

### 4.3 Multilayer Coating Architecture (Cross-Sectional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stack[Multilayer Coating Architecture]
        direction TB
        L1[Layer 1 Substrate Ti6Al4V 500 um] --> L2[Layer 2 Bond Coat Cr 0.5 um]
        L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Transition CrN 0.2 um]
        L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Main Coating TiAlN 3 um]
        L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Nanolayered Stack 500 periods of TiAlN and CrN each 5 nm]
        L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Top Functional DLC 0.1 um low friction]
    end
    style Stack fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#003366
```

### 4.4 Process-Selection Decision Tree for Coating Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Need conformal coverage on 3D parts?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Temperature sensitive substrate?}
    Q1 -->|No| P1[Use Line of Sight PVD Sputtering or Arc]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Need sub nm thickness control?}
    Q2 -->|No| P2[Use Thermal CVD 700 to 1000 C]
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Use ALD Atomic Layer Deposition]
    Q3 -->|No| P4[Use PECVD Plasma Enhanced 200 to 400 C]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU board answers:** Always mention **at least two limitations** of the chosen process. For example, "Sputtering is chosen because it gives dense, low-defect nitride films at 400 °C, *but* it has poor step coverage on high-aspect-ratio features and suffers from target poisoning in reactive mode with N₂."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**  
Define a *thin film* and a *multilayered coating*. Give two engineering applications of each.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - *Thin film* — A material layer of thickness ranging from a few nanometres to a few micrometres, deposited on a substrate by PVD or CVD, whose properties are dominated by surface and interface effects. (1 mark)
> - *Multilayered coating* — A stack of two or more alternating thin films, designed to combine the advantages of each material (e.g., hard + tough + lubricious). (1 mark)
> - *Applications:* (i) TiN single-layer coating on HSS drills to increase tool life; (ii) AlTiN/CrN nanolayered coating on inserts for dry machining of Inconel; (iii) MgF₂/ZnS multilayer on camera lenses for anti-reflection; (iv) DLC/SiC multilayer on prosthetic implants. (1 mark for any two correct)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Distinguish between **Frank–van der Merwe**, **Volmer–Weber**, and **Stranski–Krastanov** growth modes with the help of the surface energy inequality.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The deciding inequality is $\gamma_s$ vs. $(\gamma_f + \gamma_i)$:
> - **FM (layer-by-layer, 2-D):** $\gamma_s \ge \gamma_f + \gamma_i$ — substrate wets; the film prefers to cover the substrate. (1 mark)
> - **VW (island, 3-D):** $\gamma_s < \gamma_f + \gamma_i$ — film atoms prefer each other to the substrate; 3-D nuclei form. (1 mark)
> - **SK (layer + island):** Initial 2-D wetting for 1–2 monolayers, then 3-D islands once strain energy exceeds wetting energy. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Describe the **magnetron sputtering process** for depositing a TiAlN/CrN nanolayered coating on a high-speed-steel cutting insert. Include a labelled schematic of the system, the role of the magnetron, the purpose of substrate bias, and the typical process parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Hall–Petch strengthening mechanism** that gives nanolayered coatings their "anomalous hardness". A TiN/NbN superlattice with bilayer period $\Lambda = 6$ nm shows hardness of 48 GPa. The single-layer TiN hardness is 22 GPa and the Hall–Petch coefficient for the system is $k_{HP} = 12$ GPa·nm$^{1/2}$. Estimate the effective grain size $D$ using $H = H_0 + k_{HP}/\sqrt{D}$. Comment on the role of coherent interfaces. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Process description (2 marks):** Magnetron sputtering uses a low-pressure Ar plasma (0.3–1 Pa) ignited between a Ti–Al alloy target (or co-sputtered Ti + Al targets) and the substrate. Ar⁺ ions are accelerated onto the target, ejecting (sputtering) Ti and Al atoms. Reactive N₂ gas is introduced to form TiAlN *in situ*. For CrN layers, the substrate is rotated under a second Cr cathode.
- **Role of magnetron (1 mark):** Magnets behind the target trap secondary electrons in a closed drift path, increasing plasma density by ~100× and allowing high deposition rates (~1 nm/s) at low pressure.
- **Substrate bias (1.5 marks):** A negative DC or pulsed bias of –40 to –100 V accelerates Ar⁺ ions towards the growing film. This "ion bombardment" peens the film, producing compressive stress, denser microstructure, and preferred (200) orientation. Too high a bias (>–150 V) causes re-sputtering and defects.
- **Process parameters (1 mark):** Power 5–10 kW, bias –60 V, T_sub = 450 °C, N₂/Ar ratio = 1:4, base pressure < 10⁻⁴ Pa, working pressure 0.5 Pa, substrate rotation 10 rpm giving ~5 nm bilayer period.
- **Labelled schematic (1.5 marks):** See SECTION 4.1 diagram — assign 0.5 marks each for target, plasma, biased substrate, and QCM.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Starting with the Hall–Petch relation

$$
H = H_0 + \frac{k_{HP}}{\sqrt{D}}
$$

Substituting the given numbers:

$$
48 \text{ GPa} = 22 \text{ GPa} + \frac{12 \text{ GPa}\cdot\text{nm}^{1/2}}{\sqrt{D}}
$$

$$
\frac{12}{\sqrt{D}} = 26 \implies \sqrt{D} = \frac{12}{26} \text{ nm}^{1/2} = 0.4615 \text{ nm}^{1/2}
$$

$$
D = 0.213 \text{ nm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{D \approx 0.21 \text{ nm}}
$$

**Discussion of coherent interfaces (3 marks):**
- At $\Lambda = 6$ nm, the TiN and NbN layers (both rock-salt, lattice mismatch ≈ 0.6%) grow **coherently** — atoms at the interface are shared. (1 mark)
- Dislocations cannot easily glide through coherent interfaces because the shear modulus and Burgers vector differ across the interface. The interfaces act as **barriers** that force dislocations to bow or cross-slip. (1 mark)
- Additionally, the alternating stress fields of the coherent layers create a **superlattice strain hardening** that peaks when the layer thickness is below ~10 nm — beyond this, dislocations pile up at interfaces and the hardness saturates or drops. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - **Unit error:** Most students forget that $k_{HP}$ is in **GPa·nm$^{1/2}$**, so $\sqrt{D}$ must come out in **nm$^{1/2}$**. Mixing SI and CGS loses 1 mark.
> - **Domain error:** $D$ should come out as nm² only if both terms are in GPa. If you computed $D = 0.21$ (forgetting units of $\sqrt{D}$), you'll be penalised.
> - **Forgetting the physical meaning:** Always add 1–2 lines interpreting the answer. Saying "$D$ is very small" alone is not enough — say "$D < 1$ nm, indicating single-crystal-like, dislocation-starved grains within each layer, consistent with a superlattice."

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare **PVD, CVD, and ALD** as thin-film deposition techniques. Use a tabular format covering deposition temperature, growth rate, conformality, and typical applications. Justify which technique is best suited for depositing a 50-period HfO₂/Al₂O₃ gate stack with sub-nm thickness precision. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2 µm thick TiN film is sputter-deposited on a 525 µm thick (100) Si wafer. The wafer curvature changes from $R_0 = \infty$ (flat) to $R = 9$ m (concave) after deposition. Given $E_s = 130$ GPa and $\nu_s = 0.28$, calculate the **residual stress** in the film using the **Stoney equation**. State whether the stress is tensile or compressive. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** (See the comparison table in SECTION 3.4 for the structure; 4 marks for the table covering all 4 columns, 2 marks for justification, 1 mark for the correct conclusion.)  
**Conclusion:** **ALD** is the only process that can deliver sub-nm precision with 100 % step coverage — its self-limiting surface chemistry gives digital, cycle-by-cycle thickness control ideal for HfO₂/Al₂O₃ gate stacks in advanced CMOS.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Stoney's equation (biaxial form):

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, t_f \, (1 - \nu_s)} \left( \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R_0} \right)
$$

Substituting values (with $R_0 = \infty$ so $1/R_0 = 0$):

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{130 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa} \cdot (525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1 - 0.28)} \cdot \frac{1}{9}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the denominator term:**

$$
6 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 0.72 = 8.64 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared thickness:**

$$
(525 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.756 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{130 \times 10^9 \cdot 2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{8.64 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9}
$$

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{3.583 \times 10^{4}}{7.776 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.608 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_f \approx 0.46 \, \text{GPa, compressive}}
$$

**Sign convention (1 mark):** The wafer became **concave** (film on the inside of the curve), which means the film is trying to *contract* relative to the substrate — i.e., it is in a **compressive** state. This is consistent with the energetic Ar⁺ ion peening typical of sputtered TiN.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):**
> - **Forgetting $(1-\nu_s)$:** A frequent error. The biaxial modulus is $E/(1-\nu)$, not $E$. Loses 1 mark.
> - **Not stating the sign:** Always write *"compressive"* or *"tensile"* explicitly. A numerical answer without sign interpretation loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Unit slip:** If you write $1/R$ in mm⁻¹ instead of m⁻¹, the answer will be off by a factor of 1000. Always normalise to SI.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thin film** = sub-10 µm deposit; **multilayer** = 1-D periodic/aperiodic stack of films.
- Three growth modes: **FM (layer-by-layer)**, **VW (island)**, **SK (hybrid)** — governed by the **wetting inequality** $\gamma_s \gtrless (\gamma_f + \gamma_i)$.
- PVD = line-of-sight, low T; CVD = conformal, high T; **ALD = sub-nm digital control** (best for nanolayered stacks).
- **Sputtering yield $S$** peaks when $M_i \approx M_a$; Ar⁺ is a near-universal choice.
- **Stoney's equation** gives residual stress from wafer curvature — requires $t_f \ll t_s$.
- **Nanolayer hardening** = Hall–Petch + interface blocking of dislocations; peaks near $\Lambda = 5$ nm in coherent superlattices.
- **Four architectures:** monolithic, multilayer, gradient, superlattice.
- **Superlattices** (e.g., TiN/NbN) are *coherent*; multilayers (e.g., TiN/CrN) may have incoherent interfaces.
- Always state **at least one limitation** of any process you recommend.
- Memorise the **sputtering yield formula**, **Stoney equation**, **Hall–Petch equation**, and **growth-mode inequality** — these appear in nearly every KTU board paper on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single walled and multiwalled carbon nanotubes: methods of synthesis of nanomaterials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET751 - ADVANCED MATERIALS AND PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single walled and multiwalled carbon nanotubes: methods of synthesis of nanomaterials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNTs)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **Single-Walled Carbon Nanotube (SWCNT)** is a one-dimensional (1D) allotrope of carbon consisting of a single cylindrical graphitic sheet (graphene) seamlessly rolled into a hollow tube with a typical diameter of **0.4 nm to 3 nm** and a length ranging from **hundreds of nanometers to several micrometers**.
