The ends of the cylinder are capped with hemispherical fullerene-like structures (pentagonal rings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A SWCNT can be conceptually visualized as a single sheet of graphene (a 2D hexagonal lattice of sp² hybridized carbon atoms) that has been rolled into a seamless cylinder along a specific direction. The way it is rolled determines its electronic properties (metallic or semiconducting).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine taking a single sheet of hexagonal **chicken-wire fencing** (the graphene sheet) and carefully rolling it up like a piece of paper to form a thin straw. That straw is your SWCNT. Each intersection of the wires is a carbon atom, and the wires represent the strong covalent C–C bonds. Now, if you take many of these straws and nest them concentrically inside one another (like Russian nesting dolls, *Matryoshka*), you get a Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotube.

---

### 1.2 Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotube (MWCNT)** comprises **two or more concentric cylindrical graphene sheets** coaxially arranged around a common central hollow core with an interlayer spacing of approximately **0.34 nm** (similar to the d-spacing in turbostratic graphite). The outer diameter typically ranges from **2 nm to 100 nm**, while the inner diameter ranges from **0.4 nm to a few nanometers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** MWCNTs can exist in two structural models:
> 1. **Russian Doll Model** – concentric cylinders with closed ends.
> 2. **Parchment Model** – a single graphene sheet rolled multiple times around itself, resembling a rolled-up scroll.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A MWCNT is like a **telescopic antenna** or a set of **concentric drinking straws** placed one inside the other. The space between each straw is just **0.34 nm** — about the width of three hydrogen atoms lined up.

---

### 1.3 Comparison Table: SWCNT vs MWCNT

| Property | SWCNT | MWCNT |
|---|---|---|
| Number of walls | **1** | **2 to 50+** |
| Typical diameter | 0.4 – 3 nm | 2 – 100 nm |
| Synthesis cost | High | Relatively low |
| Defects | Fewer | More (often) |
| Electronic behavior | Depends on chirality (metallic or semiconducting) | Predominantly metallic (due to outer wall dominance) |
| Young's modulus | ~1 TPa | ~0.3 – 1 TPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant (KTU Exam Favorite):** The carbon–carbon bond length in graphene is **a₀ ≈ 0.142 nm (1.42 Å)**, and the interlayer spacing in MWCNTs is **d ≈ 0.34 nm (3.4 Å)**.

---

### 1.4 Methods of Synthesis of Nanomaterials — Overview

Synthesis of carbon nanotubes falls under the broader category of **"bottom-up"** nanomaterial fabrication approaches, where atoms/molecules are assembled into larger nanostructures. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the following **principal methods**:

1. **Arc Discharge Method**
2. **Laser Ablation (Laser Vaporization) Method**
3. **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** — including Plasma-Enhanced CVD (PECVD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification (Recall for KTU):**
> - **Physical methods:** Arc discharge, Laser ablation
> - **Chemical methods:** CVD, PECVD
> - **Other advanced methods:** Flame synthesis, pyrolysis, electrospinning (for nanofibers)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chiral Vector (Ch) — The Heart of CNT Geometry

The geometry of a SWCNT is uniquely defined by the **chiral vector (Ch)**, which is constructed in the hexagonal graphene lattice using two basis vectors **a₁** and **a₂** of equal length |a₁| = |a₂| = **a₀√3**, where **a₀ = 0.142 nm** is the C–C bond length.

$$\vec{C}_h = n \vec{a}_1 + m \vec{a}_2$$

where **n** and **m** are non-negative integers called the **chiral indices**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Classical CNT Types (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Armchair (n, n):** e.g., (5,5), (10,10) → Always **metallic**.
> - **Zigzag (n, 0):** e.g., (10,0), (15,0) → Metallic if n is multiple of 3, else semiconducting.
> - **Chiral (n, m)** with n ≠ m ≠ 0 → "Twisted" helicity.

### 2.2 The Chiral Angle (θ)

The chiral angle θ is the angle between the chiral vector Ch and the zigzag direction (a₁):

$$\cos\theta = \frac{n + m/2}{\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}$$

- θ = **0°** → Zigzag
- θ = **30°** → Armchair
- 0° < θ < 30° → Chiral

### 2.3 The Tube Diameter (d_t)

The diameter of a SWCNT is computed by dividing the length of the chiral vector by π:

$$d_t = \frac{|\vec{C}_h|}{\pi} = \frac{a_0 \sqrt{3(n^2 + nm + m^2)}}{\pi}$$

### 2.4 Number of Walls in MWCNTs

For a MWCNT with outer diameter **D_out** and inner diameter **D_in**, the number of concentric walls (N) is:

$$N = 1 + \frac{D_{out} - D_{in}}{2 \cdot d_{interlayer}}$$

where **d_interlayer = 0.34 nm**.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> If D_out = 10 nm, D_in = 2 nm:
> N = 1 + (10 − 2)/(2 × 0.34) = 1 + 11.76 ≈ **13 walls**.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Chiral vector | $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$ | n, m are integers |
| Diameter of SWCNT | $d_t = \dfrac{a_0\sqrt{3(n^2+nm+m^2)}}{\pi}$ | a₀ = 0.142 nm; d_t in nm |
| Chiral angle | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{n + m/2}{\sqrt{n^2+nm+m^2}}$ | 0° ≤ θ ≤ 30° |
| C–C bond length | a₀ = 0.142 nm | **1.42 Å** |
| Interlayer spacing (MWCNT) | d = 0.34 nm | **3.4 Å** |
| Number of walls (MWCNT) | $N = 1 + \dfrac{D_{out}-D_{in}}{2 \cdot 0.34}$ | Result rounded to nearest integer |
| Aspect ratio | L/d_t | For SWCNT: typically 1000–10,000 |
| Metallic SWCNT condition | n − m = 3k (k = integer) | When n ≠ m; armchair (n,n) always metallic |
| Semiconducting SWCNT | n − m ≠ 3k | Band gap $\approx \gamma_0 a_0 / d_t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The relationship $E_g \approx \gamma_0 a_0 / d_t$ (where $\gamma_0 \approx 2.7$ eV is the C–C tight-binding overlap energy) shows that **smaller-diameter semiconducting CNTs have larger bandgaps** — a critical design rule in CNT-based nanoelectronics and FETs.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** MWCNTs as structural reinforcement in polymer composites (Boeing 787 uses CNT-reinforced composites).
- **Energy:** SWCNT films as transparent conductive electrodes replacing ITO in solar cells.
- **Biomedicine:** Functionalized CNTs as drug-delivery vectors (high aspect ratio aids cellular uptake).
- **Electronics:** Semiconducting SWCNTs as the channel material in sub-7 nm CMOS transistors.
- **Sensors:** CNT chemiresistors for gas detection (NO₂, NH₃) — single-molecule sensitivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Chiral Vector in the Graphene Lattice

**Step 1 — Set up the hexagonal lattice.**
In the graphene honeycomb, the primitive vectors a₁ and a₂ are separated by **120°** with magnitude:
$$|\vec{a}_1| = |\vec{a}_2| = a_0 \sqrt{3}$$

where **a₀ = 0.142 nm** is the carbon–carbon bond length.

**Step 2 — Express the basis vectors in Cartesian form.**
$$\vec{a}_1 = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}a, \frac{1}{2}a\right), \quad \vec{a}_2 = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}a, -\frac{1}{2}a\right)$$

where $a = a_0\sqrt{3}$ is the lattice constant of graphene (a ≈ 0.246 nm).

**Step 3 — Form the chiral vector.**
$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the magnitude.**
$$|\vec{C}_h|^2 = (n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2) \cdot (n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2)$$
$$= n^2|\vec{a}_1|^2 + 2nm(\vec{a}_1 \cdot \vec{a}_2) + m^2|\vec{a}_2|^2$$

Since $\vec{a}_1 \cdot \vec{a}_2 = a^2 \cos(120°) = -\frac{a^2}{2}$ and $|\vec{a}_1|^2 = |\vec{a}_2|^2 = a^2$:

$$|\vec{C}_h|^2 = n^2 a^2 + 2nm\left(-\frac{a^2}{2}\right) + m^2 a^2 = a^2(n^2 + nm + m^2)$$

$$\therefore |\vec{C}_h| = a\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2} = a_0\sqrt{3}\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert magnitude to diameter.**
The chiral vector wraps exactly once around the circumference of the tube, so:
$$d_t = \frac{|\vec{C}_h|}{\pi} = \frac{a_0\sqrt{3(n^2 + nm + m^2)}}{\pi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final boxed formula:**
> $$\boxed{d_t = \frac{a_0 \sqrt{3(n^2 + nm + m^2)}}{\pi}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Diameter of a (10, 10) Armchair SWCNT

**Given:** a₀ = 0.142 nm, n = 10, m = 10.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the diameter formula.**
$$d_t = \frac{0.142 \cdot \sqrt{3(10^2 + 10\cdot10 + 10^2)}}{\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the term inside the square root.**
$$n^2 + nm + m^2 = 100 + 100 + 100 = 300$$
$$3 \times 300 = 900$$
$$\sqrt{900} = 30$$

**Step 3 — Plug in numerical values.**
$$d_t = \frac{0.142 \times 30}{\pi} = \frac{4.26}{3.1416}$$

$$d_t \approx 1.356 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A (10, 10) armchair SWCNT has a diameter of **≈ 1.36 nm**, which is a typical experimentally observed value.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Diameter of a (15, 0) Zigzag SWCNT

**Given:** a₀ = 0.142 nm, n = 15, m = 0.

**Step 1 — Compute the term inside the square root.**
$$n^2 + nm + m^2 = 225 + 0 + 0 = 225$$
$$3 \times 225 = 675$$
$$\sqrt{675} \approx 25.98$$

**Step 2 — Calculate diameter.**
$$d_t = \frac{0.142 \times 25.98}{\pi} = \frac{3.689}{3.1416}$$

$$d_t \approx 1.174 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A (15, 0) zigzag tube has a diameter of **≈ 1.17 nm**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Number of Walls in a MWCNT

**Given:** Outer diameter D_out = 25 nm, inner diameter D_in = 3 nm.

**Step 1 — Apply the wall-count formula.**
$$N = 1 + \frac{D_{out} - D_{in}}{2 \cdot d_{interlayer}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**
$$N = 1 + \frac{25 - 3}{2 \times 0.34} = 1 + \frac{22}{0.68}$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**
$$N = 1 + 32.35 = 33.35$$

**Step 4 — Round to nearest integer.**
$$N \approx 33 \text{ walls}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's tip:** Always round **N** to the nearest integer; partial walls are physically meaningless.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Synthesis Methods

| Method | Principle | Typical T (°C) | SWCNT / MWCNT? | Yield | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc Discharge** | Electric arc between graphite electrodes in inert gas | 3000 – 4000 | Both (MWCNT dominant) | 30 – 90% | Short tubes, requires purification |
| **Laser Ablation** | Laser vaporizes a carbon target | 1000 – 1200 | **SWCNT** (high quality) | ~70% | Expensive, low throughput |
| **CVD** | Hydrocarbon decomposition on Fe/Co/Ni catalyst | 600 – 900 | Both | 20 – 100% | Defects from catalyst residues |
| **PECVD** | Plasma-assisted CVD (lower T) | 400 – 700 | Vertically aligned MWCNT | High | Equipment cost |

---

### 3.6 Detailed Mechanism: Arc Discharge Method (KTU Board Favorite)

**Step 1 — Setup:** Two graphite rods (anode + cathode) are placed ~1–3 mm apart in a chamber filled with inert gas (He or Ar) at low pressure (~ 500 Torr).

**Step 2 — Arc generation:** A DC current (~50–100 A) at ~20–30 V is applied, creating an arc that vaporizes the anode.

**Step 3 — Carbon vapor:** Anode carbon sublimates into a plasma of C⁺, C₂, and other carbon species (~ 3000–4000 °C).

**Step 4 — Condensation:** Carbon species migrate to the cooler cathode and condense, forming a **carbonaceous deposit** containing MWCNTs (or SWCNTs if a metal catalyst like Fe/Ni is added to the anode).

**Step 5 — Collection:** The deposit is collected and purified to isolate CNTs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "A.L.C.":** **A**rc, **L**aser, **C**VD — the three principal methods, in decreasing order of synthesis temperature.

---

### 3.7 Detailed Mechanism: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

**Step 1 — Catalyst preparation:** A thin film or patterned array of transition metal nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Ni; typical size 1–10 nm) is deposited on a substrate (Si, Al₂O₃).

**Step 2 — Substrate heating:** The substrate is heated to 600–900 °C in a furnace under H₂/Ar flow to reduce surface oxides and pre-treat the catalyst.

**Step 3 — Hydrocarbon introduction:** A carbon source gas (C₂H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, or even CO) is introduced.

**Step 4 — Decomposition & diffusion:** The hydrocarbon decomposes on the catalyst surface; carbon atoms dissolve into the metal nanoparticle.

**Step 5 — Precipitation & growth:** When carbon supersaturation occurs, carbon precipitates out, nucleating a CNT that grows **lift-off mode** (catalyst at tip) or **base-growth mode** (catalyst at base).

**Step 6 — Termination:** Growth stops when the catalyst is poisoned (e.g., by amorphous carbon overcoat) or the gas flow is switched off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** CVD is the **only industrially scalable** method for CNT production because it allows controlled growth on patterned substrates — essential for nanoelectronics fabrication.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a Single-Walled Carbon Nanotube (SWCNT). With the help of a diagram, distinguish between armchair, zigzag, and chiral nanotubes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A SWCNT is a single cylindrical layer of graphene (a hexagonal sp²-bonded carbon sheet) rolled into a seamless tube of diameter 0.4–3 nm. The three types arise from the rolling direction characterized by chiral indices (n, m):
- **Armchair (n, n):** chiral angle θ = 30°; always metallic.
- **Zigzag (n, 0):** chiral angle θ = 0°; metallic if n is a multiple of 3, otherwise semiconducting.
- **Chiral (n, m) with n ≠ m ≠ 0:** 0° < θ < 30°; twisted helical structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark distribution:** Definition 1 mark, types listing 1 mark, distinguishing property 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Part A]**
*List the three principal methods of synthesis of carbon nanotubes. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of the CVD method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three principal synthesis methods are:
1. Arc Discharge
2. Laser Ablation
3. Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

**Advantage of CVD:** Industrially scalable, allows growth on patterned substrates at relatively low temperatures (600–900 °C), enabling wafer-scale CNT device fabrication.

**Disadvantage of CVD:** Catalyst residues and amorphous carbon impurities require post-synthesis purification, and tubes typically have more defects than those produced by laser ablation.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU End Semester Exam Pattern, Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the diameter of a single-walled carbon nanotube in terms of the chiral indices (n, m) and the C–C bond length. Hence calculate the diameter of a (12, 6) chiral SWCNT. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the chiral vector (1 mark):**
The chiral vector in the graphene lattice is:
$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$
where n, m are non-negative integers.

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude (2 marks):**
$$|\vec{C}_h| = a_0\sqrt{3}\,\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}$$
(derivation shown in Section 3.1, expanding the dot product with $\vec{a}_1 \cdot \vec{a}_2 = -a^2/2$).

**Step 3 — Equate circumference to diameter (1 mark):**
The chiral vector wraps around the circumference:
$$d_t = \frac{|\vec{C}_h|}{\pi} = \frac{a_0\sqrt{3(n^2+nm+m^2)}}{\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution for (12, 6) (3 marks):**
Given a₀ = 0.142 nm, n = 12, m = 6:
$$n^2 + nm + m^2 = 144 + 72 + 36 = 252$$
$$3 \times 252 = 756$$
$$\sqrt{756} \approx 27.495$$
$$d_t = \frac{0.142 \times 27.495}{\pi} = \frac{3.904}{3.1416} \approx 1.243 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $d_t \approx$ **1.24 nm** for a (12, 6) SWCNT.

---

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **arc discharge method** of CNT synthesis. Discuss its merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The arc discharge method uses a high-current DC arc (50–100 A, 20–30 V) struck between two graphite electrodes in a low-pressure inert gas (He or Ar, ~ 500 Torr). The high temperature (~3000–4000 °C) vaporizes the anode, and carbon species deposit onto the cathode as a carbonaceous soot containing CNTs.

**Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to the schematic in Section 4.3]

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Two graphite rods (anode + cathode) are placed 1–3 mm apart in an inert atmosphere.
2. A DC arc vaporizes the anode tip.
3. Carbon plasma condenses on the cathode, forming CNTs.
4. SWCNTs are obtained if metal catalysts (Fe, Ni, Co) are mixed into the anode.

**Merits (1 mark):**
- High-quality CNTs with few defects.
- Relatively simple and inexpensive setup.

**Demerits (1 mark):**
- Short, entangled tubes; difficult to control chirality.
- Requires post-synthesis purification to remove catalyst and amorphous carbon.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Alternative, KTU Pattern 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) method** for CNT synthesis in detail. Compare it with the laser ablation method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**CVD Method (4 marks):**
1. **Catalyst preparation:** A thin film of Fe, Co, or Ni nanoparticles (1–10 nm) is deposited on a Si or Al₂O₃ substrate.
2. **Substrate heating:** The substrate is heated to 600–900 °C in H₂/Ar to pre-treat the catalyst.
3. **Hydrocarbon feed:** A carbon source (C₂H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄) is introduced; it decomposes on the catalyst surface.
4. **CNT growth:** Carbon atoms diffuse into the catalyst, reach supersaturation, and precipitate as a CNT, growing either by **tip-growth** or **base-growth** mechanism.
5. **Termination:** Growth stops when the catalyst is deactivated by amorphous carbon overcoating.

**Comparison with Laser Ablation (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CVD | Laser Ablation |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 600–900 °C (low) | 1000–1200 °C (high) |
| Tube quality | Moderate defects | High quality, fewer defects |
| Scalability | Excellent (industrial) | Poor (low throughput) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Catalyst required | Yes (Fe, Co, Ni) | Yes (typically Ni/Co) |
| Chirality control | Difficult | Difficult but better |

---

**(b)** A MWCNT has an outer diameter of 20 nm and an inner diameter of 4 nm. Calculate the **number of concentric walls**. Also compute the chiral angle of a (10, 5) SWCNT. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part 1 — Number of walls (3 marks):**
Given: D_out = 20 nm, D_in = 4 nm, d_interlayer = 0.34 nm.

$$N = 1 + \frac{D_{out} - D_{in}}{2 \times d_{interlayer}} = 1 + \frac{20 - 4}{2 \times 0.34}$$

$$N = 1 + \frac{16}{0.68} = 1 + 23.53 = 24.53$$

$$\therefore N \approx 25 \text{ walls (rounded)}$$

**Part 2 — Chiral angle (4 marks):**
Given: n = 10, m = 5.

$$\cos\theta = \frac{n + m/2}{\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}$$

$$n + m/2 = 10 + 2.5 = 12.5$$
$$n^2 + nm + m^2 = 100 + 50 + 25 = 175$$
$$\sqrt{175} \approx 13.229$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{12.5}{13.229} \approx 0.9449$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.9449) \approx 19.11°$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since 0° < θ < 30°, the (10, 5) tube is a **chiral SWCNT**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use a₀ = 0.142 nm** (some students incorrectly use a = 0.246 nm — that is the *lattice constant*, not the bond length).
> 2. **Rounding too early:** Keep at least 3 decimal places until the final answer.
> 3. **MWCNT wall count:** Some students forget the "+ 1" term and incorrectly report only the number of gaps.
> 4. **Metallicity confusion:** Armchair (n, n) is **always metallic**, but zigzag (n, 0) is metallic **only when n is a multiple of 3**.
> 5. **Diagram labeling:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for a *correctly labeled* diagram — make sure to label the anode, cathode, arc, and inert gas inlet.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **SWCNT:** Single graphene cylinder, d = 0.4–3 nm, defined by chiral vector $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$.
- **MWCNT:** Multiple concentric cylinders, interlayer gap = **0.34 nm**, d = 2–100 nm.
- **C–C bond length a₀ = 0.142 nm**; graphene lattice constant a = 0.246 nm.
- **Diameter formula:** $d_t = \dfrac{a_0 \sqrt{3(n^2 + nm + m^2)}}{\pi}$.
- **Chiral angle:** $\cos\theta = \dfrac{n + m/2}{\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}$; ranges 0° to 30°.
- **Metallic SWCNTs:** Armchair (n, n) always; zigzag (n, 0) only when n mod 3 = 0.
- **Semiconducting SWCNTs:** Have a band gap $E_g \approx \gamma_0 a_0 / d_t$ (inversely proportional to diameter).
- **MWCNT wall count:** $N = 1 + (D_{out} - D_{in})/(2 \times 0.34)$.
- **Arc discharge:** ~3000–4000 °C, produces both SWCNTs (with catalyst) and MWCNTs.
- **Laser ablation:** ~1000–1200 °C, produces high-quality SWCNTs, low throughput.
- **CVD:** ~600–900 °C, scalable, requires Fe/Co/Ni catalyst, tip- or base-growth mode.
- **PECVD:** Plasma-assisted, ~400–700 °C, enables vertical alignment of CNTs.
- **Bottom-up synthesis:** All CNT methods are bottom-up, building from atoms/molecules.
- **First observation of CNTs:** Iijima, S. (1991) — MWCNTs; Iijima & Ichihashi (1993) — SWCNTs.
- **Industrial scalability:** CVD > Laser ablation > Arc discharge.
- **Defect density:** Laser ablation < Arc discharge < CVD (typical trend).
- **Catalyst role:** Transition metals (Fe, Co, Ni) enable hydrocarbon decomposition and CNT nucleation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU Board Quick Revision Hook:** Remember the **"ALC"** mnemonic — **A**rc, **L**aser, **C**VD — for the three main CNT synthesis methods, in order of decreasing temperature (4000 °C → 1200 °C → 900 °C).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Advanced Materials Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Advanced Materials Applications* refer to the strategic engineering deployment of high-performance materials—including **smart materials, nanomaterials, biomaterials, advanced composites, and engineered ceramics**—into functional devices, structures, and systems where conventional materials cannot satisfy the demanding combinations of mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrical, and biological performance criteria.

The term **"application"** in this context moves beyond mere use; it implies a deliberate **functional mapping** between a material's intrinsic property (e.g., piezoelectricity, shape memory, biocompatibility) and an engineering requirement (e.g., actuation, sensing, load-bearing, implantation).

### Key Application Domains in KTU 2024 Syllabus:

| Domain | Material Class | Core Functional Property |
|--------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Aerospace | Composites, Superalloys | High strength-to-weight ratio |
| Biomedical | Biomaterials, Hydrogels | Biocompatibility, Osseointegration |
| Electronics | Semiconductors, Nanosensors | Tunable bandgap, Surface effects |
| Energy | Piezoelectrics, Photovoltaics | Energy conversion efficiency |
| Civil | Geopolymers, UHPC | Durability, Sustainability |
| Defense | Stealth materials, Armor | Radar absorption, Ballistic resistance |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife" Concept:**
> Imagine traditional materials (like wood or basic steel) as a regular kitchen knife—it cuts, but that's all it does. Now imagine an **advanced material** as a **Swiss Army Knife**: the same physical object can sense temperature, convert vibration to electricity, self-repair a scratch, and change shape on command. **Applications** are simply identifying *which tool (function) gets unlocked in which real-world scenario.*

### Simple Intuitive Explanation:

Think of three everyday applications:

1. **Your Smartphone Screen**: Uses **Gorilla Glass (advanced ceramic)** + **OLED organics** + **piezoelectric haptic feedback**—three advanced materials working in milliseconds.
2. **A Modern Jet Engine**: Uses **single-crystal nickel superalloy turbine blades** capable of withstanding **1,600 °C** without melting.
3. **A Knee Implant**: Uses **titanium alloy with hydroxyapatite coating** so that bone literally grows into the implant surface.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Applications:**
> - **Specific Strength** = $\frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ (MPa·cm³/g)
> - **Figure of Merit (Piezoelectric)** = $d_{33} \cdot g_{33}$ (pm·m/N × 10⁻³ V·m/N)
> - **Glass Transition Temperature** $T_g$ (°C or K)
> - **Young's Modulus** $E$ (**GPa**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Application Engineering Workflow

The deployment of an advanced material into an engineering application follows a structured **5-step logic chain**:

### Step 1 — Performance Requirement Identification
- Define the **operating environment**: temperature, stress, chemical exposure, biological contact.
- Quantify **functional KPIs**: e.g., actuation strain $\geq 4\%$, response time $\leq 1$ ms, biocompatibility ISO 10993 compliant.

### Step 2 — Material Property Mapping
- Match the KPI with intrinsic material coefficients:
  - Piezoelectric coefficient $d_{ij}$ (pC/N)
  - Shape Memory Strain $\varepsilon_{SM}$
  - Bandgap $E_g$ (eV)
  - Fracture Toughness $K_{IC}$ (MPa·m^½)

### Step 3 — Compatibility Assessment
- **Thermal expansion matching** $\alpha_1 \approx \alpha_2$ to avoid delamination.
- **Electrochemical compatibility** to prevent galvanic corrosion.
- **Mechanical impedance matching** $Z = \rho c$ (where $c$ is wave speed).

### Step 4 — Geometric & Architectural Design
- Select form factor: bulk, thin film, fiber, foam, lattice, or nanocomposite.
- Determine architecture: 0-D (particles), 1-D (fibers/nanotubes), 2-D (coatings/films), 3-D (bulk/lattice).

### Step 5 — Lifecycle & Sustainability Validation
- Recycling index, embodied energy, end-of-life degradation profile.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** for KTU 2024 examination questions on material applications. Units are explicitly stated to avoid silly valuation mistakes.

| # | Formula / Equation | Application Domain | Units |
|---|------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{uts} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Tensile strength of structural composite | **MPa** |
| 2 | $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ (Voigt, parallel) | Upper bound modulus of composite | **GPa** |
| 3 | $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}$ (Reuss, series) | Lower bound modulus of composite | **GPa⁻¹** |
| 4 | $\lambda_{c} = \lambda_m V_m + \lambda_f V_f$ | Thermal conductivity (Voigt) | **W/m·K** |
| 5 | $\frac{1}{\lambda_c} = \frac{V_m}{\lambda_m} + \frac{V_f}{\lambda_f}$ | Thermal conductivity (Reuss) | **W/m·K** |
| 6 | $\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$ | Density of composite (rule of mixtures) | **g/cm³** |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{SM} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Shape memory strain (SME) | **dimensionless** |
| 8 | $d_{33} = \frac{\text{Charge generated}}{\text{Applied force}}$ | Piezoelectric charge coefficient | **pC/N** |
| 9 | $g_{33} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_{33}^T \varepsilon_0}$ | Piezoelectric voltage coefficient | **mV·m/N** |
| 10 | $k_{31}^2 = \frac{d_{31}^2}{s_{11}^E \varepsilon_{33}^T}$ | Electromechanical coupling factor | **dimensionless** |
| 11 | $\eta_{PV} = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Photovoltaic conversion efficiency | **%** |
| 12 | $E_g (eV) = \frac{1240}{\lambda (nm)}$ | Bandgap-wavelength relation (semiconductor) | **eV** |
| 13 | $\tau_{relax} = \eta \cdot \gamma$ | Viscous flow response (polymer melt) | **Pa·s** |
| 14 | $\sigma_{Hall} = R_H \cdot J_x \cdot B_z$ | Hall effect sensor response | **V** |
| 15 | $\Delta R / R_0 = K \cdot \varepsilon$ | Piezoresistive gauge factor | **dimensionless** |
| 16 | $B_r = \mu_0 (H + M)$ | Magnetic flux density (sensor/actuator) | **Tesla** |
| 17 | $\alpha_{CTE} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0 \Delta T}$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion | **10⁻⁶ /K** |
| 18 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture toughness (Linear Elastic) | **MPa·m^½** |
| 19 | $H_V = 0.1891 \frac{F}{d^2}$ | Vickers hardness | **HV** |
| 20 | $\text{Hall-Petch: } \sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Grain-boundary strengthening | **MPa** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Notation Note:** In the **rule of mixtures** equations above, $V$ denotes **volume fraction** (NOT mass fraction), and $V_m + V_f = 1$. Mixing up volume/mass fractions is the **#1 valuation error** flagged by KTU examiners in composite problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | How the Equations Are Used |
|------------------|----------------------------|
| Aircraft wing skin | Engineers use Voigt (Eq. 2) to predict the **upper-bound stiffness** of carbon-fiber/epoxy laminates and prevent over-design. |
| Turbine blade cooling | Use Reuss model (Eq. 5) to estimate the **minimum thermal conductivity** of thermal barrier coatings (TBCs). |
| Piezoelectric fuel injector | Designers use Eq. 10 ($k_{31}^2$) to maximize **electromechanical energy conversion** for diesel injection. |
| Solar cell grading | Eq. 11 ($\eta_{PV}$) directly determines **cell efficiency class** and market price. |
| MEMS accelerometer | Combines Eq. 15 (piezoresistive) and Eq. 16 (magnetic) for **multi-modal sensing**. |
| Knee implant fatigue | Uses Eq. 18 ($K_{IC}$) to ensure crack growth rate stays below 10⁻⁸ m/cycle. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Stiffness (Voigt Model)

### Problem Context
A unidirectional glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite is to be used in a **helicopter rotor blade skin**. The volume fraction of fiber $V_f = 0.45$, fiber modulus $E_f = 72$ GPa, matrix modulus $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Find the **longitudinal Young's modulus** $E_c$ using the Voigt (iso-strain) model.

### Mathematical Derivation — Step by Step

> **Assumption:** Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$ (iso-strain condition, valid when load is applied parallel to fibers).

**Step 1 — Define the load equilibrium condition.**

The total load $P_c$ on the composite is shared between fiber and matrix:

$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

**Step 2 — Convert load to stress using cross-sectional areas.**

$$P = \sigma \cdot A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 3 — Express areas as volume fractions (for unit length).**

Since $V_f = A_f / A_c$ and $V_m = A_m / A_c$, with $V_f + V_m = 1$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law $\sigma = E \varepsilon$.**

Since $\varepsilon$ is identical (iso-strain), factor it out:

$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\varepsilon$ on both sides.**

$$\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical substitution.**

$$E_c = (72)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)$$

$$E_c = 32.40 + 1.925$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 34.325 \ \text{GPa}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the **upper bound** modulus. Actual measured value will be slightly lower due to fiber-matrix interfacial defects, but 34.325 GPa is more than **9× stiffer than pure epoxy** (3.5 GPa) — justifying the use of composites in aerospace.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting Output

### Problem Context
A piezoelectric **PZT-5H** patch is bonded to a vibrating cantilever beam in a wireless sensor node. The applied dynamic stress $\sigma = 2.5$ MPa (RMS), and the relevant material coefficients are: $d_{33} = 593$ pC/N, $g_{33} = 19 \times 10^{-3}$ V·m/N, and the patch area $A = 10$ mm × 10 mm. Determine the open-circuit output voltage and the power density available.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the piezoelectric voltage equation.**

The voltage generated is related to stress by the voltage coefficient:

$$V = g_{33} \cdot \sigma \cdot t$$

where $t$ is the patch thickness. Assume $t = 1$ mm = $1 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

**Step 2 — Convert stress to consistent units.**

$$\sigma = 2.5 \ \text{MPa} = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \ \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$V = (19 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$V = 19 \times 2.5 \times 10^{0}$$

$$\boxed{V = 47.5 \ \text{V (open-circuit, RMS)}}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the power density using $d_{33}$.**

The power figure of merit is:

$$\text{FoM} = d_{33} \cdot g_{33}$$

$$\text{FoM} = 593 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 19 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FoM} = 11.27 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{C·V/N}^2 = 11.27 \ \text{pC·V/N}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the power per unit volume.**

$$P/V = \text{FoM} \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot f$$

where $f$ = vibration frequency ≈ 50 Hz (typical machinery resonance).

$$P/V = 11.27 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{6})^2 \cdot 50$$

$$P/V = 11.27 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{12} \cdot 50$$

$$P/V = 11.27 \times 6.25 \times 50 = 3,522 \ \text{W/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{P/V \approx 3.52 \ \text{mW/cm}^3}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This 3.5 mW/cm³ is enough to **power a wireless sensor node** transmitting 1 packet/second — eliminating batteries in IoT bridges, aircraft skin sensors, and tire-pressure monitors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuation Force

### Problem Context
A **Nitinol (Ni-Ti)** SMA wire of diameter $d = 1$ mm is used as an actuator in a morphing wing. Upon heating above $A_f$ (austenite finish) temperature, it recovers a strain of $\varepsilon_{SM} = 6\%$. If the recovery stress $\sigma_{rec} = 200$ MPa, determine the **recovery force** and the **work output per unit length**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the wire.**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^{-6} = 0.7854 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$A = 0.7854 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Recovery force.**

$$F_{rec} = \sigma_{rec} \cdot A = (200 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (0.7854 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$F_{rec} = 200 \times 0.7854 = 157.08 \ \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{rec} \approx 157 \ \text{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Displacement recovered (per unit length).**

For a wire of length $L = 100$ mm (assumed):

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_{SM} \cdot L = 0.06 \times 100 = 6 \ \text{mm} = 0.006 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Work output per cycle.**

$$W = \frac{1}{2} F_{rec} \cdot \Delta L = \frac{1}{2} (157.08)(0.006)$$

$$\boxed{W = 0.471 \ \text{J per 100 mm wire}}$$

> **Real-World Mapping:** Boeing's **morphing aircraft wing** demonstrator uses bundles of 100 such wires, yielding $W \approx 47$ J per cycle — sufficient to deflect a 2-meter trailing edge by 10°.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Material for an Application

| Engineering Demand | Recommended Material Class | Critical Property Equation | Justification |
|--------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Aircraft fuselage | CFRP (Carbon-Fiber Reinforced Polymer) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | High specific stiffness |
| Bone implant | Ti-6Al-4V + HA coating | $E \approx 110$ GPa (matches cortical bone $\approx 15-30$ GPa gradient) | Stress shielding reduction |
| Touchscreen digitizer | ITO (Indium Tin Oxide) on PET | $\sigma_{opt} \geq 80\%$ at 550 nm | Transparent conductor |
| Vibration damper in skyscraper | MR (Magnetorheological) fluid | $\tau_{yield} = f(B)$ | Real-time tunable damping |
| High-temperature turbine blade | Single-crystal Ni-superalloy | $T_{melt} \approx 1320$ °C, $\sigma_y$ at 1000 °C ≥ 800 MPa | Creep resistance |
| Flexible display | Polyimide + OTFT | $T_g > 350$ °C | Thermal-mechanical stability |
| Solar cell | Perovskite (CH₃NH₃PbI₃) | $\eta_{PV} > 25\%$ in lab | High absorption coefficient |
| Gas sensor | ZnO nanowire | $S = \frac{R_a}{R_g}$ response ratio | High surface-to-volume ratio |
| Self-healing coating | Microcapsule-based polymer | $\eta_{heal} = \frac{K_{healed}}{K_{original}} \geq 75\%$ | Autonomous crack repair |
| Stealth aircraft skin | Radar-absorbing RAM (ferrite + carbon) | $\tan \delta = \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | EM wave dissipation |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Rule of Mixtures" as applied to fiber-reinforced composites. State the equations for longitudinal modulus and density.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** is an analytical model that estimates the effective properties of a composite as a weighted sum of the constituent properties, weighted by their **volume fractions**. It assumes perfect fiber-matrix bonding.

For longitudinal (iso-strain, Voigt) Young's modulus:

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

For density:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

where $V_f + V_m = 1$. The RoM provides the **upper bound** for stiffness and is exact when fibers are continuous, aligned, and parallel to the load direction. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for modulus equation, 1 for density equation + assumption)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three biomedical applications of advanced materials and specify the material used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Application | Material Used | Key Property |
|---|------------|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | Artificial hip joint | Ti-6Al-4V alloy + HA coating | Biocompatibility, high fatigue life |
| 2 | Vascular stent | Shape-memory Nitinol (Ni-Ti) | Superelasticity (8% recoverable strain) |
| 3 | Contact lens / drug delivery | Hydrogel (PHEMA, PEG) | Tunable water content, permeability |

**(1 mark per correct pair of application + material + property)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Material Application

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Module 3]**

**(a)** A unidirectional glass-fiber reinforced polymer (GFRP) composite is proposed for a **wind turbine blade spar**. The volume fraction of fiber is $V_f = 0.55$. Given: $E_f = 76$ GPa, $E_m = 3.2$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.15$ g/cm³, and the **rule of mixtures** is valid.
   (i) Calculate the longitudinal Young's modulus and density of the composite. (5 marks)
   (ii) Calculate the **specific modulus** $E_c / \rho_c$ of the composite and compare it with that of **mild steel** ($E = 200$ GPa, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³). (3 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **life cycle of an advanced composite** from synthesis to end-of-life recycling. (6 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### (a)(i) Longitudinal Modulus and Density (5 marks)

**Using the Voigt (iso-strain) rule of mixtures:**

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

$$E_c = (76)(0.55) + (3.2)(0.45)$$

$$E_c = 41.80 + 1.44$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 43.24 \ \text{GPa}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Density calculation:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.55) + (1.15)(0.45)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.4025 + 0.5175$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 1.92 \ \text{g/cm}^3} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Volume conservation check:** $V_f + V_m = 0.55 + 0.45 = 1$ ✓ **(1 mark)**

---

##### (a)(ii) Specific Modulus Comparison (3 marks)

**Specific modulus of GFRP composite:**

$$\left(\frac{E_c}{\rho_c}\right)_{GFRP} = \frac{43.24}{1.92} = 22.52 \ \text{GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Specific modulus of mild steel:**

$$\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_{steel} = \frac{200}{7.85} = 25.48 \ \text{GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\text{GFRP}}{Steel} = \frac{22.52}{25.48} \approx 0.884 \ \text{(88.4\% of steel)}}$$

**Engineering Insight (1 mark):** Although GFRP has lower specific modulus than steel, **CFRP ($E_f \approx 380$ GPa, $\rho_f \approx 1.8$ g/cm³)** achieves $E/\rho \approx 140$ GPa·cm³/g — **5.5× stiffer per unit weight** than steel, making it the preferred choice for **longer (50 m+) wind turbine blades**.

---

##### (b) Life Cycle of Advanced Composite — Block Diagram (6 marks)

**Block Diagram (text-based rendering, draw a clean version on exam sheet):**

```
SYNTHESIS (Resin + Fiber) → FIBER TREATMENT (Sizing) → LAYUP
      ↓
PREPREG FABRICATION → AUTOCLAVE CURE (6 bar, 180°C) → NDT (Ultrasonic C-scan)
      ↓
MACHINING & ASSEMBLY → IN-SERVICE MONITORING (SHM sensors) → END OF LIFE
      ↓
CHEMICAL RECYCLING (Solvolysis) → FIBER RECOVERY → RE-USE IN NEW COMPOSITE
```

**Detailed Step Explanation:**

1. **Synthesis (1 mark):** Precursors include petroleum-derived epoxy resin and E-glass or carbon fiber rovings.
2. **Fiber Treatment (0.5 mark):** Sizing agents (silane coupling) applied to improve fiber-matrix interfacial adhesion.
3. **Layup & Cure (1 mark):** Hand layup or automated fiber placement followed by autoclave cure at 6 bar and 180°C.
4. **NDT (0.5 mark):** Ultrasonic C-scan detects delamination, voids, or porosity.
5. **In-Service Monitoring (1 mark):** Embedded piezoelectric or fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensors detect damage.
6. **End-of-Life Recycling (1.5 marks):** Solvolysis uses solvents (e.g., supercritical water) to break down the epoxy matrix, recovering **>90% of carbon fibers** for reuse — supporting the circular economy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Smart Material Application

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Module 3, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a piezoelectric vibration energy harvester** used in self-powered wireless sensor nodes. Derive an expression for the **open-circuit voltage** generated. (7 marks)

**(b)** Three engineering applications are proposed:
   (i) **Surgical stent** in a blocked artery
   (ii) **Aircraft wing skin** that adjusts camber
   (iii) **Anti-lock braking system (ABS) sensor** in a car

For each, identify the most suitable **smart material** and justify the choice in terms of its **primary functional property**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### (a) Piezoelectric Energy Harvester — Working Principle + Derivation (7 marks)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

A piezoelectric energy harvester converts ambient mechanical vibration into electrical energy using the **direct piezoelectric effect**, described by:

$$D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^\sigma \cdot E$$

where $D$ = electric displacement, $d$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient, $\sigma$ = applied stress, $\varepsilon^\sigma$ = permittivity at constant stress, $E$ = applied electric field.

**Working Steps:**
1. A vibrating host structure (beam, bridge, engine casing) imposes cyclic stress $\sigma(t)$ on the bonded piezoelectric patch.
2. The **asymmetric crystal lattice** (e.g., perovskite PZT) generates an oscillating charge $Q(t) = d_{33} \cdot F(t)$ on the electrodes.
3. A **charge amplifier** conditions the signal, an **ADC** digitizes it, and a **microcontroller** transmits wirelessly.
4. The harvester powers itself from ambient vibration — no battery replacement required.

**Derivation of Open-Circuit Voltage (4 marks):**

The constitutive piezoelectric equation in the **33-direction** (poling axis) is:

$$E_3 = -g_{33} \cdot \sigma_3 + \beta^\sigma_{33} \cdot D_3$$

For open-circuit condition, $D_3 = 0$ (no free charge movement):

$$E_3 = -g_{33} \cdot \sigma_3$$

The voltage across the patch of thickness $t$ is:

$$V = E_3 \cdot t = -g_{33} \cdot \sigma_3 \cdot t$$

Substituting the magnitudes:

$$\boxed{\vert V \vert = g_{33} \cdot \sigma \cdot t}$$

**Numerical example** (for clarity, not required for marks): With $g_{33} = 19 \times 10^{-3}$ V·m/N, $\sigma = 2.5$ MPa, $t = 1$ mm:

$$V = (19 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^6) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3}) = 47.5 \ \text{V (RMS)}$$

---

##### (b) Material Selection for Three Applications (7 marks)

| # | Application | Smart Material | Primary Functional Property | Justification |
|---|------------|----------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| (i) | **Surgical stent** | **Nitinol (Ni-Ti SMA)** | **Superelasticity** — recovers up to 8% strain | (2 marks) Stent is crimped to small diameter, inserted into artery, then self-expands to original "remembered" shape at body temperature. Superelastic behavior at $T > A_f$ provides **constant recovery force** without permanent deformation. |
| (ii) | **Aircraft wing skin — camber adjustment** | **Piezoelectric stack actuator (PZT)** | **Inverse piezoelectric effect** — $d_{33}$ strain | (2.5 marks) Applying a voltage to the PZT stack produces a small (0.1–0.2%) but high-force strain that flexes the wing skin, allowing real-time aerodynamic optimization (drag reduction by 5–8%). |
| (iii) | **ABS wheel-speed sensor** | **Magnetostrictive / Hall-effect material (InSb, GaAs)** | **High electron mobility → strong Hall voltage** | (2.5 marks) A magnetic encoder on the wheel generates a varying $B$ field; the Hall sensor produces $V_H \propto B$. The **fast response** ($\mu$s) and **temperature stability** of InSb make it ideal for ABS safety systems. |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 3 "Applications" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_f$ with weight fraction $W_f$ (–2 marks):** KTU examiners strictly check the volume fraction formula. Convert mass fractions to volume fractions using $\rho$ before applying RoM.
> 2. **Forgetting $V_f + V_m = 1$ constraint (–1 mark):** Always state this conservation equation explicitly.
> 3. **Using $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ for transverse loading (–3 marks):** This equation is valid ONLY for **longitudinal (parallel) loading**. For transverse, use the **inverse RoM**: $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 14-mark questions (–2 to –3 marks):** KTU valuation key reserves dedicated marks for "neat sketch with proper labelling."
> 5. **Writing $d_{33}$ in SI units directly without converting to pC/N (–1 mark):** Always convert and state the unit clearly.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions in derivations (–1 mark):** Iso-strain, perfect bonding, continuous fibers — these **assumptions are mandatory** for full marks.
> 7. **Mixing up shape memory effect (SME) with superelasticity:** SME is **temperature-driven** (heating recovers shape); superelasticity is **mechanical-stress-driven** (loading-unloading at constant $T$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Applications**

### 1. Core Concepts to Memorize
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures** — equations for $E_c$, $\rho_c$, $\lambda_c$ (Voigt and Reuss versions).
- ✅ **Piezoelectric coefficients** $d_{33}$, $g_{33}$, $k_{31}$ and their physical meaning.
- ✅ **SMA** — Austenite ↔ Martensite transformation, SME vs. Superelasticity.
- ✅ **Magnetostrictive / MR fluid / Hall effect** — actuation and sensing.
- ✅ **Biomaterial biocompatibility** — ISO 10993, osseointegration, stress shielding.

### 2. Application-to-Material Mapping (MUST KNOW)
- ✅ Aircraft → CFRP, Ni-superalloys
- ✅ Stent → Nitinol
- ✅ Hip implant → Ti-6Al-4V + HA
- ✅ Touch screen → ITO on PET
- ✅ Solar cell → Perovskite, Si
- ✅ Touch sensor / fuel injector → PZT piezoelectric
- ✅ Stealth aircraft → RAM (ferrite + carbon)
- ✅ Self-healing coating → Microcapsule polymer

### 3. Formula List to Revise the Night Before Exam
- ✅ $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$
- ✅ $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$
- ✅ $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$
- ✅ $V = g_{33} \cdot \sigma \cdot t$
- ✅ $\eta_{PV} = \frac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{P_{in}}$
- ✅ $E_g (eV) = \frac{1240}{\lambda (nm)}$
- ✅ $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ (Hall-Petch)
- ✅ $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$

### 4. Common Units to Watch
- ✅ $E$ → **GPa**
- ✅ $\sigma$ → **MPa**
- ✅ $d_{33}$ → **pC/N**
- ✅ $g_{33}$ → **mV·m/N** or **10⁻³ V·m/N**
- ✅ $\rho$ → **g/cm³** (NOT kg/m³, unless stated)
- ✅ $T$ → **°C or K** (be consistent within one problem)

### 5. Presentation Tips for KTU Exams
- ✅ Always draw a **labelled block diagram** for 7+ mark questions.
- ✅ Use **bullet points** for applications lists.
- ✅ **State assumptions** before any derivation.
- ✅ Show **all substitution steps** in numerical problems.
- ✅ End with a **justification sentence** ("Hence, CFRP is preferred for aircraft wing skin due to its specific modulus being 5× higher than steel").

> **Good luck with your KTU 2024 PEPET751 examination!**

---
